alignments.preprint_claim_text: The explainable transformer-based deep-learning framework yields predictions of survival outcomes that surpass the performance of state-of-the-art methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Clinical Transformer framework yields survival predictions that surpass the performance of state-of-the-art methods across diverse, independent datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the superior predictive performance but specifies that this holds across diverse, independent datasets, adding contextual detail to the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The clinical transformer framework enhances survival predictions and generates actionable biological insights by utilizing training strategies like gradual and transfer learning. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework's generative capability enables in silico perturbation experiments to test counterfactual hypotheses. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both claims attribute a capability to derive biological insights, but the mechanism shifts from training strategies (preprint) to a generative/in silico-perturbation capability (publication), so the claim is revised and more focused on perturbation experiments.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: By perturbing molecular features associated with immune checkpoint inhibition treatment, a subset of patients who may benefit from immunotherapy was identified and validated across three independent cohorts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: By perturbing immune-associated features in immunotherapy-naive patients, a patient subset that may benefit from immunotherapy was identified and validated across three independent immunotherapy-treated cohorts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core result (identifying a potentially benefiting patient subset validated across three cohorts) but adds/changes details about the perturbed features and patient treatment status, so the claim is revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Improving treatment prediction requires accommodating small data sets, handling sparse features, integrating diverse clinical data, and ensuring interpretability. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Clinical Transformer addresses challenges in identifying determinants of treatment response by accommodating smaller datasets and multimodal clinicogenomic data. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication echoes aspects of the preprint's recommendations (accommodating small datasets and multimodal data) but omits some items (sparse features, interpretability) and reframes them as addressed by the method, thus clarifying and narrowing the scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning methods can reduce the human workload in updating systematic reviews. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning methods can reduce human workload in updating systematic reviews. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same conclusion but expresses lower certainty, indicating a tempered confidence relative to the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best performance in identifying relevant publications was achieved with an ensemble method for two reviews and a deep learning approach for one review. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best performance in classification was obtained with an ensemble method for two reviews and a deep learning approach for one review. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same performance result with equivalent specificity and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The final performance of machine learning approaches is strongly dependent on data preparation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The final performance of machine learning approaches strongly depends on data preparation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is carried over verbatim in meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Users should not expect good performance from machine learning methods solely based on their popularity, such as deep learning. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Users should not expect good performance solely from popular machine learning approaches like deep learning without considering data preprocessing. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the cautionary conclusion but clarifies that lack of data preprocessing (rather than popularity alone) explains poor performance, reframing it as a limitation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning-based digital protein microarray platform enables rapid multiplex quantification of cytokines from critically ill COVID-19 patients. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The digital protein microarray platform enables daily cytokine analysis in clinical practice with high sensitivity and rapid operation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the platform capability for rapid multiplex cytokine measurement but frames it for routine daily clinical practice, broadening the stated application and adding operational context.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The platform demonstrated high sensitivity (<0.4pg/mL) and inter-assay repeatability (~10% CV) for daily cytokine analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The digital protein microarray platform enables daily cytokine analysis in clinical practice with high sensitivity and rapid operation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the performance claim (high sensitivity/repeatability) while placing it in the context of clinical daily analysis; numerical specifics from the preprint are summarized as 'high sensitivity' in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cytokine profiling with the platform revealed clear interleukin-6 (IL-6) elevations after tocilizumab treatment. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims do not report the specific result about IL-6 elevations after tocilizumab, so this preprint-specific finding appears omitted from the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is significant variability in cytokine profiles across critically ill COVID-19 patients. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is significant cytokine profile variability across all critically ill COVID-19 patients. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the variability finding with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The data indicate a weak correlation between IL-6 and clinical biomarkers such as Ferritin and CRP. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A weak correlation exists between IL-6 and clinical biomarkers such as ferritin and C-reactive protein (CRP). (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the weak-correlation result directly and with the same emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Area under the curve (AUC) performances varied considerably across the different data splits. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Area under the curve (AUC) performances varied considerably across the different data splits. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same empirical result about AUC variability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Performances for regression models showed a tradeoff where better training led to worse testing and vice versa. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Performances for regression models showed a tradeoff where better training led to worse testing and vice versa. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same observation about the training/testing tradeoff in regression models.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cross-validation over all cases reduced variability but required samples of 500+ cases to yield representative estimates of performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The specific numeric recommendation (500+ cases) and the assertion about cross-validation requirements are not present in the publication claims, indicating removal of this exact conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models built from different training sets may not be representative of the whole dataset. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models built from different training sets may not be representative of the whole dataset. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the conclusion but reports it with slightly lower certainty, so the core claim is unchanged while confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Performance bias could lead to inappropriate conclusions that might influence the clinical significance of the findings. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Performance bias can result from model testing when using limited datasets. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the limitation as a stronger, more general conclusion about performance bias arising from limited datasets, increasing emphasis and certainty relative to the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GWAS of autoencoder-derived imaging parameters identified 118 statistically significant loci. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GWAS of autoencoder-derived imaging parameters identified 118 statistically significant loci. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the GWAS result verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 17 of these associations reached genome-wide significance in a replication analysis that included 10,409 UK Biobank volunteers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: 41 of these associations were also significant in a replication study. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The replication result changes substantially in the publication (17 -> 41 associations), so the claim is revised and the replicated findings are broader in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The generated retinal phenotypes contribute to predictive models for glaucoma and cardiovascular disorders. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The generated retinal phenotypes contribute to predictive models for glaucoma and cardiovascular disorders. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same conclusion regarding the phenotypes' contribution to predictive models.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Self-supervised phenotyping of OCT images enhances the discoverability of genetic factors influencing retinal morphology. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Self-supervised phenotyping of OCT images enhances the discoverability of genetic factors influencing retinal morphology. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This conceptual conclusion is carried forward unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study provides epidemiologically informative biomarkers. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state this claim explicitly as a separate conclusion, suggesting the preprint's general claim about epidemiologically informative biomarkers was dropped or not emphasized.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A smartphone application was developed to automatically extract ECG waveforms from photos taken by clinicians using computer vision and machine learning. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A smartphone application was developed that automatically extracts ECG waveforms from photos and converts them to voltage-time series for analysis. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the development of the app but adds the detail that waveforms are converted to voltage-time series, clarifying the contribution without changing its scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 12,828 of 13,258 (96.8%) scanned and 5,399 of 5,743 (94.0%) photographed waveforms were correctly cropped and labelled. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application successfully extracted waveforms from 40,516 scanned and 444 photographed ECGs, with high accuracy in cropping and labeling. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports substantially different sample counts and summarizes performance as "high accuracy," indicating a revision of the reported results and a shift in dataset composition/scale.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AF diagnostic algorithm achieved 91.3% sensitivity, 94.2% specificity, 95.6% positive predictive value, 88.6% negative predictive value and 93.4% F1 score. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An atrial fibrillation diagnostic algorithm demonstrated 91.3% sensitivity and 94.2% specificity using photos of ECGs as input. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core performance metrics (sensitivity and specificity) match exactly between preprint and publication; the publication omits some ancillary metrics but does not alter the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using computer vision and machine learning techniques to detect cardiac conditions from photos of ECGs taken with smartphones is feasible. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel pipeline allows for automatic extraction of ECG signals from photos, circumventing the need for costly ECG hardware upgrades. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication shifts emphasis from general feasibility to a practical benefit (avoiding hardware upgrades), clarifying and expanding the practical implications of the original feasibility conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The self-supervised contrastive learning model was effective in capturing diagnostic brain MRI features from a limited but well-defined cohort of SLE patients with NP symptoms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The self-supervised contrastive learning model was effective in capturing common brain MRI features from a limited but well-defined cohort of SLE patients with NP symptoms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the effectiveness claim with only minor wording changes, preserving meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Between 79% and 83% of the patients were correctly classified as NPSLE or non-NPSLE in the trials conducted. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Between 79% and 83% of the patients were correctly classified as NPSLE or non-NPSLE in the six trials conducted. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the detail that six trials were conducted, a clarification of the original numeric result without changing its substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The common features found in the NPSLE population indicate a combination of tissue loss, local atrophy, and periventricular white matter lesions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The common features found in the NPSLE population indicate a combination of tissue loss, local atrophy, and periventricular white matter lesions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This conclusion is restated verbatim in the publication with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Thresholded Grad-CAM maps show areas of common features identified for the NPSLE cohort, with no such communality found for the non-NPSLE group. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Thresholded Grad-CAM maps show areas of common features identified for the NPSLE cohort, while no such communality was found for the non-NPSLE group. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this methodological/result claim with equivalent wording and meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This systematic review points out that, to date, few original research studies report AI tools based on machine or deep learning in the field of hospital clinical pharmacy. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The review indicates that few original research studies report AI tools based on machine or deep learning in hospital clinical pharmacy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication carries essentially the same conclusion but with a lower stated certainty/strength (medium vs high), indicating a tempered confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The systematic review confirms the growing interest of AI in the hospital setting. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The original articles highlighted the potential value of integrating AI into clinical hospital pharmacy practice. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the potential value shown in articles rather than explicitly asserting a broader trend of growing interest, representing a revision and somewhat tempered framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI models have a great potential and will help hospital clinical pharmacists in the near future to better manage review of medication orders. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The original articles highlighted the potential value of integrating AI into clinical hospital pharmacy practice. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both statements express the potential value of AI in hospital pharmacy; the publication frames this more cautiously but the substantive conclusion is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Various AI models were used, mainly supervised learning techniques, to analyze orders and detect inappropriate prescriptions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A systematic review identified 13 articles related to AI models for detecting inappropriate hospital medication orders. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a concrete count and focus consistent with the preprint's statement about models used to detect inappropriate orders, clarifying the scope of included studies.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This systematic review contributes to understanding AI models and the techniques behind the tools. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims do not explicitly restate this meta-level contribution about understanding techniques, so this preprint claim is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed SWAT-CNN approach identified the well-known APOE region as the most significant genetic locus for Alzheimer's Disease (AD). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed SWAT-CNN approach identified the well-known APOE region as the most significant genetic locus for Alzheimer's Disease (AD). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the finding identically, preserving meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classification model developed using SWAT-CNN achieved an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.82. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classification model developed using SWAT-CNN achieved an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.82. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The AUC result is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SWAT-CNN may hold promise for a range of biomedical applications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SWAT-CNN may hold promise for a range of biomedical applications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the tentative, generalizable conclusion about future applicability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SWAT-CNN identified phenotype-associated single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) that can be applied to develop accurate disease classification models. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SWAT-CNN identified AD-associated SNPs that can be applied to develop accurate disease classification models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication assert identification of disease-associated SNPs usable for classification models with consistent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Graph-Transformer (GT) based framework, GTP, effectively interprets morphological and spatial information at the WSI-level to predict disease grade. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Graph-Transformer (GT) model, called GTP, effectively predicts disease grade by fusing a graph-based representation of a whole slide image (WSI) with a vision transformer. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies the model architecture (fusion with a vision transformer) while preserving the core claim about effective WSI-level prediction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GTP achieved a mean overall accuracy of 0.975 ± 0.013 for binary classification of tumor versus normal and a mean accuracy of 0.932 ± 0.019 for three-label classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The GTP model achieved a mean accuracy of 91.2 ± 2.5% on three-label classification of normal versus LUAD versus LSCC based on five-fold cross-validation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Performance numbers differ substantially and the publication reports a lower three-label accuracy with specified cross-validation, indicating a revision and a more conservative result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The introduced graph-based saliency mapping technique, GraphCAM, captures regional and contextual information, highlighting WSI regions associated with the class label. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A graph-based saliency mapping technique, called GraphCAM, was introduced to identify regions highly associated with the class label. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same contribution (GraphCAM) with equivalent description of its purpose.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patch-based methods introduce label noise in training by assuming that each patch is independent with the same label as the WSI. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: This specific limitation about patch-based methods is not explicitly stated among the publication's listed claims, so it appears omitted in the published claims list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The top three ranking machine learning models for predicting grade 3 or higher toxicities in gynecological cancer patients were Support Vector Machines, Random Forest, and Logistic Regression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The top three ranking machine learning models for predicting grade 3 or higher toxicities in gynecological cancer patients treated with HDR brachytherapy were Support Vector Machines, Random Forest, and Logistic Regression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same top-three models but adds the specific context (treated with HDR brachytherapy), making the claim more specific (clarified and narrowed) without changing confidence in the result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The F1 testing scores for the top three models were 0.63 for Support Vector Machines, 0.57 for Random Forest, and 0.52 for Logistic Regression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The F1 testing scores for the top three models were 0.63 for Support Vector Machines, 0.57 for Random Forest, and 0.52 for Logistic Regression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical reported performance metrics in preprint and publication, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Significant differences in HR-CTV volume, Charlson Comorbidity Index, Length of Follow-Up, and D2cc - Rectum were found between groups with and without grade 3 or higher toxicities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Traditional statistical analysis revealed significant differences in HR-CTV volume, Charlson Comorbidity Index, Length of Follow-Up, and D2cc - Rectum between groups with and without grade 3 or higher toxicities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same significant differences but adds the analytic approach (traditional statistical analysis), which clarifies the original claim without changing its scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models were developed to predict grade 3 or higher toxicities, achieving satisfactory performance. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models were developed to predict grade 3 or higher toxicities, achieving satisfactory performance. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The core conclusion that ML models were developed and performed satisfactorily is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning presents a novel solution to creating multivariable models for personalized radiation therapy care. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include this broader/interpretive claim about novelty and personalized radiation therapy care, so the claim appears to have been removed in the published version.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The IMPACT framework demonstrates higher predictive accuracy than popular pre-existing mortality risk scores and biological ages. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: IMPACT models achieve higher accuracy than linear models and neural networks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claim superior accuracy, but the publication changes the comparators (linear models and neural networks) versus the broader set referenced in the preprint (pre-existing risk scores and biological ages), so the claim was revised and narrowed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: IMPACT discovers novel risk predictors, such as arm circumference, and interactions between risk predictors, like serum chloride with age and/or gender. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using IMPACT, we identify several overlooked risk factors and interaction effects. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the discovery of overlooked risk factors and interactions, which matches the preprint claim about novel predictors and interactions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: IMPACT may suggest that widely accepted reference intervals for serum albumin, mean cell volume, and platelet count are sub-optimal for health. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not discuss implications for specific clinical reference intervals, so this more speculative preprint conclusion does not appear in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive accuracy of IMPACT combined with its capability of discovering mortality risk predictors demonstrates the value and utility of explainable artificial intelligence in epidemiologic study design. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: IMPACT takes a consequential step towards bringing contemporary developments in XAI to epidemiology. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both statements make the same high-level claim about IMPACT's value for XAI in epidemiology, but the publication frames it more modestly (medium certainty), so the claim is clarified and confidence is tempered.

alignments.pub_claim_text: We develop highly accurate, efficient and interpretable mortality risk scores that can be used by medical professionals and individuals without medical expertise. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes an added claim about creating usable mortality risk scores for professionals and laypeople that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study estimates short-term discharges, identifies long-stay patients, predicts discharge destination, and anticipates flows in and out of intensive care units with accuracy in the 80%+ range. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We estimate short-term discharges, identify long-stay patients, predict discharge destination, and anticipate flows in and out of intensive care units with accuracy in the 80%+ range. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same performance claims (80%+ accuracy) and prediction tasks, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The implementation of the machine learning pipeline into the EHR system supports daily bed placement decisions through prediction-informed dashboards. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published paper does not describe a concrete implemented EHR dashboard supporting daily bed placement; it emphasizes readiness/production but does not assert the specific implementation, so this specific implementation claim appears removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Interpretable machine learning techniques combined with EHR data provide visibility on patient flows. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our study demonstrates that interpretable machine learning techniques combined with EHR data can be used to provide visibility on patient flows. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about interpretable ML plus EHR data providing visibility on patient flows is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach offers an alternative to deep learning techniques that is equally accurate, interpretable, frugal in data and computational power, and production-ready. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our approach provides an alternative to deep learning techniques that is equally accurate, interpretable, frugal in data and computational power, and production ready. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same comparative and production-readiness claims, so this is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AdaptiveNet predicted active disease defined as DAS28-BSR>2.6 at the next visit with an overall accuracy of 75.6%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AdaptiveNet predicted active disease defined as DAS28-BSR >2.6 at the next visit with an overall accuracy of 75.6%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported classification task and accuracy are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Regression allowed forecasting individual DAS28-BSR values with a mean squared error of 0.9, corresponding to a variation between predicted and true values at next visit of 8%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AdaptiveNet showed an increased performance of over 7% in mean squared error compared to linear regression, random forest, and support vector machines. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint reports an absolute MSE and percent variation, while the publication emphasizes relative improvement over other models; the focus shifted from absolute forecasting error to comparative performance (revised and somewhat narrowed).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The most influential characteristics to predict disease activity were joint pain, disease duration, age, and duration of treatment. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the same list of top predictive characteristics; instead it highlights different subgroup effects and mentions medication's minor role, so this specific variable-ranking claim is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep neural networks have the capacity to predict individual numeric disease activity in RA. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AdaptiveNet showed an increased performance of over 7% in mean squared error compared to linear regression, random forest, and support vector machines. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides comparative evidence of improved numeric-prediction performance, which clarifies and supports the preprint's general claim about DNN capacity to predict numeric disease activity.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Low specificity remains challenging and might benefit from alternative input data or outcome targets. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: All investigated models had limitations in low specificity. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: Both versions acknowledge the limitation of low specificity for the models; the publication restates this limitation directly.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Prediction performance was significantly higher in patients with a disease duration >3 years and positive rheumatoid factor. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes an added subgroup-finding about higher performance in longer-duration and RF-positive patients that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLMs such as RoBERTa and BERT demonstrated high effectiveness in early detection and symptom classification of depression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs such as RoBERTa and BERT demonstrated high effectiveness in early detection and symptom classification of depression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result about LLM effectiveness in detection/classification of depression is stated identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The integration of LLMs into clinical practice is in its nascent stage, with ongoing concerns about data privacy and ethical implications. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The integration of LLMs into clinical practice is in its nascent stage, with ongoing concerns about data privacy and ethical implications. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation about nascent integration and privacy/ethical concerns appears unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLMs exhibit significant potential for transforming strategies for diagnosing and treating depression. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs exhibit significant potential for transforming strategies for diagnosing and treating depression. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about LLM potential for transformation is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Full integration of LLMs into clinical practice requires rigorous testing, ethical considerations, and enhanced privacy measures. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Full integration of LLMs into clinical practice requires rigorous testing, ethical considerations, and enhanced privacy measures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The textual recommendation is the same, but the publication frames it with somewhat lower certainty (medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The COVID-19 pandemic is a propagated source epidemic, therefore repeated peaks on the epidemic curve are to be anticipated. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The COVID-19 pandemic is a propagated source epidemic, therefore repeated peaks on the epidemic curve are to be anticipated. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion verbatim and with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The errors between the predicted and validated data and trends seem to be low. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The errors between the predicted and validated data and trends seem to be low. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result about low prediction errors is carried forward unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future peaks of infections are possible even if strict public health measures are executed and sustained. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The COVID-19 pandemic is a propagated source epidemic, therefore repeated peaks on the epidemic curve are to be anticipated. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates anticipated repeated peaks (matching the preprint's conclusion) but omits the explicit conditional phrasing about strict measures, so the point is preserved but slightly rephrased/clarified.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Our proposed model has shown satisfactory accuracy in predicting the new cases of COVID-19 in certain contexts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This explicit, positive statement of model accuracy appears in the publication but was not present as a distinct claim in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: AI-based models are useful tools for forecasting epidemics as these models can be recalculated according to the newly observed data to get a more precise forecasting. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a general conclusion about AI-based forecasting utility not stated as a separate claim in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Simulated addition of the algorithm led to improvements of 64% in slide waiting time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Simulated nonautonomous algorithm integration led to savings of 35.6% of slide waiting time, 18.4% of staff waiting time, and 18.6% of histotechnician waiting time per day. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a substantially smaller (35.6% vs 64%) and more specifically framed result (nonautonomous simulation), so the original numerical claim was revised and tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The integration of AI resulted in a 36% reduction in staff waiting time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Simulated nonautonomous algorithm integration led to savings of 35.6% of slide waiting time, 18.4% of staff waiting time, and 18.6% of histotechnician waiting time per day. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports an 18.4% reduction in staff waiting time rather than the preprint's 36%, indicating a substantive downward revision.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI integration resulted in a 25% decrease in histotechnician waiting time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Simulated nonautonomous algorithm integration led to savings of 35.6% of slide waiting time, 18.4% of staff waiting time, and 18.6% of histotechnician waiting time per day. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported histotechnician waiting time saving decreased from 25% in the preprint to 18.6% in the publication, a clear numerical revision and tempering.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI integration enables increased productivity and a streamlined clinical workflow. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI integration results in reduced staff waiting times, enabling increased productivity and a streamlined clinical workflow. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same conclusion linking AI integration to productivity and streamlined workflow, adding that reduced waiting times enable this.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study addresses the impact of implementation of an AI algorithm into a real-world clinical workflow. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI integration results in reduced staff waiting times, enabling increased productivity and a streamlined clinical workflow. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the study's contribution as a concrete result (reduced waiting times leading to productivity gains) rather than merely stating the study addresses implementation impact.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Schedules containing surgical cases with either increased repair complexity or numerous tumor removal stages stand to benefit most from AI integration. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a new, specific recommendation about which schedules benefit most from AI that does not appear as a distinct claim in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Significant logistical challenges must be addressed before broad adoption of AI integration into clinical practice is realistic. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces limitations and implementation challenges not stated as separate claims in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The COVASIM agent-based model achieved an R2 value exceeding 95%, demonstrating the model’s robust predictive capability. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The COVASIM agent-based model achieved an R² value exceeding 95%, demonstrating the model's robust predictive capability. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same numeric performance claim and conclusion about predictive capability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Optimal intervention strategies identified through spline interpolation revealed the critical roles of community and workplace interventions in minimizing the pandemic’s impact. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Optimal intervention strategies identified through spline interpolation reveal the critical roles of community and workplace interventions in minimizing the pandemic's impact. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same result about optimal interventions and their roles.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The integration of network analytics with deep learning enhances public health decision-making by streamlining epidemic modeling and reducing computational costs. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The integration of network analytics with deep learning enhances public health decision-making and streamlines epidemic modeling. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the enhancement and streamlining claims but omits the explicit statement about reducing computational costs, so the claim is slightly revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This research offers a novel framework for effectively managing infectious disease outbreaks through targeted, data-driven interventions. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This research offers a novel framework for effectively managing infectious disease outbreaks through targeted, data-driven interventions. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same contribution claim as the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of a GNN classifier based on data-driven diagnostic classes outperforms a classifier using clinician diagnosis as outcome. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of a GNN classifier based on data-driven diagnostic classes outperforms a classifier using clinician diagnosis as an outcome. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same performance claim verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Unsupervised clustering identified five clusters that reveal specific feature characteristics related to clinical diagnosis of AD. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Unsupervised clustering identified five clusters that reveal specific feature characteristics related to clinical diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same clustering result, with only expanded disease name terminology.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: One identified cluster comprised mainly diagnosed AD cases, all of which were re-labelled as AD cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: One identified cluster comprised mainly diagnosed Alzheimer's disease cases, all of which were re-labelled as AD cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this specific clustering and relabelling result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The re-labelled AD cases are characterised by high cerebrospinal fluid amyloid beta levels at a younger age. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The re-labelled cases are characterized by high cerebrospinal fluid amyloid beta levels at a younger age, despite Aβ data not being used for clustering. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the clarification that Aβ data were not used for clustering, which refines the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: More objective cluster-based diagnostic labels combined with GNN classification may improve clinical risk stratification and diagnosis of AD. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: More objective cluster-based diagnostic labels combined with GNN classification may improve clinical risk stratification and diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this tentative conclusion about potential clinical utility.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher total physical activity was associated with a lower overall cancer risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individuals in the highest quintile of total physical activity had a 26% lower cancer risk compared to those in the lowest quintile. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication quantifies and specifies the association (26% lower risk in highest vs lowest quintile), clarifying the preprint's broader statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Reallocating one hour/day from sedentary behaviour to moderate-vigorous physical activity was associated with a lower risk of cancer. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Light-intensity physical activity (LIPA) and moderate-vigorous-intensity physical activity (MVPA) were associated with lower cancer risk, while sedentary behaviour (SB) was not. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports that SB was not associated with cancer while LIPA/MVPA were, which changes the preprint's specific reallocation claim and represents a tempered/revised finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Compared to individuals taking 5,000 daily steps, those who took 9,000 steps had an 18% lower risk of physical-activity-related cancer. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Daily step counts were inversely associated with cancer risk, with a dose-response relationship that began to plateau at around 9,000 steps per day. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the step-count finding as a dose-response relationship with a plateau near 9,000 steps rather than quoting the specific 18% figure, clarifying the relationship.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher total daily physical activity and less sedentary time were associated with a lower risk of certain cancers. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Light-intensity physical activity (LIPA) and moderate-vigorous-intensity physical activity (MVPA) were associated with lower cancer risk, while sedentary behaviour (SB) was not. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's finding that SB was not associated with cancer contradicts the preprint's claim that less sedentary time is associated with lower risk, representing a revision and narrowing of the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Increasing step counts by 4,000 daily steps may be a practical public health intervention for lowering the risk of some cancers. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Daily step counts were inversely associated with cancer risk, with a dose-response relationship that began to plateau at around 9,000 steps per day. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication describes a dose-response for step counts (plateauing near 9,000) but does not explicitly endorse the specific 'increase by 4,000 steps' intervention, so the preprint's practical recommendation is tempered/revised.

alignments.pub_claim_text: There was no significant association between stepping intensity and cancer after adjusting for step count. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces a new, specific null finding about stepping intensity that does not appear as an explicit claim in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The observed agreement between the LLM Claude 2 and Cochrane authors for risk of bias judgements ranged from 41% to 71%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The observed agreement between the LLM Claude 2 and Cochrane authors for risk of bias judgements ranged from 41% to 71%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Text is essentially identical between preprint and publication, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cohen’s κ values indicated slight to fair agreement for the risk of bias judgements made by Claude 2 compared to human judgements. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cohen's κ values indicated slight to fair agreement between Claude 2 and human judgements for different domains of risk of bias. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result but adds the clarification that the finding applies across different domains; meaning preserved but clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Currently, Claude’s RoB 2 judgements cannot replace human risk of bias assessment. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Currently, Claude's RoB 2 judgements cannot replace human RoB assessment. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is restated verbatim in the publication with unchanged meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The potential of large language models to support risk of bias assessment should be further explored. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The potential of large language models to support risk of bias assessment should be further explored. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The suggestion to further explore LLM potential is preserved exactly; only the label (contribution vs conclusion) changed, not the substantive claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: APOE genotype was the best predictor of dementia cases and healthy controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: APOE genotype was the best predictor of dementia cases and healthy controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There are limitations when using publicly accessible cohort data that may limit the generalizability and interpretability of predictive models. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There are limitations when using publicly accessible cohort data that may limit the generalizability and interpretability of predictive models. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation is stated unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future research should examine the use of routine APOE genetic testing for dementia diagnostics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Future research should examine the use of routine APOE genetic testing for dementia diagnostics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Recommendation is carried forward without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future research should focus on clearly unifying data across clinical cohorts. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Future research should focus on clearly unifying data across clinical cohorts. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The future-research recommendation remains the same in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of a novel artificial intelligence tool for automated immunohistochemistry requests can significantly reduce workflow inefficiencies in prostate biopsy reporting. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The use of a novel artificial intelligence tool can identify cases in which immunohistochemistry is required and generate an automated request. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The preprint emphasizes reduction of workflow inefficiencies while the publication frames the claim as the tool's capability to identify cases and generate requests; the focus shifts from outcome to capability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI tool achieved 99% accuracy and 0.99 Area Under the Curve (AUC) on the test data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI tool achieved 99% accuracy and 0.99 Area Under the Curve (AUC) on the test data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Numerical performance metrics are reported identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Automated immunohistochemistry requesting could save an average of 11 minutes per case by eliminating duplicated effort. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Automated immunohistochemistry requesting could save an average of 11 minutes per case by removing duplication of effort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The quantitative time-saving claim is preserved but the publication reports it with higher certainty/frames it as a result rather than a medium-certainty conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The average agreement with pathologists during validation was 0.81, with a mean AUC of 0.80. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The average agreement with pathologists during validation was 0.81, with a mean AUC of 0.80. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Validation agreement and AUC values are reported the same in publication and preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning methods using adequately trained deep convolutional neural networks can assess radiographic images for ankle fractures quickly and accurately without human intervention. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep convolutional neural networks (DCNNs) can assess radiographic images for ankle fractures quickly and accurately without human intervention. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is restated directly in the publication with equivalent meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of the DCNNs was better using 3-view images compared to single-view images, as indicated by greater values for accuracy, F-score, and area under the curve. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of DCNNs using 3-view images is superior to that using single-view images, as indicated by higher accuracy, F-score, and area under the curve (AUC). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance comparison is expressed equivalently in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sensitivity and specificity in detecting ankle fractures using 3-view images were 97.5% and 93.9% for Resnet50, and 98.7% and 98.6% for Inception V3, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Inception V3 demonstrated higher sensitivity and specificity in detecting ankle fractures compared to Resnet50. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Resnet50 missed 3 occult fractures while Inception V3 missed only one case. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Inception V3 demonstrated higher sensitivity and specificity in detecting ankle fractures compared to Resnet50. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of the DCNNs shows promising potential for developing image interpretation programs or serving as an assistant to clinicians in detecting ankle fractures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DCNNs showed potential for improving current image interpretation programs or serving as an assistant to clinicians in detecting ankle fractures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning-based tool developed in this study can perform accurate segmentation of lumbar muscles in axial MRI scans automatically. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning-based tool developed in this study can perform accurate segmentation of lumbar muscles in axial MRI scans automatically. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Jaccard index for the individual muscles in the external validation set ranged between 0.862 and 0.935, indicating excellent performance of the network. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The median Jaccard index for the individual muscles in the external validation set ranged between 0.862 and 0.935, indicating excellent performance of the network. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The cross-sectional area and fat fraction of the muscles segmented by the network were in agreement with published data. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific comparison to published data does not appear in the publication text, indicating the claim was removed or de-emphasized.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The externally validated deep neural network is a suitable tool for large-scale studies in spinal disorders and sarcopenia, addressing the limitations of non-automated methods. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model is suitable for large-scale studies in the field of spinal disorders and sarcopenia, addressing the limitations of non-automated methods. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have become increasingly popular for research into brain conditions due to their suitability for image processing. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have become increasingly popular for research into brain conditions due to their suitability for image processing tasks. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A qualitative systematic literature review of 60 studies reveals that modelling practices, transparency, and interpretability vary widely across CNN-based predictive modelling studies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A qualitative systematic literature review of 55 studies reveals that methodologies in CNN-based predictive modelling of brain disorders can vary widely. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The variability in modelling practices, transparency, and interpretability across studies potentially limits the clinical applications of CNNs in brain disorder research. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The variability in methodologies across studies may limit the clinical application of CNN-based predictive models for brain disorders. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The review proposes several recommendations aimed at maximizing the potential for the integration of CNNs into clinical frameworks. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Recommendations are proposed to enhance the potential for the integration of CNNs into clinical care. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PM-CAD system outperformed 6 existing established convolutional neural network models for detection of PM. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports diagnostic metrics but does not state that PM-CAD outperformed six established CNNs, so this specific comparative claim was removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The diagnostic accuracy of the PM-CAD system was comparable to radiologists with over 10 years of professional expertise, achieving 94% versus 95%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The diagnosis performance of the PM-CAD system was comparable to that of radiologists with over 10 years of professional expertise. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PM-CAD system detected the presence of PM that had been previously misdiagnosed by radiologists. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The PM-CAD system achieved a 100% accurate diagnosis for the misdiagnosis cases from radiologists. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This is the first report showing that the PM-CAD system is a viable tool for detecting PM. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The PM-CAD system is a viable tool for detecting pituitary microadenoma and is applicable to radiology departments, particularly in primary health care institutions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results suggest that the PM-CAD system is applicable to radiology departments, especially in primary health care institutions. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The PM-CAD system is a viable tool for detecting pituitary microadenoma and is applicable to radiology departments, particularly in primary health care institutions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study aims to quantify language features of psychiatric and neurocognitive disorders using natural language processing. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study intends to quantify the linguistic features of psychiatric disorders using natural language processing. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study intends to identify linguistic features that differentiate psychiatric disorders from one another and from healthy subjects. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study intends to quantify the linguistic features of psychiatric disorders using natural language processing. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The data sample collected for this study is one of the largest in the field, comprising over 900 datasets from more than 350 participants. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The data sample collected in this study is one of the largest in the field, with over 1000 records from more than 400 participants. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: It is possible to identify MDD patients with high accuracy using machine learning and EEG data. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The SHAP summary plot highlights the importance of C4-F8 connections and reveals dysfunction in certain brain areas and hyperconnectivity in others. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The SHAP plot highlights the importance of C4-F8 connections and reveals dysfunction in certain brain areas and hyperconnectivity in others. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Assortativity was found to be a promising biomarker for the classification of MDD. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Understanding and diagnosing MDD may be aided by the use of machine learning methods and complex networks. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-4 correctly responded to 73 out of 88 questions, achieving an accuracy rate of 83.0%. (certainty=high, type=result)
