alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepACSA provides fast analysis of ultrasound images, with an average duration of 4.0 seconds per image. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the 'fast' analysis claim and quantifies processing time, clarifying speed though not repeating the direct comparability phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The intra-class correlation (ICC) for DeepACSA analysis of the RF muscle was 0.96, indicating a high level of agreement with manual analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepACSA analysis of the rectus femoris muscle resulted in an intraclass correlation (ICC) of 0.989, indicating high agreement with manual analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a different (higher) ICC for the rectus femoris than the preprint, revising the numeric result and strengthening the agreement claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepACSA is the first deep learning-based algorithm to segment panoramic ultrasound images of human lower limb muscles. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not assert the 'first' claim; no direct equivalent is present, so the originality/firstness claim is not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Inaccurate model predictions occurred predominantly on low-quality images, highlighting the importance of high image quality for accurate prediction. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Inaccurate model predictions were predominantly associated with low-quality images, emphasizing the need for high-quality images for accurate segmentation. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this limitation nearly verbatim, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Incorporating deep learning-based algorithms like DeepACSA in clinical practice can reduce analysis effort and operator bias. (certainty=medium, type=implication)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports speed and reliability but does not explicitly claim reduced operator bias or a direct clinical-practice implication, so this implication claim is not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A novel full stack pipeline is introduced for the generation and evaluation of synthetic pathology data powered by a diffusion model. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The manuscript introduces a comprehensive pipeline for generating and evaluating synthetic pathology data using a diffusion model. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution with nearly identical scope and emphasis on a diffusion-based pipeline, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed evaluation pipeline demonstrates encouraging results in assessing the reliability of the generated data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed evaluation pipeline yields promising results when applied to the public GTEx dataset of 650 Whole Slide Images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same positive outcome but specifies the dataset and size (GTEx, 650 WSIs), narrowing the claim to that application context.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The workflow features a new multifaceted evaluation strategy with an embedded explainability procedure that effectively tackles critical aspects of synthetic data use in health-related domains. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not mention an embedded explainability procedure or the broader claim that it tackles critical health-domain aspects, so this specific contribution is not represented.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An ensemble-like strategy is adopted for evaluating the produced data, assessing the similarity of real and synthetic data through established metrics. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The evaluation of the generated data employs an ensemble-like approach that assesses similarity, usability, and histopathological realism. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the ensemble-like evaluation claim but expands the assessed aspects (usability, histopathological realism), thereby clarifying and broadening the evaluation criteria.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed workflow represents a potentially useful tool for the digital pathology community in their transition towards digitalization and data-driven modeling. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The workflow offers a comprehensive solution for generative AI in digital pathology, aiding the community in their transition towards digitalization. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both statements convey the same conclusion about the workflow's utility to the digital pathology community and its role in supporting digitalization.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 40.4% of tweets indicate uncertainty about weather’s impact on SARS-CoV-2 transmission. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: 40.4% of tweets indicate uncertainty about weather's impact on SARS-CoV-2 transmission. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The exact statistic and interpretation are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is no consensus among the public regarding the potential impact of weather on COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is no consensus among the public regarding the potential impact of weather on SARS-CoV-2 transmission. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is the same, but the publication reports a lower certainty level, indicating a tempered confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proportion of tweets claiming some effect of weather increased over time. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report a temporal trend; it instead provides cross-sectional proportions, so the time-increase claim is not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a major gap between scientific evidence and public opinion of weather’s impacts on COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The research approach used is effective in measuring population perceptions and identifying misconceptions related to weather's impact on public health. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's interpretive conclusion about a gap is reframed in the publication as a methodological claim about the study's effectiveness in measuring perceptions and misconceptions, representing a substantive revision in emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The models demonstrated good performance in extracting diagnostic codes from free-text clinical notes, with TF-IDF as the best word embedding model showing AU-ROC ranging from 0.9499 to 0.9915. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models showed good performance, with TF-IDF as the best vectorization model showing the highest AUROC (0.9499-0.9915) and AUPRC (0.2956-0.8072). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same AUROC results and adds AUPRC performance metrics, clarifying and broadening the reported evaluation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The models showed transferability when tested on the MIMIC-III data set, achieving AUROC ranging from 0.7952 to 0.9790. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models demonstrated transferability when tested on MIMIC-III data with AUROC from 0.7952 to 0.9790 and AUPRC from 0.2353 to 0.8084. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the transferability claim and supplements AUROC with AUPRC ranges, clarifying performance across metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study shows the feasibility to accurately extract structured diagnostic codes, impute missing codes, and correct erroneous codes from free-text clinical notes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study shows the feasibility of accurately extracting structured diagnostic codes, imputing missing codes, and correcting erroneous codes from free-text clinical notes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is restated verbatim in the publication, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model interpretability was demonstrated by the important words with clinical meanings matching each disease. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not mention interpretability or the important-word analysis, so this specific result is not represented.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Early discontinuation affects over one-third of patients enrolled in early-phase oncology clinical trials. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Early discontinuation affects more than one third of patients enrolled in early-phase oncology clinical trials. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same prevalence finding with equivalent phrasing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning model achieved an F1 score of 0.80, recall of 0.81, and AUC of 0.88 on the unseen test cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning model achieved an F1 score of 0.80, a recall of 0.81, and an area under the curve of 0.88 on the unseen test cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Metrics and cohort context are identical between preprint and publication, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using the model could have reduced the screen fail rate from 39.8% to 12.8% within the test cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using the machine learning model could have reduced the screen fail rate from 39.8% to 12.8% within the test cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same counterfactual reduction in screen fail rate with identical figures.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The most important semantic clusters for predictions comprised words related to hematological malignancies, anatomo-pathological features, and laboratory and imaging interpretation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The most important semantic clusters for predictions comprised words related to hematologic malignancies, anatomopathologic features, and laboratory and imaging interpretation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same descriptive finding about important semantic clusters, with only minor spelling differences.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning with semantic conservation is a promising tool to assist physicians in selecting patients prone to achieve SSD in early-phase oncology clinical trials. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning with semantic conservation is a promising tool to assist physicians in selecting patients prone to achieve successful screening and dose-limiting toxicity period completion. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the SSD abbreviation into explicit phrasing but preserves the original conclusion and confidence, so it is a clarification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ARDSFlag tool demonstrates an overall accuracy of 89.0% in diagnosing ARDS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ARDSFlag demonstrates an overall accuracy of 89.0% in detecting ARDS cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same overall accuracy and intent (detecting ARDS), so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ARDSFlag shows higher performance in all accuracy measures compared to two other ARDS identification methods in the literature. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ARDSFlag generates a significantly higher performance in all accuracy measures compared to other methods. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the comparative superiority claim but generalizes it to 'other methods' and reports a lower certainty level, broadening scope while tempering confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method includes separate text classifiers that achieve accuracies of 91.9% for bilateral infiltrates and 98.4% for heart failure/fluid overload. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The accuracy of ARDSFlag in identifying bilateral infiltrates in radiology reports is 91.9%±0.5%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the bilateral infiltrates classifier accuracy (with uncertainty) but omits the heart failure/fluid overload figure, narrowing and revising the original dual-classifier claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The tool utilizes machine learning and natural language processing techniques to automate the diagnosis of ARDS. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This is the first study to focus on developing a method to automate the detection of ARDS. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the contribution to emphasize novelty (first study) and elevates certainty; this shifts emphasis from a general ML/NLP contribution to a stronger claim of being the first such effort.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The trained CNN performance on the independent dataset showed an ability to discriminate between COVID, NCOVID, and HPE pathologies with AUCs of 1.0, 0.934, and 1.0, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The trained convolutional neural network (CNN) demonstrated an ability to discriminate between COVID-19, non-COVID acute respiratory distress syndrome, and hydrostatic pulmonary edema pathologies with high accuracy.
alignments.rationale: Both statements report the same result: the trained CNN discriminates those pathologies with high accuracy; wording differs only in specificity of metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CNN diagnostic performance was significantly better than physician ability, with AUCs of 0.697, 0.704, and 0.967 for the COVID, NCOVID, and HPE classes, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CNN's diagnostic performance was significantly better than that of surveyed LUS-competent physicians.
alignments.rationale: Both claim the CNN outperformed physicians; the publication restates the significance without repeating the numeric AUCs.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A deep learning model can distinguish similar appearing LUS pathology, including COVID-19, that cannot be distinguished by humans.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A deep learning model can distinguish similar appearing lung ultrasound pathology that cannot be distinguished by humans.
alignments.rationale: Identical conceptual claim that the model can detect distinctions not apparent to human readers.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance gap between humans and the model suggests that subvisible biomarkers within ultrasound images could exist.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance gap between humans and the model suggests that subvisible biomarkers within ultrasound images could exist.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same interpretive conclusion about possible subvisible biomarkers.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PSMA PET imaging provides valuable information regarding disease stage, response rate, and treatment optimization options.
alignments.rationale: The publication claims do not include this general background/clinical utility statement, so it appears omitted from the summarized publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PSMA Healthy organ segmentation network (PSMA-Hornet) is a fully-automated deep neural net that effectively segments and labels 14 healthy organs in PET/CT images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The PSMA Healthy organ segmentation network (PSMA-Hornet) can automatically obtain high-quality organ segmentations for PSMA image analysis tasks.
alignments.rationale: Both state the network automatically produces high-quality organ segmentations; the publication frames it for PSMA image analysis without restating the '14 organs' detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Self-supervised pretraining of the model improved training convergence, train-to-test generalization, and segmentation quality.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Self-supervised pretraining improved training convergence, train-to-test generalization, and segmentation quality.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same experimental result about benefits of self-supervised pretraining.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed network can be used for high-quality organ segmentations, which is beneficial for personalized radiation dosimetry and improved radioligand therapy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed network holds promise for clinical applications such as personalized radiation dosimetry and improved radioligand therapy.
alignments.rationale: Both link the network to potential clinical applications in dosimetry and radioligand therapy; wording is slightly softened but meaning is preserved.

alignments.pub_claim_text: A multi-target network produced significantly higher segmentation accuracy than single-organ networks.
alignments.rationale: This specific quantitative comparison (multi-target vs single-organ networks) appears in the publication but was not present in the preprint claims list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We developed and compared computable phenotyping based on rules using the spaCy framework and a Large Language Model (LLM), GPT-4, for disease behavior and age at diagnosis of Crohn’s disease patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study is the first to explore computable phenotyping algorithms based on clinical narrative text for sub-phenotyping Crohn’s disease.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the development claim as a 'first' exploration; the core contribution (developing/comparing approaches) is the same but the publication emphasizes novelty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Overall, we observed similar or better performance using GPT-4 compared to the rules.
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-4 demonstrates similar or better performance compared to rule-based approaches for sub-phenotyping patients with Crohn’s disease.
alignments.rationale: Both state the main result that GPT-4 matches or outperforms rule-based methods.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: On a note-level, the F1 score was at least 0.90 for disease behavior and 0.82 for age at diagnosis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The F1 score for disease behavior is at least 0.90 and for age at diagnosis is at least 0.82 on a note-level using GPT-4.
alignments.rationale: Identical numeric performance claims between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We could not find statistical evidence for a difference to the performance of human experts on this task.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is no statistical evidence for a difference in performance between GPT-4 and human experts in this task.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the lack of statistical difference but with higher stated certainty; thus confidence is amplified in the published claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our findings underline the potential of LLMs for computable phenotyping.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings suggest that LLMs like GPT-4 may support large-scale cohort analyses from electronic health records and streamline chart review processes.
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the preprint's general claim of potential into specific suggested applications (cohort analyses and chart review), broadening the implied scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GNNs are superior, with an average improvement of the f1 score of 6.48% compared to baseline models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The GNN models constructed from patient graphs demonstrate a 6.48% average improvement in f1 score compared to baseline models.
alignments.rationale: Direct restatement of the quantitative performance improvement of GNN models.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GNNs are useful for performing additional clustering analyses that allow specific identification of therapeutic clusters related to particular combinations of diagnoses.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The GNN models are effective in identifying specific therapeutic clusters related to particular combinations of diagnoses.
alignments.rationale: Same result about GNNs enabling identification of therapeutic clusters; wording is equivalent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GNNs are a promising way to model the distribution of diagnoses in a patient population.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The GNN models provide a better approach for modeling the distribution of diagnoses in patient populations based on morbidities and comorbidities.
alignments.rationale: Publication frames the promise more specifically as an improved approach tied to morbidities/comorbidities; core conclusion is preserved with slightly more detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Network building is still challenging and prone to prejudice, depending on how ICD distribution affects the patient network embedding space.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Building patient graphs is challenging and can be biased due to the dependence on ICD distribution affecting the patient network embedding space.
alignments.rationale: The limitation about challenges and bias from ICD distribution is restated equivalently in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Additional work is needed to improve and better standardize patient embedding in graph structures for future investigations and applications.
alignments.rationale: This specific forward-looking recommendation to standardize patient embeddings does not appear among the listed publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The integration of machine learning approaches with mechanistic computational modelling can yield a reliable infrastructure for probabilistic simulations of the entire organism.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This work represents a step forward toward next-generation devices for precision and predictive medicine.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the broad integration claim as a step toward precision/predictive medicine; the core idea of integration enabling organism-level simulation is preserved but presented more as progress than definitive infrastructure.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed architecture utilizes graph neural networks to forecast clinically relevant endpoints and generative adversarial networks to demonstrate transcriptomic integrability.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed digital twin model provides a panoramic overview of current and future patient conditions by monitoring and forecasting clinically relevant endpoints.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the forecasting/monitoring capability (digital twin) described in the preprint; the architectural detail (GNN/GAN) is de-emphasized in this claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The investigation of the pathological effects of overexpression of ACE2 across different signalling pathways impacts cardiovascular functions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The use of a generative adversarial network (GAN) allows for the generation of multi-tissue expression data to find associations between cytokines and gene expression in the renin-angiotensin pathway.
alignments.rationale: The publication focuses on GAN-generated multi-tissue expression and associations in the renin-angiotensin pathway, which aligns with but is narrower/specific compared to the preprint's broader ACE2-pathway impact statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The work provides a proof of concept for integrating a large set of composable clinical models using molecular data to derive future trajectories of a patient's physiological state.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed digital twin model provides a panoramic overview of current and future patient conditions by monitoring and forecasting clinically relevant endpoints.
alignments.rationale: Both statements describe a proof-of-concept digital twin integrating models and molecular data to forecast patient trajectories and clinically relevant endpoints.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The graph representation of a computational patient has the potential to address significant technological challenges in integrating multiscale computational modelling with AI.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The graph representation of a computational patient has the potential to address significant technological challenges in integrating multiscale computational modeling with artificial intelligence.
alignments.rationale: Nearly verbatim equivalence: both claim the graph representation's potential to address integration challenges.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best performance was achieved by Boruta feature selection and RF model with an average area under the curve of more than 0.999 and accuracy of 0.9936 averaged over four classes and ten folds. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best performance was achieved by Boruta feature selection and RF model with an average area under the curve of more than 0.999 and accuracy of 0.9936 averaged over four classes and 10 folds. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same numeric performance metrics and model details with only trivial formatting differences (ten folds vs 10 folds).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The lowest classification was observed for class #2 with an accuracy of 0.9888, which is already an excellent result. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The lowest classification accuracy was observed for class #2 at 0.9888, which is still considered an excellent result. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same numeric result and interpretation with equivalent certainty and meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only 33 cases from 2509 cases (∼1.4%) were misclassified in the ten-fold strategy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Only 33 cases from 2509 cases (∼1.4%) were misclassified in the 10-fold strategy, indicating consistent performance over all folds. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same numeric misclassification result but adds an interpretive clause about consistency across folds, which clarifies rather than changes the result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We developed a fast, accurate, reliable, and explainable methodology to classify contrast media phases. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We developed a fast, accurate, reliable, and explainable methodology to classify contrast media phases that may be useful in data curation and annotation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core claim but adds a specific potential use (data curation/annotation) and overall reduces stated certainty from high to medium, tempering confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This fast, automated, reliable and explainable pipeline can tag every CT image using only image matrices. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not restate the strong claim that the pipeline can tag every CT image using only image matrices; instead it adds a different utility-focused claim, so the original specific statement is effectively removed from the published text.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Smart Pooling shows efficiency of 306% at a disease prevalence of 5% and efficiency of 107% at disease prevalence of up to 50%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The efficiency gains of using the predictor to support Dorfman testing were estimated by simulating the outcome of tests. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports that efficiency gains were estimated by simulation but does not reproduce the specific numerical efficiencies cited in the preprint, so the precise quantitative claim is revised and presented with less emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our method provides a cost-effective solution to increase the coverage of testing amid the COVID-19 pandemic. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Smart Pooling, a machine learning method, increases the efficiency of informed Dorfman testing for COVID-19 by arranging samples into all-negative pools. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the same high-level claim as a contribution: increased efficiency via an ML arrangement strategy, which clarifies how the method increases coverage/testing efficiency.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Artificial intelligence can be used complementarily with well-established techniques in medical practice. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Smart Pooling, a machine learning method, increases the efficiency of informed Dorfman testing for COVID-19 by arranging samples into all-negative pools. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents a concrete example of ML complementing testing workflows (Smart Pooling), which is the closest match to the preprint's general claim about AI complementarity, though less explicit about broad medical practice.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pooled testing can yield efficiency gains even as prevalence increases without compromising the sensitivity or specificity of the diagnostics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The false-negative error rates in detecting the ORF1ab and N genes of the virus in RT-qPCR dilutions were measured. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports measured false-negative rates (addressing sensitivity) and demonstrates efficiency gains in experiments, which is a more cautious, evidence-focused treatment of the preprint's broader claim that pooling does not compromise sensitivity/specificity.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Artificial intelligence models can continuously learn from every set of samples and thus increase their performance over time. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make an explicit claim about continuous learning from each sample set and performance improvement over time, so this specific preprint claim is not carried into the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning models can overestimate their performance due to biases in the experimental design and overfitting to the training dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep Learning (DL) models for COVID-19 detection report high cross-validation accuracy but are prone to overfitting when independent test sets are not utilized. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same concern about overestimation and overfitting when independent test sets are absent, matching the preprint's point directly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed deep learning architecture gives the best performance with the best possible setup. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed CVR-Net model achieves a binary classification accuracy of 99.8% with a single train-test data source, but this accuracy decreases to 98.4% and 88.7% with multiple and independent data sources, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides more nuanced performance results showing substantial drops on independent data sources, which revises the preprint's more absolute claim about best performance and narrows its generality.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A larger, more comprehensive database with less bias is necessary for developing tools applicable in real clinical settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a requirement for better-designed datasets that include independent test sets and balanced images across prediction classes for effective DL tool development in clinical settings. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication elaborates the preprint's recommendation with specific dataset design features (independent test sets, balance) and states it with higher certainty, clarifying and strengthening the original point.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Challenges and limitations exist in comparing and interpreting various deep learning algorithms’ results. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study highlights the challenges in developing DL models using existing public datasets that lack independent test sets. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the nature of the challenges (public datasets lacking independent test sets), which clarifies the preprint's general statement about comparison and interpretation difficulties.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In patients with severe aortic stenosis, Black patients experience lower rates of transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI) compared to White patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In severe aortic stenosis, TAVI and SAVR procedures were performed at lower rates among Black patients than among White patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same disparity in procedural rates (TAVI and SAVR) between Black and White patients, matching the preprint claim directly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Black patients have a longer time from diagnosis to surgical aortic valve replacement (AVR) compared to Asian and White patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: None of the publication claims report a longer time from diagnosis to AVR by race; the specific timing claim is not represented in the published claims and appears to have been omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Black ethnicity is independently associated with increased mortality in patients with severe aortic stenosis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Black patients experienced higher mortality in severe aortic stenosis, with a hazard ratio of 1.42. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication gives the same finding and quantifies the increased mortality, directly matching the preprint's claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Racial and ethnic disparities in the diagnosis, management, and outcome of aortic stenosis persist in a universal healthcare system. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An AI framework characterizes racial and ethnic disparities in aortic stenosis management, highlighting targets for future healthcare interventions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the broader conclusion as an AI framework that identifies disparities and potential intervention targets, which clarifies the original conclusion while somewhat tempering the certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Natural language processing can be leveraged to identify health inequities in the management of aortic stenosis. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An AI framework characterizes racial and ethnic disparities in aortic stenosis management, highlighting targets for future healthcare interventions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication describes an AI framework that characterizes disparities (consistent with using NLP/AI to identify inequities), clarifying the contribution by situating it within a broader framework for intervention targeting.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Dry eye disease (DED) has a prevalence of between 5 and 50%, depending on the diagnostic criteria used and population under study. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Dry eye disease (DED) has a prevalence of between 5 and 50%, depending on the diagnostic criteria used and population under study. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same prevalence range and qualifiers as the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Artificial intelligence (AI) systems could lead to more objective diagnosis of DED. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Artificial intelligence (AI) systems could lead to more objective diagnosis of DED. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion about AI and objective diagnosis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Powerful machine learning techniques have been harnessed to understand nuances in patient data and medical images for consistent diagnosis of DED. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Powerful machine learning techniques have been harnessed to understand nuances in patient data and medical images for consistent diagnosis of DED. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this descriptive result about ML techniques applied to DED data and images.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI has been employed in a wide range of DED clinical tests and research applications, primarily for interpretation of interferometry, slit-lamp and meibography images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI has been employed in a wide range of DED clinical tests and research applications, primarily for interpretation of interferometry, slit-lamp and meibography images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication contains the same enumeration of clinical tests and image modalities where AI has been applied.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Much work is still needed on model development, clinical testing and standardisation for AI applications in DED. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Much work is still needed on model development, clinical testing and standardisation for AI applications in DED. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same limitations and need for further work in model development, testing, and standardisation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) for ADHD classification achieves a classification accuracy of 88%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The convolutional neural network (CNN) achieves a classification accuracy of 88% ± 1.12% in distinguishing ADHD patients from healthy controls. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same 88% accuracy but adds a precision measure (±1.12%), which clarifies the result without changing its scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DCNN model outperforms resting state EEG spectrograms and avoids the need for manual selection of EEG spectral or channel features. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The event-related spectrograms provide greater accuracy compared to resting state EEG spectrograms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the superiority versus resting-state spectrograms but frames it as event-related spectrograms' advantage and omits the claim about avoiding manual feature selection, narrowing and tempering the original contribution claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ADHD patients exhibit decreased power in the alpha band and increased power in the delta-theta band around 100ms compared to healthy controls. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The main features exciting the CNN nodes are a decreased power in the alpha band and an increased power in the delta-theta band around 100 ms for ADHD patients compared to healthy controls. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same spectral differences and links them to features driving the CNN, which clarifies the finding without changing its content or scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings suggest attentional and inhibition deficits as potential pathophysiological signatures of ADHD. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim corresponds to the specific pathophysiological interpretation about attentional and inhibition deficits; this interpretive claim does not appear in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The methodology has the potential to develop CNS biomarkers of practical clinical utility. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results suggest that deep networks may provide a useful tool for the analysis of EEG dynamics even from relatively small datasets. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the methodological potential more cautiously as a tool for EEG analysis rather than explicitly claiming development of clinically useful CNS biomarkers, narrowing and tempering the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ai-corona framework achieved AUC scores of 0.997, 0.989, and 0.954 on different test sets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ai-corona framework achieved AUC scores of 0.997, 0.989, and 0.954 on different test sets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric AUC results are identical between preprint and publication, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ai-corona outperforms all other deep learning models and RT-PCR in diagnosing COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Ai-corona outperforms all alternative models in COVID-19 infection diagnosis. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the superiority over alternative models but drops the explicit RT-PCR comparison, narrowing the scope and modestly tempering the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Incorporating ai-corona's assistance increases both the speed and accuracy of expert diagnosis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Incorporating ai-corona's assistance increased both the speed and accuracy of expert diagnosis. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The effect on speed and accuracy is reported in both versions, but the publication reduces certainty (high→medium), so confidence is tempered while the claim content is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The artificial neural network achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristics curve of 0.97 for the validation dataset and 0.96 for the test dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The neural network achieved an excellent performance in the detection of definite radiographic sacroiliitis with an AUC of 0.97 and 0.94 for the validation and test datasets, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both report the same validation AUC but different test AUC (0.96 vs 0.94); this is a numeric revision that tempers the original performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sensitivity and specificity of the neural network for the validation dataset were 0.90 and 0.93, respectively, and for the test dataset were 0.87 and 0.97, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sensitivity and specificity for the cut-off weighting both measurements equally were 88% and 95% for the validation and 92% and 81% for the test set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports substantially different sensitivity/specificity numbers for both splits and specifies a cut-off criterion, so the original numeric performance claims are revised and effectively tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Cohen’s kappa between the neural network and the reference judgements was 0.80 for both validation and test sets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Cohen’s kappa between the neural network and the reference judgements were 0.79 and 0.72 for the validation and test sets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides different kappa values (slightly lower, and differing between sets) so the agreement claim is revised and somewhat tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The absolute agreement on the classification yielded 91% for the validation set and 90% for the test set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No corresponding absolute agreement percentages appear in the publication claims, so this specific numeric claim was removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning (ML) offers promising support for systematic review (SR) creation. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Although LLMs show promise in supporting systematic review creation, fully established or validated applications are often lacking. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication narrows the focus from general ML to LLMs specifically and frames the promise while noting limitations, which clarifies and narrows the original general claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPT was the most commonly used large language model (LLM) to support SR production. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Generative Pretrained Transformer (GPT) was the most commonly used LLM, appearing in 89% of the studies reviewed. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same finding with a specific proportion, preserving the claim content and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLM application included 10 of 13 defined SR steps, most often literature search. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLM approaches covered 10 of 13 defined systematic review steps, with the most frequent applications in literature search, study selection, and data extraction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication confirms the 10/13 coverage but expands the list of most frequent applications beyond literature search, clarifying and broadening the original statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Validation studies predominated, but fully established LLM applications are rare. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Although LLMs show promise in supporting systematic review creation, fully established or validated applications are often lacking. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions emphasize the lack of fully established/validated applications; the publication reiterates this point, keeping the substantive conclusion consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLM research for SR conduct is surging, highlighting the increasing relevance. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The rapid increase in research on LLMs for evidence synthesis production highlights their growing relevance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same trend but reduces certainty (high→medium), tempering the confidence while leaving the claim content unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Head movement was a significant predictor of schizophrenia diagnosis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Head movement was identified as a significant predictor of schizophrenia diagnosis (p = 0.02). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly affirms head movement as a significant predictor with a p-value, directly matching the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Head movement has a negative relationship with schizophrenia symptom severity, primarily with negative symptoms of schizophrenia. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a negative relationship between head movement and schizophrenia symptom severity (p = 0.04), particularly with negative symptoms. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same negative relationship and emphasis on negative symptoms, matching the preprint result (difference in reported certainty is minor).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Remote, smartphone-based assessments were able to capture meaningful visual behavior for objective measurement of head movement. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Remote, smartphone-based assessments can effectively capture visual behavior for objective measurement of head movement. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the same conclusion but with stronger wording and higher reported certainty, amplifying the confidence in the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Measurements of head movement acquired were able to accurately classify schizophrenia diagnosis and quantify symptom severity in patients with schizophrenia. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Measurements of head movement can accurately classify schizophrenia diagnosis and quantify symptom severity in patients. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion regarding classification and quantification of severity, with consistent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Two main cognitive clusters, 'impaired' and 'intact', were identified across all cognitive domains in clinical high risk (CHR) youth compared to healthy controls (HC).
alignments.pub_claim_text: Two cognitive clusters, 'impaired' and 'intact,' were identified in the CHR group.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same primary result about two clusters with no substantive change in meaning or scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The cognitively impaired cluster group demonstrated an overall floor effect and did not deteriorate further over time, while a 'catch up' trajectory was observed in the attention and working memory domain.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The impaired group displayed a 'floor' effect with no noticeable deterioration but showed a 'catch-up' trajectory in attention and working memory.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same observation about the floor effect and catch-up in attention/working memory with equivalent framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Functioning was significantly better in the cognitively intact group than in the impaired group at baseline.
alignments.rationale: No corresponding baseline functioning comparison appears in the publication claims; the publication does not report this specific result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings indicate that attention and working memory are important to distinguish between CHR youth with intact cognition and healthy controls.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly make this claim about distinguishing CHR-intact and healthy controls, so the preprint claim is not represented.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Early evaluation covering several cognitive domains is crucial for identifying trajectories of improvement and deterioration for tailoring interventions in individuals at CHR for psychosis.
alignments.rationale: The recommendation about early multi-domain evaluation and tailoring interventions is not present among the publication claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The impaired group had a higher conversion rate and more cases diagnosed with psychotic disorders than the intact group.
alignments.rationale: This publication-level result (conversion differences) was not reported in the preprint and thus appears as an added claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Most cognitive trajectories showed a positive relationship with functional outcomes.
alignments.rationale: The publication includes a claim linking cognitive trajectories to functioning that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The study provides evidence for distinct cognitive subtypes in CHR, independent of conversion status.
alignments.rationale: This higher-level conclusion about subtypes being independent of conversion is new in the publication and not in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed classification model achieved a sensitivity of 99.4, specificity of 96.7, AUC of 98.3, and accuracy of 98.5.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed deep learning model achieved a sensitivity of 99.4, specificity of 96.7, AUC of 98.3, and accuracy of 98.5 in diagnosing B-ALL lymphoblast and normal cells.
alignments.rationale: The numerical performance metrics and diagnostic context are the same between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: State-of-the-art CNN models alone did not achieve acceptable performance in diagnosing B-ALL and normal blood cells due to the close similarity of their nuclei.
alignments.pub_claim_text: CNN models alone had low sensitivity and poor performance in diagnosing cancerous and normal cells due to the close similarity of their nuclei.
alignments.rationale: Both texts convey the same negative assessment of standalone CNN performance and the same reason (nuclear similarity).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed DL-based model can classify blood cancerous cells from normal cells with high accuracy without operator intervention in cell feature determination.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method can classify cancerous blood cells from normal cells with high accuracy without the operator’s intervention in cell feature determination.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same claim about operator-independent high-accuracy classification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ensemble classifier was developed by combining the performance of the four best-performing CNN models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ensemble classifier, based on the majority voting technique, effectively combines the capabilities of the best-performing CNN models.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds method detail (majority voting) about how the ensemble combines models, clarifying the original description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep neural network analysis of the posterior tibial artery waveform provides independent prediction of death in PAD patients at 5 years.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep neural network analysis of the posterior tibial artery waveform provides independent prediction of death at 5 years.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result about independent prediction of 5-year mortality using the waveform analysis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep neural network analysis predicts major adverse cardiac events (MACE) with a hazard ratio of 1.98 at 5 years.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis predicts major adverse cardiac events with a hazard ratio of 1.97 at 5 years.
alignments.rationale: The hazard ratio is effectively the same (1.98 vs 1.97); the publication reports the same predictive finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis predicts major adverse limb events (MALE) with a hazard ratio of 11.65 at 5 years.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep neural network analysis predicts major adverse limb events with a hazard ratio of 11.03 at 5 years.
alignments.rationale: Both claim large increased MALE risk, but the hazard ratio point estimate changed between versions, so the result was revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An artificial intelligence enabled analysis of a resting Doppler arterial waveform can identify major adverse outcomes among PAD patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: An artificial intelligence-enabled analysis of Doppler arterial waveforms enables identification of major adverse outcomes among patients with peripheral artery disease.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the conclusion but presents it with slightly lower stated certainty (medium) than the preprint (high), so the claim is clarified and confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Fundus images coupled with genotypes can predict late AMD progression with an averaged area under the curve (AUC) value of 0.85.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Fundus images coupled with genotypes can predict late AMD progression with an averaged area-under-the-curve value of 0.85.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the identical performance result combining images and genotypes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results using fundus images alone showed an averaged AUC of 0.81.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results using fundus images alone showed an averaged area under the receiver operating characteristic curve value of 0.81.
alignments.rationale: The image-only performance AUC is reported unchanged and equivalently worded.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: None of the existing machine learning methods have utilized both genetic and image data for predicting AMD progression.
alignments.pub_claim_text: None of the existing methods have used both genetic and image data for predicting AMD progression.
alignments.rationale: Both versions assert the same novelty claim about combining genetic and image data.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A modified deep convolutional neural network (CNN) was used to predict an eye as having progressed to late AMD.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A modified deep convolutional neural network was used to predict whether an eye had progressed to late AMD.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same methodological contribution with equivalent wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model was implemented in a cloud-based application for individual risk assessment.
alignments.rationale: The publication claims do not mention a cloud-based application implementation, so this contribution appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our ensemble model performs with an accuracy of 82.5% for ASD screening.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our ensemble model performs with an accuracy of 82.5% for screening Autism Spectrum Disorders (ASD).
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the exact reported accuracy for ASD screening.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Additional combinations of our model were developed to achieve higher specificity of 92.5% and sensitivity of 90%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Additional combinations of our model were developed to achieve higher specificity of 92.5% and sensitivity of 90%.
alignments.rationale: The performance trade-off claims are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We found a relationship between the neural network modalities and specific audio versus video ASD characteristics.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We found a relationship between the neural network modalities and specific audio versus video ASD characteristics.
alignments.rationale: Both versions report the same modality-characteristic relationship.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our neural network implementation was effective in taking into account different features that are currently standardized under the gold standard ASD assessment.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our neural network implementation was effective in taking into account different features that are currently standardized under the gold standard ASD assessment.
alignments.rationale: The claim about capturing standardized assessment features is unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models classified PD and ET based on balance and gait characteristics better than chance or logistic regression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models classified PD and ET based on balance and gait characteristics better than the dummy model or logistic regression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with slightly different wording ('dummy model' vs 'chance'), which clarifies the baseline comparator without changing meaning or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The highest F1-score was 0.61 for neural networks, indicating superior performance compared to other machine learning models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The highest F1-score achieved was 0.61 using a neural network model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the identical numeric result and model, preserving the claim's meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results demonstrated the utility of machine learning models to classify different movement disorders. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study demonstrated the utility of machine learning models to classify different movement disorders based on balance and gait characteristics collected from wearable sensors. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the data source detail (wearable sensors) but maintains the same overall conclusion about utility for classifying movement disorders.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Further study will provide a more accurate clinical tool to help clinical decision-making. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Future studies using a well-balanced data set are needed to confirm the potential clinical utility of machine learning models to discern between PD and ET. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the optimistic future-tool claim into a more cautious limitation, specifying need for balanced data and framing the outcome as confirmation rather than guaranteed tool development.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A deep learning system can accurately predict interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy (IFTA) from kidney ultrasound images. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A deep learning algorithm can accurately and independently predict interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy from kidney ultrasonography images. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the core conclusion but adds the term 'independently' and slightly different wording ('ultrasonography'), clarifying the claim without changing its substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning classifier achieved an accuracy of 87% in the independent test set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: At the patient level, the deep learning algorithm achieved a precision of 0.9003 and an accuracy of 0.8955. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports updated/alternate numeric performance (patient-level accuracy ~89.6%) and presents precision as well; the metric values and level differ from the preprint, so the result is revised and more specific.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The accuracy of the deep learning system was highest for IFTA grade 1 at 98% and for IFTA grade 4 at 82%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the grade-specific accuracy values present in the preprint; this specific claim is omitted in the published text.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance metrics for the deep learning classifier in the validation set showed excellent precision (90%), recall (76%), accuracy (84%), and F1-score (80%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: At the patient level, the deep learning algorithm achieved a precision of 0.9003 and an accuracy of 0.8955. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves precision (~90%) but updates accuracy (to ~89.6%) and omits some other metrics (recall, F1) or reframes them at the patient level, so the reported performance is revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The internal validation of the modified Inception transfer-learning model achieved a total accuracy of 89.5% with specificity of 0.88 and sensitivity of 0.87. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The internal validation achieved a total accuracy of 89.5% with a specificity of 0.88 and sensitivity of 0.87. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same internal validation metrics without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The external testing dataset showed a total accuracy of 79.3% with specificity of 0.83 and sensitivity of 0.67. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The external testing dataset showed a total accuracy of 79.3% with a specificity of 0.83 and sensitivity of 0.67. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Metrics and wording are the same between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In 54 COVID-19 images that first two nucleic acid test results were negative, 46 were predicted as COVID-19 positive by the algorithm, with an accuracy of 85.2%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In 54 COVID-19 images, the first two nucleic acid test results were negative, and 46 were predicted as COVID-19 positive by the algorithm, with an accuracy of 85.2%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the specific case-series result verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These results demonstrate the proof-of-principle for using artificial intelligence to extract radiological features for timely and accurate COVID-19 diagnosis. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These results demonstrate the proof-of-principle for using artificial intelligence to extract radiological features for timely and accurate COVID-19 diagnosis. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The concluding claim is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ChatGPT-4o outperformed the other AI models with a score of 77.86, much higher than Gemini Advance and Copilot. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ChatGPT-4o scored 77.86, outperforming the Gemini Advanced (57.14), Copilot (47.29), and JHDs (61.57). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same score and the same comparative conclusion against other AI models; additional comparator numbers are provided but do not change the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CG4o’s performance was only slightly below that of the senior doctor, with no statistically significant differences between CG4o and SHD. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ChatGPT-4o's performance closely approached that of the SHDs (80.43), with no statistically significant difference between the two (p > 0.05). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same comparative result and statistical conclusion with explicit numbers, preserving the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CG4o demonstrated outstanding performance in CTG interpretation, surpassing junior doctors and other AI-LLMs. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ChatGPT-4o demonstrated superior performance among the AI-LLMs and surpassed JHDs in CTG interpretation. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same conclusion about superior performance relative to junior doctors and other models using similar language.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI-LLMs, particularly CG4o, showed promising potential as valuable tools in clinical practice to assist obstetricians. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI-LLMs, particularly ChatGPT-4o, are promising tools for assisting obstetricians, improving diagnostic accuracy, and enhancing obstetric patient care. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expands on the original claim by specifying potential benefits (improving diagnostic accuracy and patient care), which clarifies and modestly amplifies the expected impact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning algorithm achieved 0.81 Area Under Receiver Operating Curve (AUC) when averaging scores from multiple images for predicting the development of referrable diabetic retinopathy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm achieved 0.81 area under receiver operating curve (AUC) when averaging scores from multiple images on the task of predicting development of referrable DR. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same numeric result and condition without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm achieved 0.76 AUC when using a single image to predict the development of referrable diabetic retinopathy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm achieved 0.76 AUC when using a single image to predict the development of referrable DR. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The single-image AUC is reported identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results suggest that the risk of diabetic retinopathy may be predicted from fundus photography alone. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our results suggest that risk of DR may be predicted from fundus photography alone. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is restated verbatim and retains the same level of certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Prediction of personalized risk of diabetic retinopathy may become key in treatment and contribute to patient compliance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Prediction of personalised risk of DR may become key in treatment and contribute to patient compliance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same forward-looking conclusion about personalized risk prediction and its clinical value.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SkinGPT-4 is the world’s first interactive dermatology diagnostic system based on multimodal large language models. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SkinGPT-4 is an interactive dermatology diagnostic system based on multimodal large language models. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core contribution but omits the superlative "world's first," reducing the novelty claim and therefore tempering and narrowing the original assertion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SkinGPT-4 can autonomously evaluate skin images, identify characteristics and categories of skin conditions, and provide interactive treatment recommendations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The system can autonomously evaluate skin images, identify characteristics and categories of skin conditions, and provide interactive treatment recommendations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this capability verbatim, maintaining meaning, scope, and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SkinGPT-4 enhances users’ comprehension of their medical conditions and facilitates improved communication between patients and doctors. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No corresponding statement about user comprehension or patient–doctor communication appears in the publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SkinGPT-4 could expedite the diagnostic process for dermatologists and promote healthcare equity in underdeveloped areas. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication claims do not include the anticipated impacts on diagnostic speed or healthcare equity present in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: SkinGPT-4 has been quantitatively evaluated on 150 real-life cases with board-certified dermatologists. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This evaluation detail is present in the publication but was not stated among the preprint claims, so it is an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The correlation between systolic phase duration estimated by the algorithm and that marked by the researcher is strong for both ABP and PPG waveforms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The correlation between systolic phase duration estimated by the algorithm and that marked by the researcher is strong for both ABP and PPG waveforms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result directly with the same meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm had a lower mean error of dicrotic notch detection for both ABP and PPG waveforms compared to the established second derivative method. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm has a lower mean error of DN detection compared to the established second derivative method for both ABP and PPG waveforms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Wording differs minimally (DN vs dicrotic notch) but the claim is substantively identical.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm demonstrates high accuracy of dicrotic notch detection for signal-to-noise ratios of >= -9 dB for ABP waveforms and >= -12 dB for PPG waveforms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm demonstrates a high rate of detectability of DN detection for SNR of >= -9 dB for ABP waveforms and >= -12 dB for PPG waveforms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the detectability/SNR thresholds with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed IEM-based algorithm can detect dicrotic notch in both ABP and PPG waveforms with low computational cost, even in cases where it is not distinctly defined. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed IEM-based algorithm can detect DN in both ABP and PPG waveforms with low computational cost, even in cases where it is not distinctly defined. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the conclusion and scope of the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm can potentially serve as a valuable tool for extracting features from ABP and PPG waveforms in medical applications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm can potentially serve as a valuable tool for extracting features from ABP and PPG waveforms in medical applications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis of 27,511 adverse events related to cochlear implant devices revealed that most events involved patient injury. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Most adverse events involving cochlear implants were related to patient injury, device malfunction, and death. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning algorithms achieved an average prediction accuracy of 88.6% for identifying the specific cochlear implant manufacturer based on adverse event narratives. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The random forest, linear SVC, naïve Bayes, and logistic algorithms achieved average prediction accuracies of 74.8%, 86.0%, 88.5%, and 88.6%, respectively, for identifying the specific cochlear implant manufacturer based on adverse event narratives. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides algorithm-specific accuracies showing 88.6% applies to one method (logistic) rather than an overall average of 88.6%, tempering and refining the original aggregate claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates that supervised machine learning can effectively analyze patterns in adverse event reports to improve patient safety and device design. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI model classified COVID-19 and non-COVID-19 patients with 0.78 sensitivity, 0.82 specificity, and 0.89 area under the curve (AUC) during 10-fold cross-validation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model classified COVID-19 and non-COVID-19 patients with 0.78 sensitivity and 0.82 specificity at 10-fold cross-validation. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: For the independent dataset, the AI showed 0.80 sensitivity and 0.81 specificity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: For the independent dataset, the deep learning model demonstrated 0.80 sensitivity and 0.81 specificity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The independent-dataset performance is stated identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Radiologists' reading obtained lower sensitivity and specificity compared to the AI model. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Radiologists' reading achieved 0.63 sensitivity and 0.78 specificity in Centre 1 and 0.64 sensitivity and 0.86 specificity in Centre 2. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication supplies center-specific numbers showing radiologists had consistently lower sensitivity but mixed specificity (one center's specificity exceeds the AI), so the blanket statement that both metrics were lower is revised and tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The preliminary experience indicates an interesting potential of AI for COVID-19 diagnosis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed model CTnet-10 achieved an accuracy of 82.1% in diagnosing COVID-19 from CT scan images. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The VGG-19 model demonstrated superior performance with an accuracy of 94.52% compared to other deep learning models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this comparative performance claim exactly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Automated diagnosis of COVID-19 from CT scan images can serve as a quick and efficient method for screening. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Automated diagnosis of COVID-19 from CT scan images can serve as a quick and efficient method for screening. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of the primary model declined with age. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The primary model performed significantly worse in Black patients aged 0 to 40 years compared with all other racial groups in this age group. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Disparities in model performance did not improve with the integration of race, ethnicity, sex, and age into model architecture. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Using probability thresholds individualized for race, age, and sex offered substantial improvements in F1 scores. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The new physiological phenotypes are generalizable to other clinical and physiological settings, potentially personalizing medical care.

alignments.pub_claim_text: DMRs in all comparisons were enriched for gene exons, CpG islands, and bivalent chromatin, and mapped to genes related to cardiac and immune functions.
