alignments.preprint_claim_text: The device allows for convenient monitoring of HIV viral loads and has the potential to combat the HIV/AIDS epidemic. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication's summarized claims do not restate the broader public-health/convenience assertion about monitoring viral loads or combating the epidemic, so this high-level conclusion appears omitted.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The strong fluorescence in the digital chip simplified the design of thermal and optical modules, allowing for a compact device. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds an engineering/design observation about fluorescence simplifying device modules that was not present in the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning with neural network models demonstrates potential technology in prostate cancer diagnosis with relatively high accuracy above 70% of sensitivity/specificity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The accuracy of neural network ML models in prostate cancer diagnosis using ultrasound images was relatively high, with an AUC value above 0.7. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Ultrasound imaging shows good accuracy in the diagnosis of prostate cancer when analyzed with machine learning models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The accuracy of neural network ML models in prostate cancer diagnosis using ultrasound images was relatively high, with an AUC value above 0.7. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Several factors, including the model of AI, mode and type of transrectal sonography, Gleason grading, and PSA level, affect AI accuracy in prostate cancer diagnosis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Factors affecting the accuracy of artificial intelligence in prostate cancer diagnosis included the AI model, mode and type of transrectal sonography, Gleason grading, and prostate-specific antigen level. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Image-based machine learning models would be helpful for doctors to decide whether or not to perform a prostate biopsy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This modality is promising for prostate cancer diagnosis as it can provide instant information for further workup and help doctors decide whether to perform a prostate biopsy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis of the six articles revealed a varied ROC curve of 0.76-0.98 for ultrasound in the diagnosis of prostate cancer. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The included articles showed a varied area under the curve (AUC) of 0.76–0.98. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We found 68 clinical concepts significantly associated with COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We found 68 concepts significantly associated with a positive COVID-19 test. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Symptoms associated with increasing risk of COVID-19 include 'absent sense of smell', 'fever', 'with cough fever', and 'ageusia'. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using ConceptWAS, we were able to detect loss of sense of smell or taste three weeks prior to their inclusion as symptoms of the disease by the CDC. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using ConceptWAS, we were able to detect loss of smell and loss of taste three weeks prior to their inclusion as symptoms of the disease by the CDC. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ConceptWAS is a high-throughput approach for exploring specific symptoms of a disease like COVID-19. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ConceptWAS offers a promise for enabling EHR-powered early disease manifestations identification. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neuropsychiatric symptoms were common up to 150 days after initial hospitalization for COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Neuropsychiatric symptoms were common up to 150 days after initial hospitalization for COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result about persistence of neuropsychiatric symptoms up to 150 days.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The frequency of neuropsychiatric symptoms was generally similar among individuals hospitalized for COVID-19 and those hospitalized for other indications. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The frequency of neuropsychiatric symptoms was generally similar among individuals hospitalized for COVID-19 and those hospitalized for other indications. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same comparative frequency result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Mood and anxiety symptoms were less frequent among COVID-19 patients compared to non-COVID-19 patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Mood and anxiety symptoms were less frequent among COVID-19 patients compared to non-COVID-19 patients. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 may benefit from standard treatments developed for rehabilitation after hospitalization. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: MONET enables AI transparency across the entire AI system development pipeline. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pregnant individuals with an SBP PRS in the top quartile had higher blood pressures throughout pregnancy compared to patients within the lowest quartile SBP PRS. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Integrating clinical and genetic factors into predictive models can inform personalized preeclampsia risk and achieve higher predictive power than the current practice. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Integrating clinical factors into predictive models can inform personalized preeclampsia risk and achieve higher predictive power than the current practice. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Adding the systolic blood pressure polygenic risk score to the models did not improve the performance significantly based on De Long test comparing the area under the curve of models with and without the polygenic score. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm identified a higher number of probable dementia cases compared with self-reported cases. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The 'Likely Dementia' status was more prevalent in older people and displayed a 2:1 female/male ratio. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Nine factors were associated with an increased risk of transition to dementia, including low education, hearing loss, and hypertension. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: “Likely Dementia” status was more prevalent in older people, displayed a 2:1 female/male ratio, and was associated with nine factors that increased risk of transition to dementia. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The list of nine associated risk factors is incorporated into the same publication claim; the substantive content is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results were replicated in the English Longitudinal Study of Ageing (ELSA) cohort with good accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Results were replicated in the ELSA cohort with good accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning clustering can be used to study dementia determinants and outcomes in longitudinal population ageing surveys. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning clustering can be used to study dementia determinants and outcomes in longitudinal population ageing surveys in which dementia clinical diagnosis is lacking. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication narrows the applicability to surveys lacking clinical diagnosis (narrowed scope) while increasing stated certainty from medium to high, thereby clarifying and amplifying the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models based on Electronic Medical Records can moderately predict depression in early pregnancy among racial/ethnic minority women. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models could moderately predict early pregnancy depression in racial/ethnic minority women using electronic medical records. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same moderate predictive ability for the same population and data source with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of the machine learning model was low, with a ROCAUC of 0.67. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best-performing machine learning model's performance was low, with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.61. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model's performance was biased against low-income minority women, despite being based on a sample mostly composed of these communities. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model performed worse for low-income minority women, with area under the receiver operating characteristic curve values of 57% for Black women and 59% for Latina women compared to 64% for White women. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides subgroup performance metrics that substantiate and clarify the preprint's claim of biased performance, without changing its conclusion or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Well-known factors associated with perinatal depression include unplanned pregnancy and being single, while underexplored factors include self-report pain levels and lower levels of prenatal vitamin supplement intake. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Known perinatal depression risk factors such as unplanned pregnancy and being single were identified, along with underexplored factors like self-reported pain and lower prenatal vitamin intake. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same set of known and underexplored risk factors; wording shifts are minor and the substantive content is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI had an AUC of 0.82. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI had an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of .82. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same numeric AUC result with equivalent certainty and meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: At the group level, the AI outperformed random forecasting with a Brier Skill Score of 0.53. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: At the group level, the AI outperformed random forecasting with a Brier Skill Score of .53. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The group-level Brier Skill Score and conclusion are reported identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: At the individual level, the AI outperformed random forecasts in 28% of cases. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: At the individual level, the AI outperformed random forecasting in 28% of cases. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The individual-level percent and interpretation are the same between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The moving average outperformed the AI at both the group and individual levels. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI forecasting tool did not outperform a very simple moving average forecasting in this prospective cohort. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same outcome (AI did not beat the moving average) but in less granular terms (cohort-level statement rather than explicitly both group and individual levels).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI model should be replaced as it did not outperform a very simple moving average forecasting this prospective cohort. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI forecasting tool did not outperform a very simple moving average forecasting in this prospective cohort. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint makes an explicit recommendation to replace the model; the publication omits that recommendation and reports the performance conclusion only, a weakening of the original prescriptive claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: If pre-enrollment diaries were included, the AI significantly outperformed both comparators. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This conditional finding about pre-enrollment diaries is new in the publication and has no counterpart in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Early detection and treatment of sepsis can help reduce morbidity and mortality rates of 20% or higher. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Monocyte Distribution Width (MDW) is a new biomarker approved by the US Food and Drug Administration for sepsis. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication discusses MDW predictive value but does not repeat the FDA-approval statement present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The value of MDW is grossly attenuated in the presence of other routinely reported hematologic parameters and vital signs data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The predictive value of Monocyte Distribution Width (MDW) is significantly reduced when other hematological parameters and vital signs are considered. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Complete blood count with differential (CBC-DIFF) together with vital signs data can be used as a substitute for MDW in high accuracy machine learning algorithms to screen for poor outcomes associated with sepsis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Complete blood count with differential (CBD-DIFF) alongside vital signs can effectively replace MDW in high accuracy machine learning algorithms for screening poor outcomes associated with sepsis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same substitution claim but tones down certainty (high→medium) and frames it as a conclusion, indicating a tempered confidence.

alignments.pub_claim_text: MDW does not significantly enhance prediction models when combined with routinely available parameters and vital signs. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Hospitals can rely on existing parameters with high accuracy machine learning models to predict sepsis outcomes effectively, thereby reducing the need for specialized tests like MDW. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The policy/operational recommendation about hospitals relying on existing parameters is newly articulated in the publication and absent from the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning model achieved an AUROC of 0.978 for detecting severe aortic stenosis with 95.4% specificity and 90% sensitivity in the temporally distinct test set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.978 for detecting severe aortic stenosis in a temporally distinct test set. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model maintained its diagnostic performance in geographically distinct cohorts, achieving an AUROC of 0.972 in California and 0.915 in New England. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model maintained diagnostic performance in geographically distinct cohorts, achieving AUROCs of 0.952 in California and 0.942 in New England. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study developed and externally validated an automated approach for severe aortic stenosis detection using single-view 2D echocardiography. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A novel deep learning model was developed to identify severe aortic stenosis using two-dimensional parasternal long axis videos from transthoracic echocardiography. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the contribution and external validation of an automated single-view 2D echocardiography deep learning approach consistent with the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Predicted probabilities among non-severe aortic stenosis cases were associated with worse quantitative metrics of aortic stenosis, suggesting an association with various stages of severity. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Predicted probabilities among non-severe aortic stenosis cases were associated with worse quantitative metrics of aortic stenosis, suggesting an association with various stages of severity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same association between predicted probabilities and quantitative disease metrics with similar framing and certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The model was interpretable, with saliency maps identifying the aortic valve, mitral annulus, and left atrium as predictive regions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Interpretability details using saliency maps are newly presented in the publication and were not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combined rule-based (CRB) algorithm achieved a precision of 0.85, recall of 0.71, and F1 score of 0.77 against the gold standard set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combined rule-based (CRB) algorithm achieved a precision of 0.85, recall of 0.71, and F1 score of 0.77 against the gold standard set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance metrics are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The prevalence of pharmacological SAMS identified using the CRB algorithm was 1.9%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The prevalence of pharmacological SAMS identified in the statin cohort was 1.9%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported prevalence and context are equivalent in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Selective risk factors for pharmacological SAMS included female gender, coronary artery disease, hypothyroidism, and use of immunosuppressants or fibrates. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Selective risk factors for pharmacological SAMS included female gender, coronary artery disease, hypothyroidism, and use of immunosuppressants or fibrates. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The same risk factors are reported with equivalent framing in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A simple pharmacological SAMS phenotyping algorithm was developed and validated for creating SAMS case/control cohorts for further analysis. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A simple pharmacological SAMS phenotyping algorithm was developed and validated for creating SAMS case/control cohorts. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The development and validation claim is preserved in the publication with equivalent meaning and intended use.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PixelPrint 4D is a 3D printing method designed to fabricate lifelike, patient-specific deformable lung phantoms for CT imaging. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PixelPrint 4D facilitates the creation of highly realistic RMPs, exceeding the capabilities of existing models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both statements describe PixelPrint 4D as a method for creating realistic, patient-specific deformable phantoms; the publication frames it as exceeding existing models.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The phantom demonstrated accurate replication of patient lung structures, textures, and attenuation profiles. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The phantom closely replicated patient lung structures, textures, and attenuation profiles. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same replication fidelity claim with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: It exhibited accurate nonrigid deformations, volume changes, and attenuation changes under compression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The relationship between attenuation and local volume changes in the phantom had a strong correlation with that of the patient. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a more specific quantitative description of attenuation–volume relationships, narrowing the preprint's broader statement about deformation and attenuation under compression.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PixelPrint 4D enables the production of highly realistic RMPs, surpassing existing models. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PixelPrint 4D facilitates the creation of highly realistic RMPs, exceeding the capabilities of existing models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same concluding claim but with higher stated certainty and stronger framing.

alignments.pub_claim_text: SSIM was measured as 0.93 between the patient and phantom lung, suggesting a high level of structural accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific SSIM measurement and quantitative structural-accuracy result appear in the publication but were not present in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The mean tumor motion errors in the phantom were 6le0.7�a1�b10.6�mm in each orthogonal direction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports numeric motion-error measurements that were not stated in the preprint and therefore represent added quantitative detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LiVersa answered all 10 questions correctly when forced to provide a 'yes' or 'no' answer. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LiVersa answered all 10 questions correctly in a knowledge assessment comparison. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same result (all 10 correct) but frames it as a knowledge assessment comparison rather than the forced yes/no detail, so the claim is clarified rather than novel.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Full detailed responses with justifications and rationales were not completely correct for three of the questions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LiVersa's outputs were more accurate than those of ChatGPT 4 and Meta AI 2, but were rated less comprehensive and safe. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the preprint's note about incomplete detailed responses into a comparative assessment (accuracy vs. comprehensiveness/safety), changing emphasis and tempering certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We demonstrated the ability to build disease-specific and PHI-compliant LLMs using RAG. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The LiVersa prototype is a proof of concept for utilizing RAG to customize LLMs for clinical use cases. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the proof-of-concept claim for RAG-customized clinical LLMs but omits the explicit PHI-compliance and disease-specific wording, narrowing the scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LiVersa demonstrated more specificity in answering questions related to clinical hepatology. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LiVersa demonstrated higher accuracy in answering questions related to hepatology. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication rephrases the preprint's claim in terms of higher accuracy (a stronger quantitative framing) and reports higher certainty, amplifying confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The LiVersa prototype is a proof of concept for utilizing RAG to customize LLMs for clinical uses. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The LiVersa prototype is a proof of concept for utilizing RAG to customize LLMs for clinical use cases. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The same proof-of-concept contribution is preserved; the publication expresses higher certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: LiVersa will likely require further refinement before potential live deployment. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces an explicit limitation about need for further refinement that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed NLP models can automatically detect Fontan patients based on clinical notes with higher accuracy than ICD codes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Natural language processing models can automatically detect Fontan patients based on clinical notes with higher accuracy than ICD codes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same core conclusion about NLP models outperforming ICD codes with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SVM achieved the best performance among the models tested, with an F1 score of 0.95 for the positive (Fontan) class. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Support vector machines achieved the best performance in identifying Fontan cases compared to other models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the specific result that SVM was the top-performing model; the numeric F1 detail is consistent with the same claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both NLP models outperformed ICD code based classification in terms of accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both natural language processing models outperformed ICD code-based classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Direct restatement of the same result with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The novel sliding window strategy improved performance over the base RoBERTa model but did not outperform SVM. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The sliding window strategy improved performance over the base model but did not outperform support vector machines. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the sliding window finding with the same comparative outcome and similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ICD code based classification tended to have more false positives compared to both NLP models. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not include the specific observation about ICD false positives, so this preprint claim does not appear in the final set of claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mini-IFA demonstrates high sensitivity and specificity for detecting antibodies against SARS-CoV-2. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results provide a proof of concept for fast and accurate measurement of antibody responses in a research setup. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the analytical accuracy as a proof-of-concept for fast and accurate measurement, which clarifies the preprint's sensitivity/specificity claim in broader terms.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The assay is most suitable for detecting IgG and IgA antibodies in patient samples. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The miniaturized immunofluorescence assay (mini-IFA) enables simultaneous measurement of IgM, IgA, and IgG responses against different viral antigens. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports simultaneous measurement of IgM in addition to IgA/IgG, which revises the preprint's narrower suitability statement and broadens the assay's claimed capability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: N and S proteins are identified as the ideal antigens for distinguishing between vaccinated and infected individuals. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This method allows differentiation between vaccine-induced and infection-induced antibody responses using SARS-CoV-2 as the model pathogen. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the capability to distinguish vaccine vs infection responses using SARS-CoV-2 as a model, which clarifies the preprint's antigen-specific conclusion without naming N/S explicitly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mini-IFA allows for simultaneous detection of multiple immunoglobulin classes, facilitating differentiation between recent and past infections. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The miniaturized immunofluorescence assay (mini-IFA) enables simultaneous measurement of IgM, IgA, and IgG responses against different viral antigens. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication confirms simultaneous measurement of multiple immunoglobulin classes with higher stated certainty, amplifying the preprint's medium-certainty claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The assay can be adapted for detecting antibodies against practically any pathogen without requiring a high biosafety level environment. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The assay can be adapted quickly to emerging pathogens due to its reliance on antigens expressed through transfection. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly states adaptability to emerging pathogens via transfection, which is consistent with the preprint's adaptation claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LightGBM demonstrated the best performance among the machine learning models evaluated for predicting Atrial Fibrillation risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best performing model for AF prediction was LightGBM, with an area-under-the-roc-curve (AUROC) of 0.729. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides the same conclusion and adds the AUROC value, clarifying the model's performance quantitatively.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The genetic risk score (GRS) of Atrial Fibrillation and age at recruitment were identified as top important features contributing to AF risk prediction using SHAP analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The provided publication claims do not mention SHAP-identified top features (GRS and age), so this specific feature-identification claim appears absent from the final listed claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a statistically significant difference in the AUROCs between penalised Logistic Regression and the other machine learning models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim corresponds to a significant AUROC difference between penalized logistic regression and other ML models, so this preprint-specific statistical claim is not reflected.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: XGBoost showed evidence of a statistically significant difference in AUROC compared to the CHA2DS2-VASc clinical tool for ischemic stroke prediction in AF patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The improved AUROC in the XGBoost model compared to CHA<sub>2</sub>DS<sub>2</sub>-VASc was statistically significant based on DeLong's test (p-value = 2.20E-06). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication directly corroborates the preprint's claim, providing the statistical test and p-value for the significant AUROC improvement.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The best performing model for ischemic stroke prediction in AF patients was XGBoost with AUROC of 0.631. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the specific AUROC value for XGBoost as the top stroke-prediction model, a numerical detail not present in the preprint claims list.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Several peripheral blood biomarkers were associated with ischemic stroke, which are not considered by CHA<sub>2</sub>DS<sub>2</sub>-VASc. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes a new conclusion about peripheral blood biomarkers associated with ischemic stroke that was not in the preprint's claims list.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The best performing ML models presented have the potential for clinical use, but further validation in independent studies is required. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a forward-looking conclusion regarding clinical potential and the need for external validation not explicitly stated in the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A deep learning based AI system can accurately segment the infected lung regions by COVID-19 using thick-section CT scans. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A deep learning–based AI system can accurately quantify the COVID-19-associated lung abnormalities and assess the disease severity and its progressions. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes a closely aligned conclusion about accurate quantification and assessment of disease progression, clarifying the segmentation claim into quantification and assessment outcomes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The computed imaging bio-markers can distinguish between the non-severe and severe COVID-19 stages with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.968. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The computed imaging biomarkers were able to distinguish between the non-severe and severe COVID-19 stages with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.97. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same result with nearly identical AUC (rounded), so the claim is effectively unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The infection volume changes computed by the AI system can assess the COVID-19 progression with a Cohen’s kappa of 0.8220. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Very good agreement (κ = 0.8220) between the AI system and the radiologists was achieved on evaluating the changes in infection volumes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides the same kappa value and agreement interpretation, directly supporting the preprint's claim about progression assessment.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of the AI system's lung abnormality segmentation is close to the inter-observer agreement with experienced radiologists. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The dice coefficient between the segmentation of the AI system and two experienced radiologists for the COVID-19-infected lung abnormalities was 0.74 ± 0.28 and 0.76 ± 0.29, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication supplies numeric dice coefficients demonstrating AI-radiologist agreement, which clarifies and strengthens the preprint's medium-certainty observation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The two-phase ML-based model accurately classified COVID-19 and pneumonia patients using CT radiomics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The two-phase ML-based model accurately classified COVID-19 and pneumonia patients using CT radiomics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication claim are virtually identical in wording and meaning, reporting the same result for the diagnostic model.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The severity prediction task using Random Forest achieved an accuracy of 0.934±0.024. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The severity prediction task using Random Forest achieved a precision of 0.868±0.123 and an AUC of 0.969±0.022. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both refer to the same severity-prediction task but the publication reports different performance metrics (precision and AUC) rather than the preprint's accuracy, altering the reported performance picture.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Nine pertinent features were obtained after feature selection for both diagnostic and severity prediction tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Nine pertinent features were obtained after feature selection for both phases of the model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with slightly different wording ('both phases' vs 'diagnostic and severity tasks'), clarifying structure without changing meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This 2-steps model showed great potential in assessing COVID-19 CT images towards improved management of patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This 2-steps model showed great potential in assessing COVID-19 CT images towards improved management of patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is reproduced verbatim in the publication with the same level of certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The COVID-19 pandemic has had a major impact on global mental health. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The COVID-19 pandemic has a major negative impact on global mental health. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies the direction of the impact as negative but otherwise retains the same high-level claim about major global mental health effects.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Developments in COVID-19 pandemic-related psychopathology can be meaningfully monitored using machine learning methods. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results demonstrate the potential of applying machine learning to data from electronic health records. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both claims concern applying ML to clinical data for monitoring/analysis; the publication frames it as demonstrated potential in EHRs, which clarifies the data source and framing of the contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: COVID-19 pandemic-related psychopathology among patients with mental illness covaries with pandemic pressure. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pandemic-related psychopathology covaries with the pandemic pressure over time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the covariation finding with higher certainty and frames it generally over time (not limited to the specified subgroup), thus broadening the scope and increasing confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The impact of COVID-19 pandemic-related psychopathology was less pronounced during the second wave compared to the first wave. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report or discuss a comparison of first and second wave impacts, so this specific temporal comparison is omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The reduced impact during the second wave may be due to habituation. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The speculative explanation (habituation) for reduced impact in the second wave is not present in the publication and has been dropped.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A natural-language-processing framework for automated information extraction and relevance scoring was developed to support event-based surveillance. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A natural language processing framework for automated information extraction and relevance scoring was developed to support event-based surveillance. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The development of the NLP framework is reported identically in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The multilayer perceptron algorithm achieved a precision of 0.19, recall of 0.50, specificity of 0.89, F1 of 0.28, and the highest balanced accuracy index of 0.46. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best classifier, a logistic regression, achieved a sensitivity of 0.82 and an index balanced accuracy of 0.61. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication changes which algorithm is reported as the best and reports substantially different performance metrics, indicating a revision of the results and overall stronger reported performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The support-vector machine algorithm demonstrated the highest recall of 0.88, which is particularly valuable for epidemiologists. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the SVM-highest-recall claim, instead highlighting a different best classifier, so the specific SVM claim is omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The overall framework is functional and can be used in production, indicating potential improvements in event-based surveillance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The overall framework works well and can be used in production, promising improvements in event-based surveillance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions assert the system is production-ready and promising for surveillance; wording is slightly different but the conclusion is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The multiclass classifier developed for cervical images demonstrates strong repeatability and classification performance on external data without retraining. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Classification performance of our model is strong on images from a new geography without retraining. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes strong external classification performance on new geographies without retraining; the preprint also mentioned repeatability, so the publication narrows wording to the geographic external-performance aspect.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Device-level heterogeneity affects model performance more significantly than geography-level heterogeneity. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Device-level heterogeneity affects our model performance more than geography-level heterogeneity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The same effect is reported in the publication but with higher stated certainty, so the claim is preserved and its confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Optimized retraining approaches are necessary to address data heterogeneity and facilitate effective use of AI models in new settings. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our work supports the need for optimized retraining approaches that address data heterogeneity to facilitate effective use of AI models in new settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the recommendation for optimized retraining but expresses it with slightly lower certainty, so the conclusion is preserved but tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI pipeline developed in this work is one of the first efforts to generate and externally validate a reliable and clinically translatable cervical cancer diagnostic classifier. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make the novelty/"one of the first" or "clinically translatable" claim explicitly; that contribution framing is omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Poisson Binomial based Comorbidity discovery (PBC) method effectively analyzes Electronic Health Records (EHRs) to identify comorbid diagnoses, procedures, and medications. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Poisson Binomial based Comorbidity discovery (PBC) method effectively analyzes Electronic Health Records (EHRs) to identify comorbid diagnoses, procedures, and medications. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The method and its effectiveness are stated identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (AI) methodologies reveal the conditionally-dependent impacts of comorbid conditions and demography on cardiovascular health outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (AI) methodologies reveal the conditionally-dependent impacts of comorbid conditions and demography on cardiovascular health outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The multimorbidity networks generated from the analysis enable extensive exploration of the comorbid and demographic factors influencing cardiovascular outcomes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The multimorbidity networks created from the analysis enable extensive exploration of the comorbid and demographic factors influencing cardiovascular outcomes. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The core claim about multimorbidity networks enabling exploration of factors is preserved; wording and role (conclusion vs contribution) differ slightly but content is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The transformation of large EHR collections into compact, portable tools addresses legal, technological, and data-scientific challenges in large-scale EHR analysis. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The transformation of large EHR collections into compact, portable tools addresses legal, technological, and data-scientific challenges in large-scale EHR analyses. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the claim but presents it with stronger certainty (high) and frames it more conclusively, amplifying confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI-based Signal-to-Knowledge diagnostic scheme for lung nodule classification can diagnose diseases directly from CT rawdata without reconstructing images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI-based signal-to-knowledge diagnostic scheme for lung nodule classification can diagnose diseases directly from the computed tomography (CT) raw data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result about diagnosing directly from CT raw data with essentially the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of the rawdata achieved almost comparable results to traditional CT imaging. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The raw data achieves almost comparable performance with CT, indicating that it is possible to diagnose diseases without reconstructing images. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication rephrases the claim and frames it more as an indication/possibility and reduces stated certainty, clarifying implications for diagnosis without reconstruction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The introduction of rawdata significantly enhances the performance of CT by providing diagnostic information that CT does not have. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Incorporating raw data through three common convolutional network structures greatly improves the performance of the CT models in all cohorts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides methodological detail (three network structures) and cohort-wide improvements with quantified gains, clarifying and strengthening the original performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study breaks new ground in the potential for direct Signal-to-Knowledge domain analysis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results demonstrate the potential for direct signal-to-knowledge domain analysis. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the idea as demonstrated by results (rather than only suggestive), increases certainty, and thereby clarifies and strengthens the original conclusion.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance gain from incorporating raw data ranges from 0.01 to 0.12. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a quantitative statement about the magnitude of performance gains that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The QNN Classifier correctly classified 28 Responders out of 34 and 6 non-Responders out of 23, demonstrating a sensitivity of 0.82 and specificity of 0.26. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The quantum neural network (QNN) classifier correctly classified 28 responders out of 34 and 6 non-responders out of 23. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same classification counts; the core performance result is unchanged though the publication splits metrics across claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The No Information Rate of the QNN Classifier was equal to 0.59. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the No Information Rate value; this specific metric present in the preprint is absent in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Preliminary clinical and technical results indicate that quantum machine learning applied to data-driven clinical decisions is a promising technology. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Preliminary results indicate that quantum machine learning applied to data-driven clinical decisions for personalized treatment of advanced knee osteoarthritis is a promising technology. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the preliminary-technology judgement but specifies the clinical application (personalized treatment of knee osteoarthritis), narrowing the original general claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Further research validation with larger, real-world unstructured datasets is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of the QNN Classifier. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Further research validation with larger, real-world unstructured datasets is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of the QNN model. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same limitation and call for further validation with essentially identical wording.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The sensitivity of the QNN classifier was 0.82 and the specificity was 0.26. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the sensitivity/specificity as a separate claim; the preprint contained these metrics but in a combined statement, so the publication's separate claim is represented here as an added item.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The usage of additional and different motif repeats proves effective in predicting P. falciparum infection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The usage of additional and different motif repeats in machine learning methods proves effective in characterizing PfHRP2 diversity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the effectiveness of additional repeats but shifts the stated outcome from predicting infection to characterizing PfHRP2 diversity, altering the claim's target.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The efficacy of the Baker et al., 2005 model has waned over recent years due to genetic mutations in the parasite. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not reproduce the preprint's specific claim about the decline in efficacy of the Baker et al. (2005) model, so this claim is omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning model explainability methods highlight which of the repeat types are most important for diagnosis. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning model explainability methods highlight which repeat types are most important for rapid diagnostic test sensitivity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the practical target (rapid diagnostic test sensitivity) rather than a general diagnostic importance and reports it with higher certainty, clarifying and strengthening the contribution.

alignments.pub_claim_text: This work showcases a novel methodology for identifying potential targets for future versions of rapid diagnostic tests. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a methodological-contribution statement about identifying targets for future RDTs that was not explicit in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study identifies a powerful combination of five features that help predict mortality in COVID-19 patients with 96% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A powerful combination of five features: neutrophils, lymphocytes, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP), and age helps to predict mortality with 96% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication gives the exact same five-feature set and accuracy, preserving the result unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning algorithms have been compared to achieve consistent high accuracy across the days that span the disease. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Robust testing with three cases based on days to outcome confirms the strong predictive performance and practicality of the proposed model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports concrete robustness testing across day-based cases, converting the preprint's comparative claim into a more specific, stronger validation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model predicts with an accuracy of 90% as early as 16 days before the outcome. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best performing method using XGBoost feature importance and neural network classification predicts with an accuracy of 90% as early as 16 days before the outcome. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same early-prediction accuracy and timing, adding methodological detail but not changing the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study would help accelerate the decision making process in healthcare systems for focused medical treatments early and accurately. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study provides solutions that would help accelerate the decision-making process in healthcare systems for focused medical treatments in an accurate, early, and reliable manner. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution with slightly expanded wording (accuracy, early, reliable), clarifying the expected benefits.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Ensemble-based models perform better than other model types at predicting both 5-day ICU admission and 28-day mortality from COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Ensemble-based models perform better than other model types at predicting both 5-day ICU admission and 28-day mortality from COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the ensemble-performance result verbatim; the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CRP, LDH, and procalcitonin levels were important for ICU admission models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CRP, LDH, and O2 saturation were important for ICU admission models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a different set of top features for ICU admission (O2 saturation replacing procalcitonin), indicating a revision in which variables are most important.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: eGFR <60 ml/min/1.73m2, ventilator use, and potassium levels were the most important variables for predicting mortality. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: eGFR <60 ml/min/1.73 m2, and neutrophil and lymphocyte percentages were the most important variables for predicting mortality. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication lists a different set of top mortality predictors (neutrophil/lymphocyte percentages instead of ventilator use and potassium), representing a substantive revision of which features are seen as most important.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Implementing such models would help in clinical decision-making for future COVID-19 and other infectious disease outbreaks. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Implementing such models could help in clinical decision-making for future infectious disease outbreaks including COVID-19. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same conclusion about the potential clinical utility of these models for COVID-19 and other infectious disease outbreaks.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models for diagnosing COVID-19 may not be reliable and degrade in performance over time due to domain shifts.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Domain shifts strongly affect machine learning models for COVID-19 diagnosis.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint's point in stronger, more direct language about domain shifts affecting COVID-19 diagnostic models, keeping the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed methods can identify domain shifts and reverse their negative effects on model performance.
alignments.rationale: The published paper does not report a claim that their methods both identify domain shifts and reverse their effects, so this specific contribution is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Frequent re-training and reassessment, along with stronger weighting of more recent samples, helps maintain model performance and credibility over time.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Frequent re-training and re-assessment are indispensable for robust models enabling clinical utility.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains and strengthens the retraining/reassessment recommendation but omits the specific suggestion about stronger weighting of recent samples, narrowing the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The diagnosis models constructed and tested on large-scale data sets adapt to observed domain shifts and maintain high ROC AUC values.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not claim that their diagnosis models both adapt to domain shifts and maintain high ROC AUCs as stated; that specific result is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our machine learning model predicts in-hospital mortality with an AUC of 0.918.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our machine learning model predicts in-hospital mortality with an AUC of 0.918.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this numeric performance claim verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model predicts transfer to ICU with an AUC of 0.821.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model predicts transfer to ICU with an AUC of 0.821.
alignments.rationale: Identical performance claim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The need for mechanical ventilation is predicted by the model with an AUC of 0.654.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The need for mechanical ventilation is predicted by the model with an AUC of 0.654.
alignments.rationale: Identical numeric result reported.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models based on dichotomous features perform nearly as well as those based on numerical features.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models based on dichotomous features perform nearly as well as those based on numerical features.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about feature types is carried over unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Numerical values of CRP and blood sugar, along with dichotomous indicators for increased PTT and GOT, are among the best predictors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: CRP and blood sugar levels, along with dichotomous indicators for increased PTT and GOT, are among the best predictors of in-hospital outcomes.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same set of predictors but presents the claim with slightly lower stated certainty (medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed multimodal machine learning-based objective pain assessment algorithm recognizes different pain levels with an average balanced accuracy of over 80%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The multimodal pain recognition models achieved an average balanced accuracy of over 80% across the different pain levels.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same performance metric and claim for the multimodal model.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study represents the first multimodal pain recognition work conducted on postoperative patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study presents a novel, multimodal machine learning framework for objective pain recognition in postoperative patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the contribution as a novel multimodal framework for postoperative pain recognition, clarifying the claim of first/novel work without changing scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method addresses challenges such as imbalanced distribution of pain classes and scarcity of ground-truth labels.
alignments.rationale: The published paper does not explicitly claim it addresses those specific dataset and labeling challenges, so this contribution is not present as stated.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Conformal prediction mitigates the risks of singleton predictions by estimating the uncertainty of a predictive model.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Conformal prediction offers a versatile framework for quantifying the uncertainty of predictive models in healthcare.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core claim that conformal prediction quantifies model uncertainty and mitigates risky singleton predictions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates the impact of a binary transductive model and a regression-based inductive model in predicting drug response.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The impact of a binary transductive model and a regression-based inductive model in predicting drug response was demonstrated.
alignments.rationale: The experimental demonstration reported in the preprint appears in the publication without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A multi-class inductive predictor effectively addresses distribution shifts in molecular subtyping.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A multi-class inductive predictor was shown to effectively address distribution shifts in molecular subtyping.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the result that the multi-class inductive predictor handles distribution shifts.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Conformal prediction has the potential to overcome the safety limitations of current methods in genomic medicine applications.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Conformal prediction has the potential to overcome the safety limitations of current methods in genomic medicine applications.
alignments.rationale: The concluding statement about potential to improve safety in genomic medicine is retained verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: IDaRS yields high prediction accuracy for the three main CRC genetic pathways and key mutations by deep learning based analysis of the WSIs of H&E stained slides.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The iterative draw and rank sampling method yielded mean AUROCs for the prediction of hypermutation, microsatellite instability, chromosomal instability, BRAF mutation, and TP53 mutation that were higher than those from previously published methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication quantifies performance and compares to prior work, clarifying and contextualizing the preprint's broad accuracy claim for multiple pathways and mutations.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: IDaRS achieves state-of-the-art AUROC values of 0.90, 0.83, and 0.81 for prediction of the status of MSI, CIN, and HM tumors for the TCGA cohort.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The iterative draw and rank sampling method yielded mean AUROCs for the prediction of hypermutation, microsatellite instability, chromosomal instability, BRAF mutation, and TP53 mutation that were higher than those from previously published methods.
alignments.rationale: The published paper reports improved mean AUROCs and additional mutation targets but does not present the exact numerical AUROCs as stated in the preprint, so the specific numeric claim is revised/represented differently.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A relatively high proportion of tumor infiltrating lymphocytes and necrosis are found to be strongly associated with HM and MSI.
alignments.pub_claim_text: High proportions of tumour-infiltrating lymphocytes and necrotic tumour cells were found to be associated with microsatellite instability.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the association with MSI and describes the same histologic correlates; wording is slightly adjusted but meaning is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Automated prediction of genetic pathways and key mutations from image analysis of simple H&E stained sections can provide time and cost-effective decision support.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed algorithm could be used to stratify patients for targeted therapies with potentially lower costs and quicker turnaround times than current methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the preprint's decision-support/cost-saving claim in terms of patient stratification for targeted therapies with reduced cost and turnaround time, a clarified application.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed mixture of experts (MoE) scheme for detecting artifacts outperformed simpler multiclass models in terms of performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed mixture of experts (MoE) scheme for detecting artifacts outperformed simpler multiclass models in terms of performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Text is effectively identical between preprint and publication; claim content, scope and certainty are the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The MoE using MobileNet DCNNs achieved an F1 score of 86.15% and a sensitivity score of 97.93% on unseen data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The MoE using MobileNet DCNNs achieved an F1 score of 86.15% and a sensitivity score of 97.93% on unseen data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Numerical results and wording are identical in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pathologists rated the predictive performance of the MoE scheme with a Cohen kappa of 0.82, indicating substantial agreement for diagnostic usability. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Field experts rated the predictive performance of the MoE scheme with a Cohen Kappa of 0.82, indicating substantial agreement for its diagnostic usability. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Core metric (Cohen kappa 0.82) is retained but wording broadens 'pathologists' to 'field experts' and reported certainty is reduced, so the claim is clarified and confidence tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a trade-off between performance and computational complexity in the proposed artifact detection pipeline. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a trade-off between performance and computational complexity in deep learning models for artifact detection. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is preserved but wording is generalized from the specific proposed pipeline to deep learning models broadly; certainty remains the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients have a set of core information needs related to AI in healthcare that are essential for calibrating trust. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients have a set of core information needs regarding AI tools, oversight, and healthcare experience that are relevant to calibrating trust. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the same claim but explicitly names oversight and healthcare experience, clarifying the content without changing scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Specific information factors pertaining to the AI model, oversight, and healthcare experience are relevant to patients' trust in AI. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients have a set of core information needs regarding AI tools, oversight, and healthcare experience that are relevant to calibrating trust. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This preprint result is encompassed by the publication claim which explicitly lists the same factors; wording is slightly rephrased but meaning is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Identifying patient information needs is a critical starting point for designing strategies for information delivery in healthcare AI systems. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Identifying patient information needs is a critical starting point for calibrating trust in healthcare AI systems. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The core action (identifying needs) is retained but the stated primary purpose shifts from designing information delivery strategies to calibrating trust, so the claim is revised in emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patient-centered engagement is important for promoting transparency in healthcare AI. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings highlight the importance of patient-centered engagement when developing AI model documentation and communicating information about these technologies. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Publication preserves the importance of patient-centered engagement but specifies contexts (documentation and communication), clarifying the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLMs can prioritize disease-associated genes as well, or better than, dedicated bioinformatics methods relying on calculated phenotype similarity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs can prioritize disease-associated genes as well, or better than, dedicated bioinformatics methods relying on pre-defined phenotype similarity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core comparative result is preserved; wording change from 'calculated' to 'pre-defined' does not alter the substantive claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLMs use only natural language information as background knowledge and do not require ontology-based phenotyping or structured genotype-to-phenotype knowledge. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs may offer an alternative to traditional bioinformatics methods to prioritize disease-associated genes based on disease phenotypes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's strong claim that structured resources are not required is softened in the publication to a more cautious suggestion that LLMs may offer an alternative; this is a revision with reduced confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLMs can be used to provide diagnostic support that helps in identifying plausible candidate genes for undiagnosed patients with rare diseases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs can be used to provide diagnostic support that helps in identifying plausible candidate genes for undiagnosed patients with rare diseases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The diagnostic-support result is stated identically in both versions.

alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs may potentially enhance diagnostic accuracy and simplify the process for rare genetic diseases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This broader, optimistic conclusion about enhancing diagnostic accuracy/simplification is presented in the publication but does not have a direct, explicit counterpart in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The top machine learning algorithm, XGBoost, achieved an average area under the receiver operator curve of 0.736 for predicting cancer diagnoses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific XGBoost result (AUC 0.736) does not appear in the publication; the publication emphasizes different summary comparisons, so this specific preprint result was omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Linear models trained with only 10 features were found to be statistically indifferent from the machine learning performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Linear models with only 10 features generated an average AUC that was comparable to the complex models using all features and greater than the average AUC of the QCancer models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the comparability claim but adds context (comparison to QCancer), slightly rephrasing the original statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The linear models were significantly more accurate than the prominent QCancer models, with a p-value of 0.0019. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Linear models with only 10 features generated an average AUC that was comparable to the complex models using all features and greater than the average AUC of the QCancer models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the finding of superiority over QCancer but omits the specific p-value and phrases the result in terms of average AUC, so the claim is presented in a clarified form.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The consideration of often omitted feature types may have contributed to the increase in accuracy of the linear models. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The high performance of the 10-feature linear model may be caused by the consideration of often omitted feature types, including census records and genetic information. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same causal hypothesis but adds examples of omitted feature types, clarifying the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach led to the discovery of significant novel risk features relevant to multiple cancers. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the claimed discovery of novel risk features; instead it focuses on model performance and implications, so this specific contribution was dropped.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The average area under the receiver operator curve (AUC) of the linear models was greater than the average AUC of the ML models when all features were used. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces a summary comparison favoring linear models over ML models when all features are used; this specific comparative claim is not present in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The high performance of the 10-feature linear models indicates that unbiased selection of diverse features may lead to impressively accurate predictions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This general conclusion about unbiased, diverse feature selection is articulated in the publication but is not explicitly stated in the preprint as a standalone claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings suggest that personalized screening schedules could increase cancer survival. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This downstream implication on screening and survival appears as a new, speculative conclusion in the publication and was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning approaches for feature selection can identify key features of Alzheimer-related pathologies associated with dementia. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning approaches can effectively identify critical features of Alzheimer-related pathologies associated with dementia. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution/ conclusion with equivalent meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Seven feature ranking methods consistently ranked 22 out of 34 neuropathology features as important for dementia classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Seven feature ranking methods consistently identified 22 out of 34 neuropathology features as important for dementia classification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The quantitative result is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best performing dementia classifier achieved 79% sensitivity, 69% specificity, and 75% precision using the top eight ranked neuropathology features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best-performing dementia classifier achieved 79% sensitivity, 69% specificity, and 75% precision using the top eight neuropathological features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Performance metrics and feature count are identical; claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A substantial proportion (40.4%) of dementia cases was consistently misclassified by all seven algorithms and any combination of the 22 ranked features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A substantial proportion (40.4%) of dementia cases was consistently misclassified when assessing all seven classifiers and the 22 ranked features. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric misclassification rate and phenomenon are preserved, but reported certainty is reduced in the publication, so content is unchanged while confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ChatGPT outperformed Google Gemini with a 70% score on the VKT test, while Gemini scored 60%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ChatGPT outperformed Google Gemini with a 70% score on the VKT test, while Gemini scored 60%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication claim are verbatim and convey the same result and certainty.
