alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both ChatGPT and Google Gemini excelled in Clinical Knowledge with a perfect score of 100%, but struggled in Clinical Reasoning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both ChatGPT and Google Gemini excelled in Clinical Knowledge scoring 100% but struggled in Clinical Reasoning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Content and certainty match closely between preprint and publication with only minor wording differences.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Caution is advised when using ChatGPT and Google Gemini due to their limited accuracy in clinical reasoning. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Caution is advised when using ChatGPT and Google Gemini due to their limited accuracy in clinical reasoning. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The recommendation and its justification are present verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Healthcare professionals should carefully formulate questions and be aware of the potential influence of the online prevalence of information on ChatGPT’s and Google Gemini’s responses. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This specific guidance about question formulation and online prevalence is not present in the publication's claim set.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study highlights the potential utility of large language models like ChatGPT in supplementing clinical knowledge for physical therapists. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include this targeted statement about utility for physical therapists; it is not represented among the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLMs demonstrated high accuracy in matching brand and generic names (GPT-4o: 97.38% for brand, 94.71% for generic, p < 0.0001). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs demonstrated high accuracy in matching brand and generic names, with GPT-4o achieving 97.38% for brand and 94.71% for generic names. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numerical results and conclusion are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLMs showed significant inconsistencies in word association tasks. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sentiment analysis revealed significant differences in responses between brand and generic names, particularly in GPT-3.5-turbo-0125. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a more specific characterization (sentiment analysis and model-specific differences) of the preprint's general finding about inconsistencies, so the claim is clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPT-3.5-turbo-0125 exhibited biases favoring brand names for effectiveness (OR 1.43, p < 0.05) and being side-effect-free (OR 1.76, p < 0.05). (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-3.5-turbo-0125 exhibited biases favoring brand names for effectiveness and being side-effect-free. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The bias findings are preserved in the publication with the same direction and certainty (numerical ORs omitted but meaning retained).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DDI detection accuracy was poor across all models (<26%), with no significant differences between brand and generic names. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DDI detection accuracy was poor across all models, with less than 26% accuracy and no significant differences between brand and generic names. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The DDI detection accuracy claim is repeated verbatim with matching certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Despite high proficiency in name-matching, LLMs exhibit inconsistencies when processing brand versus generic drug names in more complex tasks. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs exhibit inconsistencies when processing brand versus generic drug names in more complex tasks. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The overall conclusion about inconsistencies in complex tasks is preserved between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Medical datasets are special in that in general each image adds less diversity than in non-medical datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Medical datasets are special in that in general each image adds less diversity than in nonmedical datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is effectively identical in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ENRICH achieves nearly maximal performance on classification and segmentation tasks on several medical image datasets using only a fraction of the available images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ENRICH achieves nearly maximal performance on classification and segmentation tasks on several medical image datasets using only a fraction of the available images and without up-front data labeling. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the detail that this performance is achieved without up-front labeling, clarifying but not changing the original performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ENRICH outperforms random image selection, the negative control. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ENRICH outperforms random image selection, the negative control. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The negative-control comparison is the same in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ENRICH can also be used to identify errors and outliers in imaging datasets. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ENRICH can also be used to identify errors and outliers in imaging datasets. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The utility for error/outlier identification is preserved without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ENRICH is a simple, computationally efficient method for prioritizing images for expert labeling and use in DL. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ENRICH is a simple, computationally efficient method for prioritizing images for expert labeling and use in DL. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The methodological claim and its characterization remain the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Combining text and structured data improved model performance, compared to using only text or only structured data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Combining text and structured data improved model performance, compared with using only text or only structured data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is directly reflected in the publication with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of unstructured clinical text data in the development of prognostic prediction models has been found beneficial in addition to structured data in most studies. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The use of unstructured text in the development of prognostic prediction models has been found beneficial in addition to structured data in most studies. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The high-level conclusion about unstructured text benefits is maintained between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: External validation, public availability, and explainability of the developed models was limited. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: External validation, public availability, and attention for the explainability of the developed models were limited. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation is present in both versions; the publication slightly rephrases but keeps the same meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EHR text data is a source of valuable information for prediction model development and should not be neglected. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Text data are a source of valuable information for prediction model development and should not be neglected. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same core claim but reports reduced certainty (high to medium), so the claim is clarified and confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future focus on explainability and external validation of the developed models is suggested to promote robust and trustworthy prediction models in clinical practice. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The explicit forward-looking recommendation for future focus is not represented among the publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A total of 18 distinct algorithms were identified, demonstrating significant variations in methods, features, datasets, and benchmarks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A total of 18 distinct algorithms were identified, demonstrating significant variations in methods, features, datasets, and benchmarks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The identification and characterization of the 18 algorithms is identical between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Due to dataset-specific optimization, it is challenging to draw broad conclusions about comparative performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Due to dataset-specific optimization, it is challenging to draw broad conclusions about comparative performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The limitation regarding dataset-specific optimization is preserved without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Large variations exist in the used datasets and benchmarks, further hindering direct algorithm comparison. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Large variations exist in the used datasets and benchmarks, further hindering direct algorithm comparison. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim about variation in datasets and benchmarks is present and unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings emphasize the need for standardization and comparative studies for advancing QC in MR imaging. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings emphasize the need for standardization and comparative studies for advancing QC in MR imaging. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The call for standardization and comparative studies is retained verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Efforts should focus on identifying a dataset-independent measure as well as algorithm-independent methods for assessing the relative performance of different approaches. (certainty=low, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The specific recommendation to focus on dataset- and algorithm-independent measures is not present in the publication claims; instead the publication adds an algorithm categorization not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our deep learning models predict opioid prescription with an F1-score of 0.88 ± 0.003 and an AUC-ROC of 0.93 ± 0.002. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our deep learning models predict opioid prescription with an F1-score of 0.88 and an AUC-ROC of 0.93. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric performance metrics are the same between preprint and publication (publication omits the ± notation) and the claim content is effectively identical.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We constructed a model to predict OUD diagnosis achieving an F1-score of 0.82 ± 0.05 and AUC-ROC of 0.94 ± 0.008. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We constructed a model to predict OUD diagnosis achieving an F1-score of 0.82 and AUC-ROC of 0.94. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported performance metrics match between versions (publication omits uncertainty bounds), so the claim is unchanged in substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model for OUD prediction outperformed prior algorithms for specificity, F1 score and AUC-ROC while achieving equivalent sensitivity. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model for OUD prediction outperformed prior algorithms for specificity, F1 score, and AUC-ROC while achieving equivalent sensitivity. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The comparative performance claim is word-for-word preserved in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This represents an important contribution of our work as opioid prescriptions are more common than OUDs. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not repeat this rationale about prevalence-driven importance and instead presents a different contribution (novelty of prescription prediction), so the original contribution statement was removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Algorithms such as those described in this paper will become increasingly important to understand the drivers underlying this national epidemic. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The broad prognostic/importance statement about future relevance is not present in the publication's claims, so it was removed.

alignments.pub_claim_text: No prediction models for opioid prescription as an outcome were found in the literature, making our model the first to predict opioid prescribing behavior. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds an explicit novelty claim (first model for opioid prescription) that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The shallow custom network resulted in the highest classification performance when trained on T2-w or ADC MR images fused with age information. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The highest tumor-type classification performance was achieved using a vision transformer model pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned on ADC images with age fusion. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims report which model achieved highest performance with age fusion, but the architecture changed from a shallow custom network to a vision transformer in the publication, so the preprint claim was revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classification performance for infratentorial tumors was measured with a Matthew’s correlation coefficient (MCC) of 0.71 for ADC and 0.64 for T2-w. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both model architectures performed similarly across the experiments, with no differences between the pre-training strategies. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The specific MCC values reported in the preprint are not present in the publication, which instead emphasizes no differences between architectures; this revises and tempers the original performance-specific claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classification performance for both infra- and supratentorial tumors was measured with a Matthew’s correlation coefficient (MCC) of 0.70 for ADC and 0.57 for T2-w. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both model architectures performed similarly across the experiments, with no differences between the pre-training strategies. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication drops the specific MCC figures and instead reports similar performance across architectures/pretraining, which revises and narrows the original quantitative claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Classification of pediatric brain tumors on MR images could be accomplished using deep learning. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both model architectures performed similarly across the experiments, with no differences between the pre-training strategies. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The general conclusion that deep learning can classify pediatric brain tumors is retained but the publication provides stronger, more specific conclusions about model behavior and consistency, effectively clarifying and strengthening the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The fusion of age information improved model performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Age fusion marginally improved the model's performance in classifying pediatric brain tumors. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions report that age fusion helps, but the publication qualifies the effect as marginal, which clarifies and slightly tempers the original statement.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Grad-CAMs indicated that the models' attention focused on the brain region during classification. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes Grad-CAM interpretability results that were not present in the preprint, so this is an added claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: PCA of the feature space showed greater separation of the tumor-type clusters when using contrastive pre-training. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The PCA/contrastive pre-training analysis is new to the publication and was not in the preprint, making it an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates the use of physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) to identify multiple time-dependent parameters in epidemiological models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study demonstrates the effectiveness of physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) in identifying time-dependent parameters and data-driven fractional differential operators in epidemiological models. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The research reports results for the spread of COVID-19 in various locations, including New York City, Rhode Island, Michigan, and Italy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The research reports results for the spread of COVID-19 in New York City, Rhode Island, Michigan, and Italy by inferring unknown parameters and unobserved dynamics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions report the same set of geographic applications and inference goals; the publication adds phrasing about inferring unobserved dynamics but does not change the core claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study indicates that different time-dependent derivative orders can be determined for each compartment in fractional differential models. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study indicates that different time-dependent derivative orders for each compartment can be determined using fractional differential models represented by neural networks. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about determining compartment-specific derivative orders in fractional models is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The research investigates the identifiability of unknown functions for different datasets and quantifies the uncertainty associated with neural networks and control measures. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The investigation quantifies the uncertainty associated with neural networks and control measures in forecasting the pandemic. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We show that we can quickly and accurately estimate ICH volume with high agreement with time-consuming manual segmentation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We show that we can quickly and accurately estimate ICH volume with high agreement with time-consuming manual segmentation. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep neural networks trained with an appropriate anatomic context in the network receptive field can effectively perform ICH segmentation. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: 3D networks with appropriate anatomic context outperformed both 2D and random forest models. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: This is the first publicly available neural network model for segmentation of ICH, evaluated with the presence of both IVH and SDH. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The model can be incorporated into the workflow of an ICH clinical trial series to quickly and accurately segment ICH and estimate total hemorrhage volume. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The random forest model demonstrated better performance in coping with feature variance compared to the Lasso regression model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mean cross-validated AUC classifying MG patients as high or low risk for MC showed a predictive accuracy of 68.8% for a regularized Lasso regression and 76.5% for a random forest model. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The literature review assesses the capacity of AI to aid in addressing the observed disparity in healthcare between high- and low-income countries. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The research highlights the challenges of model generalisation to out-of-distribution data. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A Bayesian machine learning neural network model was trained to generate a neuroimaging phenotype and AD-score representing the probability of Alzheimer’s disease. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model demonstrated correlation of the AD-score with cognitive scores in individuals with an AD-score above 0.5. (certainty=high, type=result)

(certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of the model to a healthy population identified a cohort at risk for Alzheimer’s disease with a cognitive profile consistent with the disease. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MCII status can explain a significant proportion of the overall compositional variance in the gut microbiome. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The neural network proved to be highly effective in predicting which patient will achieve MCII. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients who achieved clinical improvement had higher alpha-diversity in their gut microbiomes at both baseline and follow-up visits. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: This case study aims to encourage the research community to explore new machine learning approaches. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
