alignments.pub_claim_text: 46% of the models reviewed used feature selection methods that can lead to data leakage. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific quantified prevalence (46%) is reported in the publication but was not present as a separate claim in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Best practice guidelines that promote the avoidance and recognition of data leakage may help the field avoid biased results. (certainty=low, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds an explicit recommendation for best-practice guidelines that was not in the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients and controls were classified into two separate groups based on the cerebellar transcriptomic biosignature of 25 features, achieving an Area Under Curve of 0.929 and Average Precision of 0.955. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients and controls were classified into two separate groups based on the cerebellar transcriptomic biosignature of 25 features, achieving an Area Under Curve of 0.929 and Average Precision of 0.955. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance metrics and biosignature classification result are stated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using 6 of the characteristic features discovered during the selection process, 99.6% of predictive performance was achieved. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific claim about 6 features retaining 99.6% of performance does not appear in the publication's claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The 3 genes contributing most to the predictive power of the model are also deregulated in temporal lobe epilepsy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the specific claim about the top 3 genes being deregulated in temporal lobe epilepsy.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The cerebellar transcriptomic biosignature suggests a potential genetic overlap with temporal lobe epilepsy and new genetic contributions to the pathogenesis of bipolar disorder. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The biosignature includes both genes previously connected to bipolar disorder and genes not linked before with any psychiatric disease. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the point about previously linked and novel genes (new contributions) but drops the specific claim about overlap with temporal lobe epilepsy, narrowing and tempering the original inference.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Automated machine learning (AutoML) accurately identified 25 genes that can separate bipolar patients from healthy controls with high predictive power. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: While related to the preprint's classification claim, this explicit AutoML framing and emphasis is presented as an additional publication claim not separately stated in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The discovered features lead to new biological insights and focus on biological interpretation by removing irrelevant and redundant markers. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This explicit emphasis on biological interpretation and removal of irrelevant markers is articulated in the publication but not as a distinct preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A model was developed that predicts severe COVID-19 disease with a sensitivity of 57% and a specificity of 90% for patients aged 40-59 years. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A model was developed that predicts severe COVID-19 disease with a sensitivity of 57% and specificity of 90% for patients aged 40-59 years. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The age-stratified performance numbers are reproduced exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model predicts severe disease with a specificity of 50% and a sensitivity of 90% for patients aged 60-79 years. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include the specific performance claim for the 60-79 years age group present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Lactate dehydrogenase level (LDH) was identified as an important factor in predicting the severity of illness across all age groups. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Lactate dehydrogenase level (LDH) was identified as an important factor in predicting the severity of illness across all age groups. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The LDH importance claim is explicitly preserved in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning was used to identify risk factors for severe COVID-19 with high accuracy. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning was used to identify risk factors for severe COVID-19 with high accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication maintains the claim that machine learning identified risk factors with high accuracy; wording and intent are consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A tool is planned to be developed for determining indications for hospitalization for patients undergoing home care. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed model aims to assist in determining the indications for hospitalisation for patients undergoing home care. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The preprint described a planned tool; the publication frames the developed model as aiming to assist with hospitalization decisions, which clarifies and slightly shifts the contribution framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed LASSO model can efficiently use data from multiple major cities and achieve similar short-to-medium term forecasting performance to semi-mechanistic models for E&W epidemics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed LASSO model can efficiently use data from multiple major cities and achieve similar short-to-medium term forecasting performance to semi-mechanistic models for E&W epidemics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claim text is essentially identical in content and certainty to the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The LASSO model captures annual to biennial bifurcation of measles epidemics in E&W caused by susceptible response to the late 1940s baby boom. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The LASSO model captures annual to biennial bifurcation of measles epidemics in E&W caused by susceptible response to the late 1940s baby boom. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result and level of certainty as the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both approaches capture short-term forecasts, but accuracy suffers for both methods as longer-term predictions are attempted in highly irregular, post-vaccination outbreaks in E&W. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both approaches capture short-term forecasts, but accuracy suffers for both methods as longer-term predictions are attempted in highly irregular, post-vaccination outbreaks in E&W. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: Identical limitation statement present in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The LASSO model can both qualitatively and quantitatively reconstruct mechanistic assumptions, notably susceptible dynamics, in the TSIR model. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The LASSO model can both qualitatively and quantitatively reconstruct mechanistic assumptions, notably susceptible dynamics, in the TSIR model. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim is carried forward without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results characterize the limits of predictability of infectious disease dynamics for strongly immunizing pathogens with both mechanistic and machine learning models. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results characterize the limits of predictability of infectious disease dynamics for strongly immunizing pathogens with both mechanistic and machine learning models. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The concluding characterization is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A meta-classifier involving SVM and M3 achieved an accuracy of 94.4% in gender classification for both Dataset-1 and Dataset-2. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A meta-classifier involving SVM and M3 achieved an accuracy of 94.4% in gender classification for both Dataset-1 and Dataset-2. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact match in result and certainty between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Including automatically-classified gender information in the analyses of Dataset-2 revealed gender-specific trends that closely resemble national data. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Including automatically classified gender information in the analyses of Dataset-2 revealed gender-specific trends that closely resemble national survey data. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication clarifies 'national data' as 'national survey data' but conveys the same substantive result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The automated gender detection pipeline developed in this study may aid in cohort-specific social media data analyses. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The automated gender detection pipeline developed in this study may facilitate cohort-specific analyses of social media data. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Wording changed slightly ('aid' -> 'facilitate') but the conclusion and certainty remain the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Wav2vec accurately detected Parkinson's disease based on speech, outperforming feature extraction using mel-frequency cepstral coefficients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Wav2vec accurately detected Parkinson's disease based on speech, outperforming feature extraction using mel-frequency cepstral coefficients in cross-database classification scenarios. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication adds the context of cross-database scenarios, clarifying the evaluation setting while preserving the core performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Wav2vec proved effective in regression, modeling various quantitative speech characteristics related to intelligibility and aging. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Wav2vec proved effective in regression, modeling various quantitative speech characteristics related to articulation and aging. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication changes the descriptor from 'intelligibility' to 'articulation', a substantive shift in which speech attributes were emphasized.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The feature importance experiments discovered shared features across trained models, with increased sharing for related tasks. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study found significant overlaps between classification and regression models, with increased sharing for related tasks. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication frames the finding as overlaps between model types and preserves the core observation about increased sharing for related tasks.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Wav2vec contributes to improved generalizability in speech-based models for Parkinson's disease diagnosis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Wav2vec demonstrated performance comparable to intra-dataset evaluations in cross-database scenarios using Italian and English-read texts. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides specific cross-database evidence supporting the preprint's general claim about improved generalizability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study proposes wav2vec embedding as a promising step toward a speech-based universal model to assist in the evaluation of Parkinson's disease. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Wav2vec embeddings are proposed as a promising step toward a speech-based universal model to assist in the evaluation of Parkinson's disease. (certainty=low, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution statement is retained but the publication reports a lower certainty level, indicating tempered confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates the feasibility of generating virtual stains for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and a multiplex immunofluorescence (mIF) panel from autofluorescence images of unstained non-small cell lung cancer tissue. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study demonstrates the feasibility of generating virtual stains for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and a multiplex immunofluorescence (mIF) immuno-oncology panel from autofluorescence images of unstained non–small cell lung cancer tissue. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the feasibility result with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The virtual stains reproduce key morphologic features and protein biomarker expressions at both tissue and cell levels compared to real stains. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The virtual stains reproduce key morphologic features and protein biomarker expressions at both tissue and cell levels compared with real stains. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about reproduction of morphology and biomarkers is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study extends the generalizability of the deep learning technique for virtual staining to non-small cell lung cancer and a different stain modality. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study extends the generalizability of the deep learning technique for virtual staining to lung cancer and multiplex immunofluorescence staining modalities. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication slightly rephrases the scope to 'lung cancer' and explicitly names multiplex immunofluorescence staining, clarifying the modalities while preserving the conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The virtual mIF enables the identification of key immune phenotypes important for immuno-oncology. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The virtual mIF enables the identification of key immune phenotypes important for immuno-oncology. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this result without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our methodology demonstrated good accuracy and reliability, achieving a fourth place finish (among 20 participating teams) in the 2022-23 and a second place finish (among 20 participating teams) in the 2023-24 CDC FluSight challenges. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our methodology demonstrated good accuracy and reliability in forecasting influenza hospitalizations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the general claim of good accuracy and reliability but omits the combined season-ranking details present in the preprint, narrowing the explicit scope of the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We developed a cross-domain data augmentation method that allowed us to train advanced machine learning models using symptom-based (syndromic) surveillance data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our method leverages influenza-like illness (ILI) data to infer historical estimates of influenza hospitalizations. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication focuses on leveraging ILI data for hospitalization estimates rather than explicitly describing the cross-domain augmentation approach, clarifying and narrowing the claimed methodological contribution and presenting it with lower certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our forecasts demonstrated superior accuracy to the CDC’s composite model in the 2022-23 season and matched its performance in 2023-24. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We achieved a fourth place finish among 20 participating teams in the 2022-23 CDC FluSight challenges. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports specific contest rankings for 2022-23 but does not maintain the explicit comparative claim against the CDC composite model across both seasons; this evidences a tempered and narrowed presentation of the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This approach successfully overcame data limitations, outperforming the majority of teams participating in the CDC FluSight project for 2022-23 and 2023-24. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We achieved a second place finish among 20 participating teams in the 2023-24 CDC FluSight challenges. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports specific season-level rankings supporting the claim of strong performance but presents them as individual season results rather than the broader conclusion that the approach outperformed the majority across both seasons.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our study demonstrates a robust and data-efficient strategy for training machine learning models for use in public health forecasting. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our findings highlight the utility of data augmentation and knowledge transfer in the application of machine learning models to public health surveillance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes data augmentation and knowledge transfer as the key takeaways, a clearer framing of the preprint's broader claim, but with reduced certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning algorithms can accurately predict PHQ-9 depression scale scores from NHANES data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning algorithms can accurately predict PHQ-9 depression scale scores from National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with equivalent certainty and wording (NHANES spelled out), so the claim is effectively unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The test-set mean absolute error was 3.03 on datasets with audiology-only predictors and 2.55 on datasets with all predictors. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The MAE numeric result appears in the preprint but is not present in any publication claims, indicating the numeric performance claim was omitted in the published version.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Participants’ self-reported frustration when talking to members of family or friends due to hearing loss was the fifth-most influential of all predictors. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Participants' self-reported frustration when talking to members of family or friends due to hearing loss was the fifth-most influential of all predictors. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this specific predictor ranking verbatim with the same certainty, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The most influential audiometric predictors of higher scores on a validated depression scale were social dynamics of hearing loss and not objective measures. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The most influential audiometric predictors of higher scores on a validated depression scale were social dynamics of hearing loss and not objective audiometric testing. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The content is the same (social dynamics over objective testing) but the publication states higher certainty, so the claim is unchanged semantically with amplified confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Such models could be useful in predicting depression scale scores at the point-of-care in conjunction with a standard audiologic assessment. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Such models could be useful in predicting depression scale scores at the point-of-care in conjunction with a standard audiologic assessment. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The recommendation is preserved verbatim but the published paper expresses greater confidence, so semantic/scope are unchanged and confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning algorithm trained with a research cohort can accurately categorize parkinsonian syndromes when tested on an independent clinical replication cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning algorithm trained on a research cohort can accurately classify parkinsonian syndromes in an independent clinical replication cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same overall result and certainty about cross-cohort classification performance, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: High accuracy was achieved in classifying PD versus PSP, PD versus MSA-P, PD versus MSA-C, PD versus atypical parkinsonian syndromes, and PSP versus MSA-C in both cohorts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: High accuracies were achieved in classifying PD-PSP, PD-MSA-C, PSP-MSA-C, and PD-atypical parkinsonism with balanced accuracies ranging from 0.840 to 0.983. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves many high-accuracy pairings but reports specific accuracy ranges and omits or reclassifies some pairings (e.g., MSA-P), narrowing the original broad claim and tempering its generality.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of the machine learning algorithm was slightly lower in the replication cohort compared to the training cohort. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Performance for classifying PD-MSA-P and MSA-C-MSA-P was lower, with balanced accuracies between 0.765 and 0.784. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state a generalized slight drop but specifies which pairings had lower performance, clarifying the original observation about lower replication performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Adding DTI metrics tended to increase performance in the training cohort but did not improve performance in the replication cohort. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Normalization using healthy control data improved DTI performance, but it remained lower than that using volumetry alone or combined with DTI. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication modulates the role of DTI—noting normalization helps but DTI still underperforms relative to volumetry—altering the original claim about where DTI helped vs. not, so it is revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning approach based on volumetric and DTI data can accurately classify subjects with early-stage parkinsonism in a clinical setting. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning approach based on volumetry allows for accurate classification of early-stage parkinsonism during clinical assessments. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The published conclusion emphasizes volumetry (dropping or de-emphasizing the DTI contribution), narrowing the original combined-data claim while retaining high confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A wearable sensor array coupled with machine learning methods can accurately discriminate PSP from PD. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A wearable sensor array coupled with machine learning methods can accurately discriminate PSP from PD. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same core conclusion/result about accurate discrimination with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The RF classifier outperformed the LR classifier, allowing discrimination of PSP from PD with 86% sensitivity and 90% specificity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The RF classifier outperformed the LR classifier, achieving 86% sensitivity and 90% specificity for discriminating PSP from PD. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical numeric performance and comparison between classifiers are preserved in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using data from the single lumbar sensor only resulted in a modest reduction in classification accuracy, which could be restored using 3 sensors. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using data from a single lumbar sensor resulted in only a modest reduction in classification accuracy, which could be restored using three sensors. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same finding about sensor count and recoverable accuracy with three sensors.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: For maximum specificity, the full six sensor array was needed. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: For maximum specificity, the full six sensor array is needed. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The requirement of the six-sensor array for maximum specificity is preserved with matching certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The choice of array complexity depends on context, suggesting the more complete array is advantageous for diagnostic purposes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The choice of array complexity depends on context, with a high specificity needed for diagnostic purposes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies the context (emphasizing high specificity for diagnostics) while preserving the original recommendation about complexity trade-offs.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPT-4 achieved performance comparable to the Framingham Risk Score (FRS) in cardiovascular risk prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The GPT-based score showed commendably comparable performance in CVD prediction when compared to traditional models. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core finding of comparable performance but broadens the comparator from FRS to 'traditional models' and reports lower certainty, so the claim is clarified and confidence tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Kaplan–Meier survival analysis of GPT-4 demonstrated distinct survival patterns among groups, revealing a strong association between the GPT risk prediction output and survival outcomes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim explicitly reports the Kaplan–Meier survival analysis or its strong association conclusion, indicating this specific survival-analysis claim was dropped or de-emphasized.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ChatGPT can achieve performance comparable to conventional models in predicting cardiovascular risk. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The GPT-based score showed commendably comparable performance in CVD prediction when compared to traditional models. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the comparable performance conclusion against conventional/traditional models with similar certainty, so this conclusion is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ChatGPT exhibits enhanced accessibility, flexibility, and the ability to provide user-friendly outputs. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-4 offers new opportunities for cardiovascular disease risk prediction compared to traditional methods. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames broader 'opportunities' rather than explicitly listing accessibility/flexibility/usability, which clarifies and slightly broadens the original usability-focused claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning algorithms built on radiography images can be used as a decision support mechanism to aid radiologists to speed up the diagnostic process. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning algorithms built on radiography images can be used as a decision support mechanism to aid radiologists to speed up the diagnostic process. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion verbatim with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The applicability of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for COVID-19 detection in chest X-ray images is critically analyzed. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The applicability of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for COVID-19 detection in chest X-ray images is critically analyzed. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same contribution statement without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CNN decisions should not be taken into consideration until clinicians can visually inspect and approve the regions of the input image used by CNNs that lead to its prediction. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CNN decisions should not be taken into consideration until clinicians can visually inspect and approve the region(s) of the input image used by CNNs that lead to its prediction. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The recommendation about clinician inspection of CNN-highlighted regions is preserved in the publication with the same emphasis and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Causes of Outcome Learning (CoOL) approach identifies combinations of exposures that could be responsible for an increased risk of a health outcome in population sub-groups.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Causes of Outcome Learning approach (CoOL) seeks to discover combinations of exposures that lead to an increased risk of a specific outcome in parts of the population.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution: discovering exposure combinations that increase outcome risk in population subgroups with equivalent scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The computational phase of the CoOL approach uses a tailored neural network for the non-negative additive model and Layer-wise Relevance Propagation for risk decomposition.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The computational phase of CoOL involves fitting a non-negative model on an additive scale, decomposing risk contributions, and clustering individuals based on these contributions.
alignments.rationale: Both describe the computational phase and risk decomposition, but the publication omits explicit mention of LRP/NN while adding clustering; this clarifies and slightly broadens the computational description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CoOL approach allows for exposures acting alone and in synergy with others.
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not explicitly state that exposures act alone or synergistically, so this specific claim about interaction modes is not carried forward.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CoOL approach encourages epidemiologists to identify combinations of pre-outcome exposures as potential causes of health outcomes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This approach encourages researchers to identify combinations of exposures as potential causes of health outcomes.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the exhortation to identify exposure combinations as potential causes with equivalent emphasis and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Expanding the ability to discover complex causes could result in more effective, targeted, and informed public health interventions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Expanding the ability to discover complex causes could result in more effective, targeted, and informed public health interventions.
alignments.rationale: The intervention implication is carried verbatim into the publication with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The one-year mortality classifier achieved an AUC ROC of 0.87.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The 1-year mortality classifier achieved an Area Under the Curve Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUC ROC) of 0.87.
alignments.rationale: The numeric performance claim is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The one-year frailty classifier obtained an AUC ROC of 0.89.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The 1-year frailty classifier obtained an AUC ROC of 0.89.
alignments.rationale: The frailty classifier's reported AUC is reproduced exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This is the first study predicting one-year frailty status based on a frailty index.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not assert the novelty claim of being the first study; instead it frames the work as providing new models, so the specific 'first' assertion is omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Mortality and frailty criteria are weakly correlated and have different distributions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Mortality and frailty criteria were weakly correlated and had different distributions, indicating that these assessment measurements are complementary for palliative care decision-making.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the weak correlation/different distributions claim and extends its implication for complementary use in palliative care, broadening the conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive models may be part of decision support systems for care services in non-cancer older patients after their external validation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study provides new models that can be part of decision-making systems for palliative care services in older patients after their external validation.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the potential for decision-support but narrows the target context from 'non-cancer older patients' to 'palliative care services', a more specific application.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The artificial neural network (ANN) developed in this work scores patients on the Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HDRS) and the Young Mania Rating Scale (YMRS) in moderate agreement with clinician assessments.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed deep learning pipeline demonstrates clinically significant agreement between predictions and assessments by an expert clinician, with a quadratic Cohen’s κ and macro-average F1 score both of 0.609.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides specific agreement metrics that quantify the preprint's statement of moderate/clinically significant agreement, clarifying the result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using physiological data recorded further away from when HDRS and YMRS were collected by the clinician leads to a deterioration in ANN performance.
alignments.rationale: None of the published claims explicitly report the temporal-distance effect on performance, so this specific performance-degradation claim is not present in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The task of inferring all items from HDRS and YMRS is challenging, indicating a need for research into domain-adaptation for real-world implementations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Investigating solutions to machine learning challenges such as multi-task learning and class imbalance is essential for the task of symptom scoring in mood disorders.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the recognition of challenges and the need for further ML research but frames them more broadly (multi-task learning, class imbalance) rather than specifically naming domain-adaptation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Predictive Biomarker Modeling Framework (PBMF) successfully found biomarkers that identify immunooncology-treated individuals who survive longer than those treated with other therapies.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Predictive Biomarker Modeling Framework (PBMF) identifies biomarkers of immuno-oncology-treated individuals who survive longer than those treated with other therapies.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result that PBMF identifies biomarkers associated with longer survival for IO-treated individuals.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients identified with the predictive biomarker uncovered by the PBMF had a 15% improvement in survival risk compared to those in the original trial.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients identified with this predictive biomarker show a 15% improvement in survival risk compared to those in the original trial.
alignments.rationale: The numeric survival-improvement result is reproduced verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PBMF identified potential predictive biomarkers for two other phase 3 clinical trials, achieving at least a 10% improvement in survival risk.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the specific claim about two additional phase 3 trials or the ≥10% improvement, so that specific assertion was not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PBMF provides a broad, rapid, and robust approach to inform biomarker strategy, offering actionable outcomes for clinical decision-making.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The PBMF provides a general-purpose, rapid, and robust approach to inform biomarker strategy.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same characterization of PBMF as a general, rapid, robust approach to inform biomarker strategy; the actionable outcome phrasing is consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study identified a novel sub-group of NSMP EC patients, termed ‘p53abn-like NSMP’, that had markedly inferior progression-free and disease-specific survival.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A subgroup of NSMP endometrial cancer patients, termed ‘p53abn-like NSMP’, has markedly inferior progression-free and disease-specific survival.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the identification and survival finding for the p53abn-like NSMP subgroup without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Shallow whole genome sequencing revealed a higher burden of copy number abnormalities in the ‘p53abn-like NSMP’ group compared to NSMP.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ‘p53abn-like NSMP’ group has a higher burden of copy number abnormalities compared to NSMP, indicating it is biologically distinct.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the finding of higher copy number abnormality burden and adds the interpretation of biological distinctness, but the core result is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The work demonstrates the power of AI to detect prognostically different and otherwise unrecognizable subsets of EC.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study demonstrates the power of AI to detect prognostically different subsets of endometrial cancer that are unrecognizable by conventional criteria.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion about AI detecting prognostically distinct, conventionally unrecognizable subsets.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The identification of the ‘p53abn-like NSMP’ group refines image-based tumor classification.
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI-powered histopathology image analysis can differentiate between p53abn and NSMP endometrial cancer subtypes.
alignments.rationale: Both claims concern AI-enabled histopathology improving subtype differentiation/classification; the publication frames it as differentiation between subtypes, consistent with refining image-based classification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An electronic frailty index incorporating multi-domain data can efficiently predict all-cause mortality in patients undergoing TAVR. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An electronic frailty index incorporating multidomain data can efficiently predict all-cause mortality in patients undergoing TAVR. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Claims are effectively identical in wording and meaning (only minor formatting of 'multi-domain' vs 'multidomain').

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning survival analysis model found that the most important predictors of mortality were APTT, INR, severity of tricuspid regurgitation, cumulative hospital stays, cumulative number of readmissions, creatinine, urate, ALP, and QTc/QT intervals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning survival analysis model found that the most important predictors of mortality were activated partial thromboplastin time, INR, severity of tricuspid regurgitation, cumulative hospital stays, cumulative number of readmissions, creatinine, urate, alkaline phosphatase, and QTc/QT intervals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication spells out acronyms and expands abbreviations but retains the same predictors and meaning, so this is a clarification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GBST significantly outperformed random survival forests and multivariate Cox regression for mortality prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GBST significantly outperformed random survival forests and multivariate Cox regression for mortality prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is stated identically in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pandemic dreams showed a higher proportion of anger and sadness words compared to dreams before the COVID-19 outbreak. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pandemic dreams showed a higher proportion of anger and sadness words compared to dreams reported before the Covid-19 outbreak. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Wording differences are minor; the substantive result is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pandemic dreams had higher average semantic similarities to the terms contamination and cleanness. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pandemic dreams had higher average semantic similarities to the terms 'contamination' and 'cleanness'. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same claim; publication only adds quotes around the terms.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The features of pandemic dreams explained 39% of the variance in PANSS negative subscale, indicating a link to mental suffering related to social isolation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The features of pandemic dreams explained 40% of the variance in the PANSS negative subscale related to socialization. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric value is rounded (39→40) and the interpretation shifts from a general 'mental suffering related to social isolation' to framing specifically 'related to socialization', a narrower emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results corroborate the hypothesis that pandemic dreams reflect mental suffering, fear of contagion, and important changes in daily habits. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results corroborate the hypothesis that pandemic dreams reflect mental suffering, fear of contagion, and important changes in daily habits that directly impact socialization. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication adds specificity that the changes in daily habits 'directly impact socialization', narrowing the stated impact domain while retaining the overall conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Unstructured clinical text can be used to reduce selection bias and improve medical practice. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Unstructured clinical text can be used to reduce selection bias and improve medical practice. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed framework based on natural language processing uncovers interpretable potential confounders from text. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed framework based on natural language processing uncovers interpretable potential confounders from text. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The framework and its role are stated the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method shifts the hazard ratio estimate towards the results of established randomized clinical trials. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method shifts the hazard ratio estimate towards the results of established randomized clinical trials. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same empirical finding reported in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The uncovered terms can be interpreted by oncologists for clinical insights. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The uncovered terms can be interpreted by oncologists for clinical insights. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Text of the claim is the same though the publication labels it as a result rather than a conclusion; substantive meaning is unchanged.

alignments.pub_claim_text: This proof-of-concept study enables more credible causal inference using observational data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This claim appears in the publication but was not present in the preprint and extends the stated implications toward causal inference credibility.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PheWAS identified many risk factors for suicidal behavior (SB) and suicidal ideation (SI)-only, as well as specific psychiatric disorders involved in the progression from SI-only to SB. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PheWAS identified many risk factors for suicidal behavior (SB) and suicidal ideation (SI) only, as well as specific psychiatric disorders involved in the progression from SI-only to SB. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the PheWAS findings verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Polygenic risk scores (PRS) for suicide attempt were only associated with SB, even after accounting for psychiatric disorder PRS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Polygenic risk scores (PRS) for suicide attempt were only associated with SB, even after accounting for psychiatric disorder PRS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SI PRS were only associated with SI-only, although this association was not present after accounting for psychiatric disorder PRS. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SI PRS were only associated with SI-only, although this association was not present after accounting for psychiatric disorder PRS. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No substantive change between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These findings advance understanding of distinct genetic and clinical risk factors for SB and SI-only. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These findings advance understanding of distinct genetic and clinical risk factors for SB and SI-only. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion is carried over unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The identified risk factors will aid in early detection and intervention efforts for suicidal ideation and behavior. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The identified risk factors will aid in early detection and intervention efforts for suicidal ideation and behavior. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Practical implication claim remains the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The rheumatology research community is increasingly adopting novel AI techniques. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Multiple and novel data science techniques have been used in a wide range of rheumatic and musculoskeletal diseases including rare diseases. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication shifts from an observed 'increasing adoption' by the community to a broader statement that multiple novel techniques have been used across many diseases, including rare ones; the focus and framing are altered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is an upward trend in the number of articles that combine AI and rheumatology. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report or retain an explicit statement about an upward trend in article counts, so this specific claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Rheumatic and musculoskeletal rare diseases are gaining from AI techniques. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Multiple and novel data science techniques have been used in a wide range of rheumatic and musculoskeletal diseases including rare diseases. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication explicitly mentions rare diseases among areas where techniques have been used, which corresponds to and slightly reframes the preprint claim that rare diseases are gaining from AI.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sample size and the data type used in research are heterogeneous. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The sample size and the data type used in the research articles are heterogeneous. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about heterogeneity is the same; the publication increases the stated certainty but the substantive claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Independent validation of the models should be promoted. (certainty=low, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly include a recommendation promoting independent validation, so this recommendation appears omitted.

alignments.pub_claim_text: New technical approaches are expected to arrive in the short-middle term. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This forward-looking statement about new technical approaches appears in the publication but is not present in the preprint, so it is an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best performing machine learning model achieved high predictive accuracy for dengue importation with an area under the receiver operating characteristic score of 0.94. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best performing machine learning model achieved high predictive accuracy, with an area under the receiver operating characteristic score of 0.94 and a maximized sensitivity score of 0.88. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the AUC 0.94 result but adds an additional performance metric (sensitivity 0.88), so the original claim is clarified with extra detail rather than changed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The most important predictor variables for dengue importation were the source country’s dengue incidence rate, population size, and volume of air passengers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The predictor variables identified as most important for predicting dengue importation were the source country’s dengue incidence rate, population size, and volume of air passengers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same set of important predictors with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Network centrality measures significantly influenced the predictions of dengue importation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Network centrality measures, describing the positioning of European countries within the air travel network, were influential to the predictions of dengue importation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the claim that centrality measures influenced predictions but adds contextual detail about what the centrality measures describe, thus clarifying the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates the utility of model-agnostic methods to provide a comprehensive understanding of the predictions made by the machine learning model. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrated the high predictive performance of a machine learning model in predicting dengue importation. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint emphasizes the utility of model-agnostic interpretability methods, but the publication focuses on demonstrating predictive performance; the interpretability emphasis appears reduced/narrowed in the published claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Similar machine learning approaches can be utilized in the development of an operational early warning surveillance system for dengue importation. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Similar approaches can be utilized in the development of an operational early warning surveillance system for dengue importation. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim about potential use in an operational early warning system is retained verbatim with comparable certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PK-RNN-V E offers a root mean squared error (RMSE) of 5.39 and outperforms the traditional Bayesian model (VTDM model) with an RMSE of 6.29. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PK-RNN-V E offers a root mean squared error (RMSE) of 5.39. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the identical RMSE of 5.39; the outperforming aspect is reported separately in another publication claim but the RMSE value itself is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep-learning based pharmacokinetic model for vancomycin (PK-RNN-V E) provides better prediction accuracy by integrating multiple patient-specific features from time sequence electronic health record data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PK-RNN-V E outperforms the traditional Bayesian model (VTDM model) with an RMSE of 6.29. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the result that PK-RNN-V E outperforms the Bayesian model, but it omits the explanatory wording about integration of time-sequence EHR features; the mechanistic description is reduced in the published phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Current traditional Bayesian models for vancomycin levels were tested in only a limited patient population and take limited patient-specific features. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not reproduce the specific limitation claim regarding limited testing populations and limited features for traditional Bayesian models, so this limitation statement appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The increasing availability of electronic health record (EHR) data offers an opportunity to develop TDM models for real-world patient populations. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state the opportunity presented by growing EHR availability for TDM model development, so this broader opportunity claim is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning dimensionality reduction methods achieved marginal but non-significant improvements over traditional stepwise variable selection in risk stratification models for chest pain patients. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: All eight dimensionality reduction methods achieved comparable performance with the traditional stepwise variable selection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication characterizes the methods as yielding 'comparable performance,' which clarifies the preprint's description of marginal/non-significant improvements into a more direct statement of comparability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The multidimensional scaling algorithm had the best performance among the dimensionality reduction methods, with an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.901. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The multidimensional scaling algorithm performed the best with an area under the curve of 0.901. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the identical performance result for multidimensional scaling (AUC 0.901) with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: HRnV-based models using stepwise logistic regression outperformed several existing clinical scores for predicting 30-day major adverse cardiac events (MACE). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: All prediction models generated in this study outperformed several existing clinical scores in ROC analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication broadens the preprint's specific HRnV-based finding to state that all models in the study outperformed existing scores, so the original specific claim is expanded in scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Traditional stepwise approaches provide model transparency and interpretability, unlike machine learning dimensionality reduction models which are considered black boxes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Dimensionality reduction models are black box models, making them difficult to explain and interpret in clinical practice. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication make the same point about dimensionality reduction methods being black-box and difficult to interpret; publication frames it as a limitation with similar meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A deep learning framework was developed to analyze samples of the Cytosponge®-TFF3 test for detecting Barrett’s Esophagus. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A deep learning framework was developed to analyze samples of the Cytosponge-TFF3 test for detecting Barrett’s esophagus. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the development of the deep learning framework for Cytosponge-TFF3 with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework was trained and independently validated on data from two clinical trials, analyzing a total of 4,662 pathology slides from 2,331 patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework was trained and independently validated on data from two clinical trials, analyzing a total of 4,662 pathology slides from 2,331 patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The training and validation dataset size and provenance are restated exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: By substituting manual review with automated review in low-priority classes, pathologist workload can be reduced by up to 66% while matching the diagnostic performance of expert pathologists. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: By substituting manual review with automated review in low-priority classes, pathologist workload can be reduced by 57% while maintaining diagnostic performance. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the workload-reduction conclusion but reports a smaller quantitative effect (57% vs 66%), so the numeric claim is revised and the effect appears tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results lay the foundation for tailored, semi-automated decision support systems embedded in clinical workflows. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state the broader claim about laying the foundation for tailored, semi-automated decision support systems, so this forward-looking conclusion appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ANTsX ecosystem consists of multiple open-source software libraries that house top-performing algorithms for processing and analyzing biological and medical imaging data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ANTsX ecosystem consists of multiple open-source software libraries that house top-performing algorithms for processing and analyzing biological and medical imaging data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this contribution claim verbatim with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Recent enhancements to the ANTs library include statistical, visualization, and deep learning capabilities through interfacing with R and Python. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Recent enhancements to the ANTs library include statistical, visualization, and deep learning capabilities through interfacing with R and Python. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates these specific enhancements identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning extensions ANTsRNet and ANTsPyNet contain several popular network architectures and trained models for specific applications. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning extensions ANTsRNet and ANTsPyNet contain several popular network architectures and trained models for specific applications. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: This contribution is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning analog for generating cortical thickness data from structural T1-weighted brain MRI significantly improves computational efficiency and provides comparable-to-superior accuracy over existing ANTs workflows. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning analog for generating cortical thickness data from structural T1-weighted brain MRI significantly improves computational efficiency and provides comparable-to-superior accuracy over existing ANTs workflows. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the performance and efficiency claims for the deep learning cortical thickness analog unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The comprehensive ANTsX approach serves as an important framework for medical image analysis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The comprehensive ANTsX approach serves as an important framework for medical image analysis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same concluding statement about ANTsX serving as an important framework.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI-based image recognition prototype demonstrates high agreement with manual weighing for dietary intake assessment in a hospital setting. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI-based image recognition prototype demonstrates high concordance with manual weighing for assessing dietary intake in hospitals. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with near-identical wording and emphasis on high concordance, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The concordance correlation coefficient for starches between the AI prototype and manual weighing is 0.957, indicating strong agreement. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The concordance correlation coefficient (CCC) for cereals and starchy food was 0.957, indicating strong agreement between the AI prototype and manual weighing. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric CCC (0.957) and interpretation are identical; only minor wording (starches vs cereals and starchy food) differs.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The pilot study indicates that the AI-based system can accurately assess food types and portions, suggesting its potential for routine use in clinical nutrition practices. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The pilot study indicates that further improvements are necessary before the AI-based system can be reliably used in direct patient care. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication changes the implication from potential routine use to a caution that further improvements are needed, narrowing and tempering the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Mean differences in measurements between the AI prototype and manual weighing were -12.01g for starches, 1.19g for animal protein sources, and -14.85g for vegetables. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific numeric mean-difference results reported in the preprint do not appear in the publication claim list, indicating they were not carried forward into the summarized publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning algorithms could predict hospital stays with a high risk of self-harm based on readily available data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning algorithms could predict hospital stays with a high risk of self-harm based on readily available data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same high-level result with the same emphasis and data availability caveat.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The random forest model and the ensemble model were the best-performing algorithms for predicting self-harm, with AUC-ROC values of 0.70 and 0.77, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best performing algorithms were the random forest model (AUC-ROC 0.70) and the ensemble model (AUC-ROC 0.77). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the identical model performance results and AUC-ROC values.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study represents an advance in the prediction of inpatient self-harm by limiting the information required for predictions to data that is universally available at the point of admission. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims do not include the evaluative statement that the study constitutes an 'advance' via data-limitation, so this interpretive conclusion appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: External validation of these findings is required to assess the real-world application of the machine learning models developed in this study. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings should be validated externally with other real-world, prospective data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same limitation/recommendation for external validation using prospective real-world data, with equivalent emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Weakly supervised learning can effectively detect the location of AIS and HI lesions in DWI. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Weakly supervised learning was able to spatially locate small stroke lesions in different positions and showed more sensitivity than human reading in identifying concurrent hemorrhagic infarction based on DWI. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same conclusion but adds specifics (small lesion positions and comparison to human reading), clarifying the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ResNet provided higher sensitivity and a lower missed diagnosis rate than VGG for spatially-locating AIS lesions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The residual neural network displayed a higher sensitivity than the visual geometry group network in spatially locating small stroke and hemorrhagic infarction lesions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same comparative result (ResNet > VGG) for spatial localization, with similar emphasis on higher sensitivity.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In the HI dataset, the sensitivity of ResNet was 87.73% for AIS detection and 86.20% for HI detection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The residual neural network displayed a higher sensitivity than the visual geometry group network in spatially locating small stroke and hemorrhagic infarction lesions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports higher sensitivity for the ResNet but does not reproduce the specific numeric sensitivities presented in the preprint; the core finding is preserved but less numerically explicit.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep weakly supervised learning can reduce the labeling workload. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed approach can reduce the labeling workload. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication claim reduced labeling workload from the proposed weakly supervised approach with equivalent framing.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The residual neural network had significantly greater patient-level sensitivity than human readers in identifying concurrent hemorrhagic infarction with GRE as the reference standard. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This publication claim introduces a comparison to human readers that was not present in the preprint claims list; it appears as a new (added) result.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Weak labeling of lesions required significantly less time than full labeling of lesions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a specific timing/result about weak vs full labeling not explicit in the preprint claims, so it is treated as an added, more specific claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: QAT values from the DN were found to increase from 8.65% ± 1.38% to 21.38% ± 1.82% over a two-year period. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: QAT values from the DN were found to increase from 8.65% ± 1.38% to 21.38% ± 1.82% over a two-year period. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric result is reproduced identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Comparison of CNN model results to intensity-based measures demonstrated a systematic drop in the Dice coefficient over time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CNN model demonstrated a systematic drop in the Dice coefficient over time, decreasing from 0.86 ± 0.03 to 0.45 ± 0.04. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides the same observation with added numeric Dice values, but the underlying result (systematic drop) is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The trends observed in DN QAT values were consistent with clinical scores for AT, bronchiectasis, and mucus plugging. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The trends observed in DN QAT values were consistent with clinical scores for AT, bronchiectasis, and mucus plugging. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion linking QAT trends to clinical scores.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DN approach was found to be less susceptible to variations in expiratory deflation levels than the threshold-based approach. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DN approach was found to be less susceptible to variations in expiratory deflation levels than the threshold-based approach. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The neural network model demonstrated a mean absolute error (MAE) of 3.9 days in estimating gestational age, which is more accurate than the 4.7 days MAE for standard fetal biometry. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI model's performance in estimating gestational age was consistent across different locations, with a mean absolute error difference of -0.6 days in North Carolina and -1.0 days in Zambia. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model also showed improved accuracy in a test set of women who conceived by in vitro fertilization, with a MAE of 2.8 days compared to 3.6 days for biometry. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results indicate a potential for broader access to ultrasound diagnostics for pregnant individuals in low-resource settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Performance on previously-unseen subjects is strongly overestimated when models are trained using segment-based holdout. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Segment-based holdout does not reflect the real-world performance of a translational DNN model in clinical settings. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Automatic segmentation achieves comparable performance to human experts with a mean DICE coefficient of 0.76 and a Pearson Correlation Coefficient of 0.986 for mean T2* within the masks. (certainty=high, type=result)
