alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep convolutional neural networks (DNNs) demonstrated appreciable performance in differentiating dermatological images at both low and high image resolutions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DNN framework differentiated dermatological images with appreciable performance at both low and high image resolutions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The technical result about resolution-dependent performance is preserved with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The accuracy of DNNs improved when clinical data such as age, sex, and lesion localization were added to the framework. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The accuracy of the DNN framework was improved when clinical data such as age, sex, and lesion localization were added. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The improvement from adding clinical covariates is stated equivalently in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The lowest accurate DNN outperformed general practitioners in classifying skin lesions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The least accurate DNN outperformed general practitioners in categorizing skin lesions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The comparative performance statement is the same, only minor wording differences exist.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Physicians' accuracy in diagnosing skin lesions was statistically improved when they used the output of the DNN framework as guidance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: General practitioners' accuracy was statistically improved when allowed to use the output of the DNN framework as guidance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication narrows the group from 'physicians' broadly to 'general practitioners,' changing the target population and thus revising the scope of the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study aims to integrate AI tools into the practice of general practitioners to enhance diagnosis accuracy in settings lacking high-resolution equipment. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning frameworks trained on large datasets can help non-dermatologist physicians improve their accuracy in categorizing the seven most common pigmented skin lesions. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication states a more concrete, positively framed outcome (helping non-dermatologist physicians with seven pigmented lesions) compared to the preprint's broader implementation aim, narrowing scope and increasing stated confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models can accurately quantify histological features of ulcerative colitis from H&E-stained whole slide images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Convolutional neural network models can quantify histologic features pertinent to ulcerative colitis disease activity from whole slide images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core claim that CNN/ML models quantify histologic features is preserved with comparable specificity and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Model-predicted histological features correlate with disease severity and pathologist-assigned Nancy Histological Index (NHI) scores. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The extracted slide-level human-interpretable features demonstrated strong correlations with disease severity and pathologist-assigned Nancy histological index scores. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions report strong correlations between model-derived features and disease severity/NHI scores with similar language.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study achieved a weighted kappa of 0.93 and a Spearman correlation of 0.93 when predicting NHI scores compared to manual pathologist consensus. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A random forest classifier based on 13 human-interpretable features can accurately predict Nancy histological index scores with a weighted kappa of 0.91. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a different weighted kappa (0.91 vs 0.93) and specifies the classifier type and feature set, revising the numeric and methodological details.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning approach can predict histological remission with high accuracy of 0.94. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model can predict histologic remission with a high accuracy of 0.97 based on the absence of neutrophil extravasation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a different accuracy (0.97 vs 0.94) and specifies the basis for remission prediction (absence of neutrophil extravasation), indicating a revision to the reported result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This approach has the potential to enable standardized and robust assessment of histologic remission for improved evaluation of disease activity and prognosis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Tissue and cell predictions from the models showed comparable agreement to pathologist annotations. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the preprint's broader potential conclusion as evidence of comparable agreement to pathologists, clarifying practical comparability though not repeating the exact forward-looking statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study aims to systematically examine and distinguish the concepts of 'precision medicine' and 'personalised medicine' across various disciplines. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study aims to systematically examine the current understanding of 'precision medicine' and 'personalised medicine' using Rodgers' evolutionary concept analysis. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both statements describe the same study aim; the publication adds the specific method (Rodgers') but does not change the aim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis will identify the respective attributes and related terms of 'precision medicine' and 'personalised medicine'. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application and interpretation of 'precision medicine' and 'personalised medicine' are heterogeneous across various disciplines. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint promised identification of attributes/terms; the publication emphasizes heterogeneity of application/interpretation, which clarifies the nature of the identified attributes rather than contradicting the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The systematic concept analysis will clarify the application and distinction of the two concepts in given settings and circumstances. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The systematic concept analysis will clarify the distinction between 'precision medicine' and 'personalised medicine' in their application. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both assert the analysis will clarify the distinction in application; wording and intent are effectively the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This broader concept analysis will facilitate interdisciplinary communication and translational medical research. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The research will facilitate interdisciplinary communication and translational medical research. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The contribution/conclusion about facilitating interdisciplinary communication and translational research is reiterated unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed FLAIM method resulted in the highest improvement in AUC of 7.78%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication contains only an author name correction and does not report the FLAIM results, so this preprint result is absent in the published record.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The FLAIM framework achieved an accuracy of 81.30% and an AUC of 81.38% using the support vector machine model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No corresponding performance metrics or method claims appear in the publication; the published item only notes an author name correction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of the FLAIM framework shows promising clinical outcome prediction for ICU patients with paralytic ileus after the first 24 hours of admission. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include this clinical conclusion; it contains only a correction notice unrelated to the claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The author names Fahad Shabbir Ahmad and Raza-Ul-Mustafa were incorrectly published in the original article. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: This publication-only correction has no counterpart in the preprint and is thus an added non-scientific/metadata claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning approaches have the potential for better predictive performance and generalisability over existing childhood asthma prediction models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning approaches improved the predictive performance of existing regression-based models for childhood asthma. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint claimed potential; the publication reports actual improvement, so the claim is strengthened (amplified) and revised from possibility to demonstrated effect.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best predictive performance was demonstrated by a Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithm for both the CAPE model (AUC=0.71) and CAPP model (AUC=0.82). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Support Vector Machine algorithms provided the best performance for both the CAPE and CAPP models, with AUC values of 0.71 and 0.82, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same performance findings and AUC values for the SVM models.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both models demonstrated good generalisability in the Manchester Asthma and Allergy Study cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both models demonstrated good generalisability in the Manchester Asthma and Allergy Study cohort. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about generalisability in the same external cohort is present in both versions with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using machine learning approaches improved upon the predictive performance of existing regression-based models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning approaches improved the predictive performance of existing regression-based models for childhood asthma. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This preprint conclusion matches the publication conclusion directly that ML improved over regression-based models.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The CAPE model identified eight predictors and the CAPP model identified twelve predictors for school-age asthma. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the specific predictor counts which were not stated in the preprint; this is an added detailed result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CovRNN shows higher performance than traditional models in predicting COVID-19 patient outcomes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CovRNN models showed high prediction accuracy and transferability through consistently good performances on multiple external datasets. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint claimed superiority over traditional models; the publication reports high accuracy and transferability but does not explicitly claim or demonstrate superiority, so the claim is tempered and narrowed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CovRNN achieved an area under the receiving operating characteristic (AUROC) of 93% for mortality and mechanical ventilation predictions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CovRNN binary models achieved AUROCs of 93.0% for the prediction of in-hospital mortality, 92.9% for the prediction of mechanical ventilation, and 86.5% for the prediction of a prolonged hospital stay. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides more precise AUROC values and extends the reported outcomes (adds prolonged stay); the original numbers for mortality and ventilation are preserved and clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: External validation confirmed AUROCs in similar ranges for CovRNN. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: External validation confirmed AUROCs in similar ranges for in-hospital mortality prediction, mechanical ventilation, and prolonged hospital stay. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly states external validation across the listed outcomes; this maintains the claim and broadens the referenced outcomes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CovRNN demonstrated high prediction accuracy and transferability across multiple external datasets. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CovRNN models showed high prediction accuracy and transferability through consistently good performances on multiple external datasets. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about high accuracy and transferability across external datasets is consistent between preprint and publication.

alignments.pub_claim_text: CovRNN achieved a concordance index of 86.0% for in-hospital mortality and 92.6% for mechanical ventilation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents concordance index metrics that were not present in the preprint; this is an added quantitative result.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The results demonstrate the feasibility of a COVID-19 predictive model that delivers high accuracy without the need for complex feature engineering. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a procedural/conclusion claim about feasibility and lack of complex feature engineering not stated in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Natural Language Processing (NLP) is a promising tool for the automated extraction of epidemiological data from texts. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Natural Language Processing (NLP) can facilitate the establishment of datasets for epidemiological data extraction. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both statements express the same conclusion that NLP is useful for creating epidemiological datasets; wording differs slightly but meaning is consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The spaCy NLP library performed best among the tested algorithms for automated extraction of location data, with a sensitivity of 0.92 and an F1 score of 0.83. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The spaCy NLP library demonstrated the best performance in automated extraction of location data with a sensitivity of 0.92 and an F1 score of 0.83. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical performance metrics and conclusion about spaCy are reported in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Automated geocoding services such as Google geocoding, Geonames, and Geoparser performed poorly, returning correct GIS information in only 60.4%, 52.7%, and 33.8% of cases, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Automated geocoding services performed poorly in locating historical plague outbreaks, with correct GIS information returned in only 60.4%, 52.7%, and 33.8% of cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same performance percentages and conclusion about poor geocoding performance for the historical data.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The rate of correct matches for historical regions and places was particularly low when using automated geocoding services. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Automated geocoding services performed poorly in locating historical plague outbreaks, with correct GIS information returned in only 60.4%, 52.7%, and 33.8% of cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both describe particularly poor performance for historical/place matching; the publication frames it as numeric results while the preprint emphasized the low match rate, but the substantive claim is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NLP tools have limitations, but they are potentially useful to accelerate the collection of data and the generation of a global plague outbreak database. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NLP tools have limitations but are potentially useful for accelerating the collection of data for a global plague outbreak database. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both emphasize limitations alongside the potential usefulness of NLP to accelerate dataset creation for plague outbreaks; meaning is consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: White matter disease was found to be associated with Parkinson’s disease, with an adjusted hazard ratio of 1.67 for patients <70 years and 1.33 for those ≥70 years. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: White matter disease was associated with Parkinson disease, with a hazard ratio of 1.67 for patients under 70 years and 1.33 for those 70 years and older. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Textual content and effect estimates are effectively identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Greater severity of white matter disease was associated with an increased incidence of Parkinson’s disease per 1000 person-years. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Greater severity of white matter disease was associated with increased incidence of Parkinson disease, ranging from 1.52 in patients without white matter disease to 4.90 in those with severe disease. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same association with more numeric detail but does not alter the underlying claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Covert brain infarction was not associated with Parkinson’s disease. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Covert brain infarction was not associated with Parkinson disease, as indicated by an adjusted hazard ratio of 1.05. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions state no association; the publication adds the adjusted HR but does not change the conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The association between white matter disease and Parkinson’s disease was strengthened with younger age. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The association between white matter disease and Parkinson disease was strengthened with younger age. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about stronger association at younger ages is identical in wording and intent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NLP-enhanced search facilitated significantly more accurate task completion than both string search and no search. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NLP-enhanced search facilitated more accurate task completion than both string search and no search. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim is the same in both versions about improved accuracy with NLP-enhanced search.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NLP-enhanced search and string search facilitated similar task speeds, both showing an increase in speed over no search function. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NLP-enhanced search and string search facilitated similar task speeds, both showing an increase in speed compared to the no search function. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The content is the same (similar speeds) but the publication raises the certainty level, so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 93% of evaluators agreed that NLP-enhanced search would make clinical workflows more efficient than string search. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: 93% of evaluators agreed that NLP-enhanced search would make clinical workflows more efficient than string search. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical survey result reported in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NLP-enhanced search improved both accuracy and speed of clinical EHR IR tasks compared to browsing clinical notes without search. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NLP-enhanced search improved both accuracy and speed of clinical EHR IR tasks compared to browsing clinical notes without search. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion is the same in both preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NLP-enhanced search improved accuracy and reduced the number of searches required for clinical EHR IR tasks compared to direct search term matching. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NLP-enhanced search improved accuracy and reduced the number of searches required for clinical EHR IR tasks compared to direct search term matching. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim about accuracy and reduced searches between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Text classification models can accurately predict a patient’s illness severity using only free-form text descriptions of patients without any manual data extraction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Text classification models can accurately predict a patient’s illness severity using only free-form text descriptions of patients without any manual data extraction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Core claim identical across preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best performing models were the BioClinicalBERT model on the truncated note task and the fastText model on the full note task, achieving macro-average AUROC scores of 0.845 and 0.865 respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best performing models were the BioClinicalBERT model on the truncated note task with a macro-average AUROC of 0.845 and the fastText model on the full note task with a macro-average AUROC of 0.865. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Model identities and performance metrics are identical between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Shapley values reveal human-interpretable model predictions that logically support model predictions and are understandable to clinicians. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Shapley values reveal human-interpretable model predictions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication shortens and frames the finding more strongly (result vs conclusion), removing some interpretability detail; confidence is increased.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Text classification models can serve as an additional patient safety tool in the perioperative setting and reduce manual chart review for medical billing. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These models can be an additional patient safety tool in the perioperative setting and reduce manual chart review for medical billing. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint conclusion verbatim, keeping scope and certainty the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Trials funded by industry were more likely to be blinded compared to trials that were not industry funded. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: RCTs published in high-impact journals are more likely to be blinded. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims concern blinding likelihood but the predictor changed from funder type (industry) to journal impact, so the claim was substantively revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Industry-funded RCTs had a smaller sample size than non-industry-funded RCTs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No corresponding publication claim about sample size by funder is present; the preprint claim does not appear in the published set.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Trials funded by industry had more citations than non-industry-funded trials. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the citation difference by funder; this preprint claim is not represented in the publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is important variability in key metrics of trial quality between industry-funded and non-industry-funded trials. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The quality of an RCT should be evaluated based on its methodological rigor, not its funder type. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both statements touch on assessing trial quality and funder relevance, but the publication shifts from describing variability to recommending evaluation criteria, changing the claim's emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SurgeryLLM incorporated domain-specific knowledge from current evidence-based surgical guidelines when presented with patient-specific data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SurgeryLLM incorporated domain-specific knowledge from current evidence-based surgical guidelines when presented with patient-specific data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The successful incorporation of guideline-based information represents a substantial step toward enabling greater surgeon efficiency. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The successful incorporation of guideline-based information represents a substantial step toward enabling greater surgeon efficiency. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion unchanged in wording and intent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The incorporation of guideline-based information improves patient safety. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The incorporation of guideline-based information improves patient safety. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Claim is identical across versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The incorporation of guideline-based information optimizes surgical outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The incorporation of guideline-based information optimizes surgical outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Unchanged claim about optimizing outcomes between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning assisted DSC-MRI radiomics can correctly predict glioma IDH subtyping in 71% of cases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Gliomas were correctly stratified by mutation status in 71% and by grade in 53% of the cases.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same 71% accuracy for stratification by mutation status (IDH), directly matching the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning assisted DSC-MRI radiomics can correctly predict glioma grade II-IV in 53% of cases, with 87% of predictions having an error distance of less than 1 grade.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Gliomas were correctly stratified by mutation status in 71% and by grade in 53% of the cases.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the 53% grade-stratification result but omits the additional favourable detail about the 87% small error distance, which tempers the reported confidence in the finer-grained performance detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Shape features are most effective in distinguishing grade II from grade III gliomas.
alignments.pub_claim_text: WHO grade II-III differentiation was mostly driven by shape features while texture and intensity features were more relevant for the III-IV separation.
alignments.rationale: Both texts explicitly attribute II-III differentiation mainly to shape features, matching the preprint claim directly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Texture and distribution features are most effective in distinguishing grade III from grade IV gliomas.
alignments.pub_claim_text: WHO grade II-III differentiation was mostly driven by shape features while texture and intensity features were more relevant for the III-IV separation.
alignments.rationale: Both state that texture-related features drive the III-IV separation, but the publication refers to "intensity" rather than "distribution" features, constituting a small revision in feature characterization.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed machine learning framework has the potential to contribute to the clinical translation of diagnostic tools in neuro-oncology.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning assisted DSC-MRI radiomics hold potential to address the inherent variability and presents a promising approach for non-invasive glioma molecular subtyping and grading.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the translational potential and further emphasizes addressing variability and promise for non-invasive subtyping/grading, slightly amplifying the stated promise.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ARIMA demonstrated the best performance using only historical case data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: ARIMA demonstrated the best performance using only historical case data.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the ARIMA result verbatim, indicating no change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SARIMAX significantly improved predictive accuracy by incorporating climate covariates.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SARIMAX significantly improved predictive accuracy by incorporating climate covariates.
alignments.rationale: The SARIMAX claim is reproduced exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The LSTM model, particularly when combined with climate covariates, proved to be the most accurate machine learning model.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The LSTM model, particularly when combined with climate covariates, proved to be the most accurate machine learning model.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the LSTM (with covariates) as the most accurate model, matching the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Ensemble models, such as the combination of LSTM and ARIMA, showed substantial improvements over individual models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Ensemble models, such as the combination of LSTM and ARIMA, showed substantial improvements over individual models.
alignments.rationale: The ensemble performance claim is preserved without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Integrating climate data significantly improved the accuracy of forecasts.
alignments.pub_claim_text: SARIMAX significantly improved predictive accuracy by incorporating climate covariates.
alignments.rationale: The general preprint claim about climate data improving accuracy maps well to the publication's SARIMAX/covariate result, though the publication presents this through specific model results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The novel end-to-end workflow for pathology powered by hyperspectral microscopy and deep learning conserves tissue samples by generating virtual histological stains.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel end-to-end workflow for pathology powered by hyperspectral microscopy and deep learning conserves tissue samples by generating virtual histologic stains.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the contribution claim verbatim, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The virtual images generated by the deep learning model reproduce the histological features present in real stained images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The virtual images generated from autofluorescence reproduce the histologic features present in real-stained images, as demonstrated by a randomized NASH scoring comparison study.
alignments.rationale: The publication confirms reproduction of histologic features but specifies the source (autofluorescence) and cites a randomized NASH scoring comparison, adding methodological detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is moderate to good concordance between pathologists’ scoring on corresponding real and virtual stains in a NASH scoring comparison study.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The virtual images generated from autofluorescence reproduce the histologic features present in real-stained images, as demonstrated by a randomized NASH scoring comparison study.
alignments.rationale: The publication documents the randomized NASH scoring comparison that underlies the concordance claim but does not restate the specific "moderate to good" phrasing, so the core result is preserved and framed with study design detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The end-to-end automated pathology platform is comparable to pathologists for NASH CRN scoring when evaluated against expert pathologist consensus scores.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The end-to-end automated pathology platform is comparable with an independent panel of pathologists for NASH Clinical Research Network scoring.
alignments.rationale: Both statements assert comparability of the automated platform with pathologists on NASH CRN scoring, using equivalent language.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study provides proof of concept for the virtual staining strategy, which could improve cost, efficiency, and reliability in pathology.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study provides proof of concept for the virtual staining strategy, which could improve cost, efficiency, and reliability in pathology.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the proof-of-concept conclusion identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The image-based model for automated diagnosis of electrocardiographic images achieved an average AUROC of 0.99 and AUPRC of 0.68 on the held-out test set.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The multilabel automated diagnosis model for electrocardiographic images demonstrates high performance on a distinct test set validated by at least two cardiologists, with an average AUROC of 0.99 and AUPRC of 0.86.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same AUROC but a substantially higher AUPRC (.86 vs .68) and emphasizes validation by cardiologists on a distinct test set, changing the numeric performance and broadening validation context.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of the image-based model was superior to that of signal-based models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The image-based model performs superiorly to signal-based models and learns clinically relevant cues based on Grad-CAM.
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the superiority of the image-based model over signal-based approaches and adds that it learns clinically relevant cues, preserving the original comparative claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Expert review confirmed that all 120 high confidence false positive predictions were actually true positives with incorrect labels.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the specific expert review finding about the 120 high-confidence false positives being true positives; this claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model learned generalizable features, as confirmed using Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The image-based model performs superiorly to signal-based models and learns clinically relevant cues based on Grad-CAM.
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication describe feature learning validated with Grad-CAM, preserving the claim that the model learned clinically relevant/generalizable cues.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed model extends the automated diagnosis of key rhythm and conduction disorders to printed ECGs and allows for the identification of hidden features.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of AI to ECGs across broad settings is facilitated by the development of this model.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the contribution more generally as facilitating AI application across broad ECG settings (broader than the specific 'printed ECGs' phrasing), clarifying and widening the scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CliniFact contains 1,970 scientific claims from 992 unique clinical trials related to 1,540 unique publications.
alignments.pub_claim_text: CliniFact contains 1,970 instances from 992 unique clinical trials related to 1,540 unique publications.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same dataset size and composition, matching the preprint claim directly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Intrinsic evaluation yields a Cohen’s Kappa score of 0.83, indicating strong inter-annotator agreement.
alignments.rationale: The publication's summarized claims do not include the reported Cohen's Kappa inter-annotator agreement statistic, so this specific metric appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Discriminative LLMs, such as PubMedBERT, achieved 81% accuracy and 79% F1-score, outperforming generative LLMs, such as Llama3-70B, which reached 52% accuracy and 39% F1-score.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Discriminative models, such as BioBERT, outperformed generative counterparts, such as Llama3-70B, in evaluating LLMs against CliniFact.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the qualitative ordering (discriminative outperform generative) but changes example discriminative model and omits the explicit numeric performance figures, reducing confidence in the detailed metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results demonstrate the potential of CliniFact as a benchmark for evaluating LLM performance in clinical research claim verification.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our results demonstrate the potential of CliniFact as a benchmark for evaluating LLM performance in clinical research claim verification.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion about CliniFact's potential as a benchmark, unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm achieved an OD dice of 0.938 and an OC dice of 0.801.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm achieved an OD dice of 0.938 and an OC dice of 0.801.
alignments.rationale: The numbers and wording are identical between preprint and publication, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning system obtained an area under the receiver operator characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.948 for glaucoma discriminating based on CDR calculations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm obtained an area under the receiver operator characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.948 for glaucoma discriminating based on CDR calculations.
alignments.rationale: The AUC value and claim are the same in both versions with only minor wording differences (algorithm vs system).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sensitivity and specificity of the algorithm for glaucoma discrimination were 0.850 and 0.853, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The sensitivity and specificity of the algorithm for glaucoma discrimination were 0.850 and 0.853, respectively.
alignments.rationale: Exact same numeric results and phrasing in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates the potential of the deep learning system to assist ophthalmologists in analyzing OD and OC segmentation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning system demonstrated potential to assist ophthalmologists in analyzing OD and OC segmentation.
alignments.rationale: Conclusion language is effectively identical between preprint and published version.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning-enhanced compartmental model predicts that R 0 will become less than one around June 19 to July 3, 2020.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning-enhanced compartmental model predicts that R<sub>0</sub> will fall to <1 around August 17-19, 2020, indicating the epidemic will effectively start to die out.
alignments.rationale: Both claims concern the date R0 falls below 1, but the publication shifts the predicted date later (mid-August) and frames the epidemic consequence explicitly, so the prediction was revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model predicts that the US 'Infected' population will peak around June 18 to July 2, 2020, between 1.34 million and 1.41 million individual cases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model predicts that the US 'infected' population will peak around August 16-18, 2020, at 3,228,574 to 3,308,911 individual cases.
alignments.rationale: Both claims predict the timing and magnitude of the US peak, but the published estimates are substantially later and much larger, representing a revision and broadening of the projected peak.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The models predict that the number of accumulative confirmed cases will cross the 2 million mark around June 10 to 11, 2020.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model predicts that the number of accumulative confirmed cases will cross the 5 million mark around August 7, 2020.
alignments.rationale: Same type of cumulative-case forecast but publication shifts the threshold, timing, and magnitude substantially, so the claim was revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning techniques are effective in estimating stochastic parameters with greatly reduced dependency on data particularity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning techniques are effective in estimating stochastic parameters with greatly reduced dependency on data particularity.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about deep learning effectiveness is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The overall view classification accuracy for the deep learning model was 93.4% for frames and 97.8% for video clips.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The overall classification accuracy of the deep learning view classifier for still frames was 93.4%.
alignments.rationale: The preprint bundled frame and clip accuracies; the publication separates them. The 93.4% frames accuracy matches exactly, so the claim about frames is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Frames were correctly classified with an accuracy of 96.0% for cardiac, 99.8% for thoracic, 95.2% for abdominal upper quadrants, and 95.9% for suprapubic.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Per view still frames were classified with high accuracy, including 96.0% for cardiac, 99.8% for pleural, 95.2% for abdominal upper quadrants, and 95.9% for suprapubic views.
alignments.rationale: Per-view accuracies are the same; the publication uses slightly different terminology ('pleural' vs 'thoracic'), so wording was clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A deep learning model can be developed to accurately classify pediatric FAST views.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A deep learning classifier can accurately predict pediatric FAST views.
alignments.rationale: The core conclusion that a DL classifier can accurately identify pediatric FAST views is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Accurate view classification is the important first step to support developing a consistent and accurate multi-stage deep learning model for pediatric FAST interpretation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Accurate view classification is important for quality assurance and feasibility of a multi-stage deep learning FAST model.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the importance toward quality assurance and feasibility; the meaning is similar but slightly clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best performing DL-CNB model achieved an AUC of 0.816 in predicting positive ALN metastasis in the independent test cohort.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best-performing DL-CNB model achieved an AUC of 0.816 in predicting positive ALN metastasis in the independent test cohort.
alignments.rationale: Exact match in result and phrasing (minor hyphenation only).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DL-CNB model incorporating clinical data yielded the best accuracy of 0.831, particularly for patients younger than 50 years with an AUC of 0.918.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DL-CNB model incorporating clinical data yielded the best accuracy of 0.831, particularly for patients younger than 50 years with an AUC of 0.918.
alignments.rationale: Identical numerical results and subgroup finding in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The top signatures most predictive of ALN metastasis were characterized by nuclei features including density, circumference, circularity, and orientation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The top signatures most predictive of ALN metastasis were characterized by nucleus features including density, circumference, circularity, and orientation.
alignments.rationale: The predictive features listed are the same; only minor singular/plural wording differs.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study provides a novel deep learning-based biomarker on primary tumor CNB slides to predict the metastatic status of ALN preoperatively for patients with early breast cancer.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study provides a novel DL-based biomarker on primary tumor CNB slides to predict the metastatic status of ALN preoperatively for patients with EBC.
alignments.rationale: Conclusion content is the same; publication uses abbreviations (DL, EBC) but conveys the same claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only one quarter of the evaluated publications made code publicly available, fulfilling the minimum requirement for reproducibility and reusability.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Only one-quarter (41 of 160 publications) made code publicly available.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides specific counts, clarifying the preprint's 'one quarter' statement without changing its meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Among the publications that made code available, three quarters analyzed their results statistically.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Among the 41 studies that made code available, three-quarters (30 of 41) analyzed their results statistically.
alignments.rationale: Same relative proportion, publication adds exact counts, so the claim is clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Less than half of the publications that made code available released their trained model weights.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Half of the studies that made code available (20 of 41) released their trained model weights.
alignments.rationale: The preprint stated 'less than half' while the publication reports exactly half (20 of 41), indicating a corrected/revised quantitative claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only about a third of the publications that made code available used an independent cohort for evaluation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Approximately a third (16 of 41) of the studies that made code available used an independent cohort for evaluation.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides exact numbers for the 'about a third' claim, clarifying but not changing the substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning mammographic density predictions on low dose mammograms are highly correlated with standard dose equivalents for feature extraction and end-to-end approaches across multiple image resolutions. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning mammographic density predictions on low dose mammograms are highly correlated with standard dose equivalents across multiple image resolutions. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion about high correlation across resolutions; wording is effectively equivalent though the publication omits the explicit 'feature extraction and end-to-end' phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Increasing resolution results in improved performance of both feature extraction methods for standard and simulated low dose images. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Increasing resolution results in improved performance of both feature extraction methods for standard and simulated low dose images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is verbatim between preprint and publication; reported certainty is higher in the publication, so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning models can reliably make high quality mammographic density predictions on low dose mammograms. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning models can reliably make high quality mammographic density predictions on low dose mammograms. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion appears verbatim and with the same level of certainty in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 55.16% of all patients presenting to this ED had pain on arrival. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: 55.16% of all patients presenting to this ED had pain on arrival. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact match between preprint and publication on the reported prevalence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There were significant differences in demographics, arrival and departure patterns between those patients with and without pain. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No matching claim about demographic or arrival/departure differences appears in the publication; the preprint claim was omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The COVID-19 pandemic initially precipitated a decrease followed by a sharp, sustained rise in the prevalence of pain on arrival. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Covid-19 pandemic initially precipitated a decrease followed by a sharp, sustained rise in pain on arrival. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The temporal pattern is described the same way, but the publication reports lower certainty, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of a clinical text deep learning model has successfully identified the prevalence of pain on arrival. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Applying a clinical text deep learning model has successfully identified the incidence of pain on arrival. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication uses 'incidence' instead of the preprint's 'prevalence', an epidemiologically meaningful change in term usage, so the claim is revised though the overall conclusion about model utility remains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combination of the clinical text deep learning model and interrupted time series analysis has identified the effects of the COVID-19 pandemic on pain care in the ED. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combination of clinical text deep learning models and interrupted time series analysis has reported on the effects of the Covid-19 pandemic on pain care in the ED. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication softens language from 'identified the effects' to 'reported on the effects' and lowers certainty, so the claim is clarified and confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PUMA dataset includes 155 primary and 155 metastatic melanoma H&E stained regions of interest with nuclei and tissue annotations. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The PUMA dataset includes 155 primary and 155 metastatic melanoma regions with nuclei and tissue annotations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the dataset composition with equivalent content; H&E mention is stylistic and does not change meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Hover-NeXt model, trained on the PUMA dataset, demonstrated the best performance for lymphocyte detection, approaching human interobserver agreement. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Hover-NeXt model, trained on the PUMA dataset, demonstrated the best performance for lymphocyte detection, approaching human interobserver agreement. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Direct match in result and reported certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Heuristic post-processing of deep learning models improves the detection of non-common classes, such as epithelial nuclei. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Heuristic postprocessing of deep learning models improved the detection of noncommon classes, such as epithelial nuclei. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same finding with equivalent wording; capitalization/spacing differences are cosmetic.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The PUMA dataset is the first melanoma specific dataset that can be used to develop melanoma-specific nuclei and tissue segmentation models. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The PUMA dataset is the first melanoma-specific dataset that can be used to develop melanoma-specific nuclei and tissue segmentation models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the contribution claim verbatim (hyphenation difference only).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Incorporating tissue and nuclei segmentation is a step towards improved deep learning nuclei segmentation performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Incorporating tissue and nuclei segmentation is a step toward improved deep learning nuclei segmentation performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Same conceptual claim in both versions; phrasing is effectively identical.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EfficientNet achieved the highest detection performance, with an F1 score of 0.82, precision of 0.88, recall of 0.79, and AUC of 0.71. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EfficientNet achieved the highest detection performance, with an F1 score of 0.82, precision of 0.88, recall of 0.79, and AUC of 0.71. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical numeric results and conclusion reported in publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Among segmentation models, nnUNetv2 performed best, achieving a DSC of 0.74, with precision and recall values of 0.73 and 0.83, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Among segmentation models, nnUNetv2 performed best, achieving a DSC of 0.74, precision of 0.73, and recall of 0.83. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same performance metrics and outcome reported in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pairwise t-tests showed that nnUNetv2 outperformed ResAttUNet, ResUNet, and UNet in segmentation accuracy (p < 0.01). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pairwise t-tests showed that nnUNetv2 outperformed other models in segmentation accuracy (p < 0.01). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the statistical finding but uses broader wording ('other models') rather than listing each comparator; meaning is the same but slightly generalized in wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The radiomic features derived from deep learning-based segmentations were comparable to those from manual segmentations, supporting the clinical applicability of these methods. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Radiomic features derived from deep learning-based segmentations were comparable to manual segmentations, supporting clinical applicability. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Equivalent conclusion about radiomic feature comparability and clinical applicability is retained in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Further analysis of lesion detection and spatial distribution, as well as lesion quality differentiation, underscores the models’ potential for improving diagnostic workflows and patient outcomes in clinical settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include this broader forward-looking claim about workflow and patient outcome improvements; it appears to have been omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLMs, particularly ChatGPT and GPT-4, showed diverse applications in clinical reasoning, social media, and education within psychiatry. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs, particularly ChatGPT and GPT-4, showed diverse applications in clinical reasoning, social media, and education within psychiatry. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this descriptive finding verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: They can assist in diagnosing mental health issues, managing depression, evaluating suicide risk, and supporting education in the field. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: They can assist in diagnosing mental health issues, managing depression, evaluating suicide risk, and supporting education in the field. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same set of application areas and level of certainty are presented in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The review points out limitations of LLMs, such as difficulties with complex cases and potential underestimation of suicide risks. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The review points out limitations of LLMs, such as difficulties with complex cases and potential underestimation of suicide risks. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation statement is carried into the publication without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Early research in psychiatry reveals LLMs’ versatile applications, from diagnostic support to educational roles. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Early research in psychiatry reveals LLMs' versatile applications, from diagnostic support to educational roles. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is the same but the publication reports a lower certainty level, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future investigations are poised to explore the extent to which these models might redefine traditional roles in mental health care. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Future investigations are poised to explore the extent to which these models might redefine traditional roles in mental health care. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The forward-looking claim is unchanged in content but is reported with lower certainty in the publication, indicating tempered confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model can automatically differentiate between eight different hand movements, achieving a mean accuracy of 98.3% within the SCI group.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI system can automatically differentiate between eight different hand movements, achieving a mean accuracy of 98.3% within the SCI group.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this result verbatim with the same numeric performance and population.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model could distinguish with 100% accuracy whether a participant had an injury or not.
alignments.rationale: No publication claim corresponds to the preprint's statement about 100% injury-vs-no-injury classification; the claim is absent in the published list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Proportional control of a movement can only be correctly estimated if the latent space embedding of the movement follows an elliptical path.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Proportional control of a movement can only be correctly estimated if the latent space embedding of the movement follows an elliptical path.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this conclusion exactly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Analysis of the latent space of the model revealed that proportionally controllable movements exhibited an elliptical path, while movements lacking proportional control followed a chaotic trajectory.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis of the latent space revealed that movements lacking proportional control followed a chaotic trajectory.
alignments.rationale: The preprint combined both elliptical and chaotic observations; the publication separates the elliptical observation into another claim (Q2) and retains the chaotic-trajectory result here, narrowing the scope of this specific claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The presence of highly functional spared spinal motor neurons in the extrinsic muscles of the hand can generate proportional flexion and extension signals.
alignments.rationale: This biological/physiological statement appears in the publication but has no counterpart in the preprint claims list, so it is an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI models have demonstrated expert-level performance in image-based recognition and diagnostic tasks.
alignments.rationale: The publication focuses on themes and limitations in fairness literature and does not restate the general claim about expert-level diagnostic performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There are unknown disparities based on demographic factors that could worsen patient care for some groups.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Discussion regarding fairness in AI-based image analysis is often limited to analyzing existing bias without establishing methodologies to overcome model disparities.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes concerns about disparities as a critique of the literature's limited discussion and lack of methods to address disparities, which clarifies but does not exactly restate the preprint's broader claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most papers in the field lack the use of a standardized set of metrics to measure fairness and bias in algorithms.
alignments.rationale: No publication claim explicitly states the lack of standardized metrics; related points about methodological limitations are present but not a direct match.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Exploration of adversarial learning for demographic/camera/institution agnostic models is an important direction to minimize disparity gaps for imaging.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Exploration of adversarial learning for demographic/camera/institution agnostic models is an important direction to minimize disparity gaps for imaging.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this recommendation and assigns it higher certainty (publication=high vs preprint=medium), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Privacy preserving approaches present encouraging performance for both natural and medical image domains.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Privacy preserving approaches show encouraging performance for both natural and medical image domains.
alignments.rationale: The publication contains the same claim with similar wording and certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The systematic review identified four prevailing themes in 'fair' deep learning modeling techniques for image applications.
alignments.rationale: The identification of four themes is reported in the publication but was not present among the preprint claims, so it is an added result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning models applied in this study demonstrate highly predictive abilities with accuracy over 95%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine-learning models demonstrated high predictive abilities with accuracy over 95%.
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the preprint's reported predictive performance directly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This model could serve as a fast, gene-specific method for physicians or genetic counselors assisting with diagnosis and reporting.
alignments.rationale: No publication claim asserts the clinical implementation/utility for clinicians as stated in the preprint; this specific translational claim is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The weight of the ACMG criteria shows gene specificity, advocating for the application of machine learning methods with the ACMG criteria.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The weight matrix of the ACMG criteria was significantly different between the three MODY genes HNF1A, HNF4A, and GCK.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the gene-specific differences in ACMG weights, which corresponds directly to the preprint's claim of gene specificity.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Different weights of the ACMG criteria are needed in relation to different MODY genes for accurate functional classification.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Different weights of the ACMG criteria are needed in relation to different MODY genes for accurate functional classification.
alignments.rationale: This conclusion is present in the publication with similar certainty and wording.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The ACMG criteria can be applied as feature vectors in a machine-learning model to obtain precision-based results.
alignments.rationale: Application of ACMG criteria as feature vectors appears in the published claims but was not listed among the preprint claims, so it is an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The number of new COVID-19 cases was significantly higher with nowcasting (694) compared to without nowcasting (389) over the entire study period.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The number of new COVID-19 cases increased from 389 without nowcasting to 694 with nowcasting.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the numeric nowcasting result identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The underdiagnosis of COVID-19 was found to be 37.29% for the entire period and 81.73% for the six days from the date of onset of symptoms to diagnosis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The underdiagnosis of COVID-19 was found to be 37.29% for the entire period and 81.73% for the six-day period.
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the underdiagnosis percentages and timeframes stated in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The underdiagnosis was more critical in the six days from the date of onset of symptoms to diagnosis than in the entire period.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The underdiagnosis was more critical in the six days from the date of onset of symptoms to diagnosis than in the entire period.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about relative underdiagnosis severity is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Nowcasting with machine learning techniques can help to estimate the number of new COVID-19 cases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Nowcasting with machine learning techniques can help to estimate the number of new disease cases.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same conclusion but reports it with higher certainty (publication=high vs preprint=medium), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed deep learning decision making tool for identifying seminoma subtypes achieved an area under the ROC curve of 0.896.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed deep learning decision making tool for identifying seminoma subtypes achieved an area under the ROC curve of 0.896.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this quantitative performance result exactly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model confirms the presence of two distinct subtypes within pure seminoma.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model confirms the presence of 2 distinct subtypes within pure seminoma.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this conclusion with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model unveils morphological differences between the two seminoma subtypes that are imperceptible to the human eye.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model unveils morphological differences between the seminoma subtypes that are imperceptible to the human eye.
alignments.rationale: The publication carries the same conclusion about imperceptible morphological differences with similar certainty.
